This incredibly dense core is where we find our first two particles. So let's start with the proton. The heavy hitter with the positive charge. Yes, exactly. Each proton carries one fundamental unit of positive charge. In classical physics, we denote that fundamental charge as E, which equals 1.6 times 10 to the negative nineteenth coulombs. Which is just a terrifying number to look at if you're trying to do quick math in your head. It is. It's awful. Which is why in chemistry, we thankfully simplify it. We just call the charge of a proton plus one. Okay, much better. Plus one. Much easier. And alongside this positive charge, a proton has a significant amount of mass. We define this mass as approximately one atomic mass unit or a mu. One a mu, got it. But the single most important thing to remember about the proton is that it dictates the identity of the atom. The number of protons is called the atomic number, which we denote by the capital letters Z. So Z acts as a unique ID. Like if I find an atom in a meteorite and I count its protons, I know exactly what element it is no matter where in the universe it came from. Without question, all oxygen atoms have exactly eight protons, all carbon atoms have exactly six. None. If you have a carbon atom and you magically fuse a seventh proton into its nucleus, it is instantly no longer carbon. It's nitrogen. The atomic number z is non negotiable. Okay, I have to push back here for a second. If I have a carbon nucleus with six protons all packed into this tiny microscopic space, I mean positive charges repel each other, right? Totally repulsive, yeah. So why doesn't the nucleus just explode from the electrostatic repulsion? Six positive charges crammed together should be flying apart violently. That is a phenomenal question. And it points directly to why our second subatomic particle, the neutron, is so incredibly Because without them it would just blow apart. Exactly. The electrostatic repulsion is incredibly strong. But within the tiny confines of the nucleus, an even stronger force takes over. It's appropriately named the strong nuclear force. The strong nuclear force, okay. Right, and this force binds protons and neutrons together, completely overcoming the electrostatic repulsion. So the neutrons basically act like nuclear glue. Exactly, they're the glue. Neutrons are also located in the nucleus, and as the name implies, they are electrically neutral, so they carry charge of zero. Zero charge. Got it. And their mass is just slightly larger than a proton's, but for MCAT math, we also consider a neutron to be roughly one amu. Okay, so they're basically the same weight. Pretty much. And together protons and neutrons make up the mass number of the atom which is denoted by the capital letter A. Okay, so Z is atomic number, the protons. And A is mass number, protons plus neutrons. You got it. And since they both weigh one Amu and they're both in the nucleus, basically 100% of the atom's weight is just sitting in that central core. Yeah, the nucleus accounts for practically the entirety of the atom's mass. Yeah. Now, every atom of a given element absolutely must have the same number of protons, nature is a bit more flexible with the glue. The neutrons can vary. Yes, the number of neutrons can vary. Giving us isotopes. Same element, different weight. Exactly. Atoms that share the same atomic number Z, but have different mass numbers A, those are isotopes. Carbon is like the classic example here. The vast majority of carbon on Earth is carbon-twelve. So six protons, six neutrons. But about 1% is carbon-thirteen, which has six protons and seven neutrons. Okay, a little heavier. Right. And a very tiny radioactive fraction is carbon-fourteen, with six protons and eight neutrons. So when I see a notation like carbon-fourteen, that number 14 is the total mass, the A. Yes, exactly. So if I need to find the neutrons, I just take the 14, subtract the six protons that make it carbon, and I'm left with eight neutrons. The math is just that simple. On the MCAT, you will often see this written in standard isotopic notation. What does It's the chemical symbol, say C for carbon, with the mass number A as a superscript on the upper left and the atomic number Z as a subscript on the lower left. Upper left is mass, lower left is identity. Got it. Yeah. Alright, so we've mapped out the central vault of the atom. We have our positive protons dictating identity and our neutral neutrons providing mass and stability. Right. What about the third block? The electron. Ah, the electron. Electrons inhabit the empty space surrounding the nucleus. They carry a charge equal in magnitude to the proton but opposite in sign. So minus one. Minus one. But their mass is the real differentiator here. The mass of an electron is roughly one two thousandth the mass of a proton. Wow. That is incredibly small. It is so negligible that we completely ignore it when calculating the mass number. It just doesn't matter for weight. Okay, so if electrons are basically weightless ghosts that don't even count toward the atom's mass, do they actually matter? Like why do we care about them? Because while the protons define the atom's identity, the electrons define the atom's personality. Oh, love that framing. The personality, tell me more about that. Well, how does an atom interact with the world around it? Does it bond with oxygen? Does it explode in water? Does it conduct electricity? Light all the actual chemistry stuff. Exactly. Nucleus is locked away deep inside and never touches anything. All chemical interactions are dictated entirely by the electrons because they form the outer boundary of the atom. So the protons are like the CEO sitting in the vault, but the electrons are the front frontline workers actually shaking hands and making deals. That's a perfect analogy. And we categorize those workers based on their distance from the CEO. Oh, okay. How does that work? Electrons orbiting closest to the positive nucleus are tightly bound by electrostatic attraction. We call these core electrons. Core electrons. Right. They are deeply held and they rarely participate in chemical reactions. Which leaves the ones on the outermost edge. The valence electrons, these are furthest from the nuclear pole. They possess the highest potential energy and are the least tightly bound. So they're the ones making deals. Yes. Valence electrons are the ones that are shared, swapped and stolen to form chemical bonds. This connects directly back to isotopes actually. Not at all? Not at all. This is a vital concept for you to internalize. In a neutral atom, the number of positive protons equals the number of negative electrons. Carbon-twelve and carbon-fourteen both have six protons, which means they both have six electrons arranged in the exact same configuration. So a chemical reaction can't like see the extra neutrons hidden in the vault? The outside of the atom looks identical. Yes. Isotopes have different physical properties like mass or density, or in the case of carbon-fourteen radioactivity, but they have identical chemical properties. That makes so much sense. Now you mentioned the nucleus is locked away and the electrons are on the boundary, but how far away is that boundary? Okay, before we move on, the Kaplan text brings up hydrogen's isotope specifically. I know hydrogen is unique because it normally doesn't even have a neutron, right? Correct. The most abundant isotope of hydrogen has one proton, one electron, and zero neutrons. Because it's the first and most basic atomic structure, we call it protium from the Greek for first. So we have our basic inventory, protons give the ID, neutrons bring the glue and the mass, and electrons handle the chemistry. That's the summary, yeah. But as we start weighing these atoms, we run into a major point of confusion for a lot of students. Like how does this inventory translate to the numbers we actually see printed on the periodic table? And that exact point of confusion drives us directly into section 1.2, Atomic mass versus atomic weight. These two terms sound completely interchangeable, but I'm guessing in chemistry, treating them the same is a guaranteed way to lose points. They are profoundly different. Let's start with atomic mass. We already touched on this. Atomic mass is essentially synonymous with mass number. It is discrete. It applies to one specific individual isotope. So the atomic mass of carbon 12 is 12. The atomic mass of carbon 13 is 13. Yes. And the units are atomic mass units or Amu. To be technically precise, one Amu is defined as exactly one twelfth the mass of a single Carbon-twelve atom. Since carbon 12 has six protons and six neutrons, one Amu is effectively the mass of a single proton or neutron. So atomic mass is really just counting the heavy particles in a specific atom. It's a whole number. But atomic weight is a different concept entirely. In the real world, you don't work with individual isotopes. Right, you're scooping up billions of them. Exactly. If you mine a block of copper from the earth, you aren't just getting one type of copper. You're getting a mixture of all the naturally occurring isotopes of copper. And the atomic weight is the average of all those isotopes? It is the weighted average. That distinction is critical. The atomic weight is the number you see printed on the periodic table. Let's ground this with an analogy. The Kaplan text uses chlorine, which I think is a great example. It's perfect. Chlorine in nature exists primarily as two isotopes: chlorine-thirty five and chlorine-thirty seven. So if it's just an average, is the atomic weight of chlorine-thirty six? No, because it is not a fiftyfifty split. Chlorine-thirty five is much more stable and abundant. In nature, about 75% of chlorine is chlorine-thirty five and only 25% is chlorine-thirty seven. Oh, I see. It's like a bakery. Imagine a bakery makes thousands of donuts a day. 75% of their donuts are standard glazed and they weigh exactly 35 grams. Okay, standard glazed. And then 25% of their donuts are jumbo double stuffed weighing exactly 37 grams. If you buy a random box of 100 donuts and weigh the whole box to find the average weight of a single donut, you're not going to get 36 grams. Right, you have to calculate the weighted average. You multiply the mass of each type by its fractional abundance. So 0.75 times 35 grams plus 0.25 times 37 grams. And if you run that math. The average donut weighs 35.5 grams. And if you look at the periodic table, the atomic weight of chlorine is roughly 35.5 a moo. Precisely. But here's the MCAT trap I really need you to be hyper aware of. If I reach into that bakery box and pull out one single specific donut, will it weigh 35.5 grams? Yeah, it's either a standard 35 gram donut or a jumbo 37 gram donut. Doughnut. The bakery doesn't actually bake any 35.5 gs doughnuts. Exactly. No single atom of chlorine ever weighs 35.5 mU. The atomic weight is a mathematical abstraction representing a bulk population. So if I'm looking at bromine on the periodic table and its weight is 79.9 mmu, that just tells me that the bulk population of bromine averages out to 79.9. Yes. In fact, knowing that bromine's weight is 79.9 allows you to deduce its isotopic abundance. How so? Well since its isotopes are primarily bromine at 79 and bromine -eighty one, and the average is almost dead center at 80, you know intuitively that Bromine must be roughly a fiftyfifty split in nature. The MPAD absolutely expects you to make those logical leaves. Okay, so atomic weight is this abstract average. But why do chemists use it so religiously? Like why don't we with do the specific isotopes we have in a sample? Because of the brilliant bridge it provides between the micro and the macro. The atomic weight serves two purposes. Yes, it represents the mass of the average atom in Amu. But much more practically, it represents the mass of exactly one mole of that element in grams. The mole. Okay, this is where high school chemistry trauma kicks in for a lot of people. Let's unpack the mole. It's really not that scary. It is simply a counting unit. If I say a dozen, you know, I mean 12 of something. Right. If I say a gross, I mean 144. If I say a mole, I just mean Avogadro's number. Six point zero, two times 10 to the twenty third of something. A six with 23 zeros behind it. It's just an unfathomably large number. It has to be large because atoms are unfathomably small. But the number isn't arbitrary at all. Avogadro's number is the exact mathematical conversion factor that links the microscopic atomic mass unit to the microscopic gram. Let me make sure I'm visualizing this right. If I have one average atom of carbon, it weighs 12 mO. Correct. If I scoop up exactly one mole of carbon atom, so six point zero, two times 10 to the twenty third of them, and I put them on a scale in a laboratory, the scale will read exactly 12 grams. Yes, the number translates perfectly. If you look at oxygen, the periodic table says 16. That means one average atom is 16 ammo and one mole of oxygen atoms is exactly 16 grams. Wow. This concept is called molar mass. It allows you to count atoms simply by weighing them. So if an MCAT question tells me I have 32 grams of oxygen atoms, I don't need a microscope to know how many atoms I have. I know oxygen is 16 grams per mole. 32 divided by 16 is two. I have exactly two moles of oxygen atoms. You've hit the nail on the head. That is the fundamental utility of atomic weight. All right, so we've successfully cataloged a static atom. We know the particles, we know the mass, we know how to weigh them in a lab. But atoms aren't static. Electrons don't just sit there. They move, they interact, they absorb energy. How do we start to understand the internal dynamics of this system? To understand how the atom behaves, we have to look at how our model of the atom evolved over time. And that brings us to Section 1.3 Rutherford, Planck, and Bohr. The heavy hitters of physical chemistry. We are going to walk through three historical breakthroughs. For the MCAT, you don't just need to know the names, you need to understand the experiments and crucially, the mathematical equations they left behind. Let's start with Ernest Rutherford in 1911. What was the scientific consensus before him? Well, before Rutherford, physicists believed in the plum pudding model. They knew atoms contained negative electrons and they knew atoms were generally neutral. So they assumed the atom was just a sphere of positive charge with negative electrons randomly embedded in it, like raisins in a plum pudding. So the mass and the charge were just spread out evenly. Exactly. Enter the gold foil experiment. Right. Rutherford designed this elegant experiment. He took a radioactive source that fired alpha particles, which were essentially heavy positively charged helium nuclei and he shot them at a piece of gold foil that was hammered incredibly thin, just a few atoms thick. Okay, picture that. And he surrounded the foil with a circular screen that would flash whenever an alpha particle hit it. So if the Plum Pudding model was right, what should have happened? If the positive charge was spread out like a soft cloud, the heavy, fast moving alpha particles should have punched straight through the foil like a cannonball through tissue paper. And well, mostly they did. Mostly. But roughly one in every 8,000 alpha particles hit the gold foil and violently ricocheted back toward the source. Wow. Rutherford later said it was as if he had fired a 15 inches naval shell at a piece of tissue paper and it bounced back and hit him. Because it didn't hit a soft cloud, it hit something impossibly dense. It hit the nucleus. Rutherford proved that the atom's positive charge and almost all of its mass were not spread out, but concentrated into a tiny, incredibly dense volume at the center. So Rutherford gives us the physical structure, a dense central nucleus with empty space around it. Then comes Max Planck. Yes. In 1900, Max Planck developed the first quantum theory to explain how matter emits heat and light. Classical physics assumed that energy was continuous, like a ramp. Like a wheelchair ramp. Right. You can stand at any infinite point on a ramp, meaning energy could be emitted in any microscopic amount. But Planck found out energy isn't a ramp, it's a staircase. He discovered that energy emitted as electromagnetic radiation comes in discrete, indivisible bundles. He called these bundles quanta. Quanta. You can stand on step one of a staircase or step two. You physically cannot hover in the air between the steps. Energy can only be transferred in these specific quantized amounts. This sounds incredibly abstract. Did he leave us an equation to calculate the size of these quantum steps? He did. The Planck relation. If you are taking notes write this down. E = HFF. Okay, E HF. Let's break down the variables and more importantly the units. The MCAT loves units. They certainly do. E is the energy of the quantum of light measured in Joules. H is a proportionality constant known as Planck's constant, which is 6.626 times 10 to the negative thirty fourth Joules times seconds. Joules times seconds. And F is the frequency of the radiation measured in Hertz. Let's do the unit math on that. Hertz is defined as cycles per second, or one over seconds. So if we multiply Planck's constant joules times seconds by frequency one over seconds, the seconds cancel out, leaving us with joules. The units perfectly prove the equation. They do. And conceptually, because age is just a constant number, this equation tells us that energy and frequency are directly proportional. Meaning if one goes up, the other goes up. Exactly. If the frequency of the radiation goes up, say going from red light to blue light, the energy of the quantum goes up. Okay, so Rutherford gave us the nucleus, Planck gave us the staircase of energy, how do we tie them together? That took the genius of Niels Bohr in 1913. Bohr combined Rutherford's nucleus and Planck's quantum theory to create the Bohr model of the hydrogen atom. Yes. But Bohr faced a major problem. Classical mechanics said that a negative electron orbiting a positive nucleus should constantly lose energy and rapidly spiral into the nucleus, destroying the atom. Right. To fix this, Bohr applied Planck's staircase concept to the electron itself. He postulated that the electron's angular momentum is quantized. Exist in specific discrete orbital lanes. Exactly. Bohr proposed the equation for angular momentum. L equals nh over two pi. Let's break that down. L is angular momentum. H is Planck's constant. Pi is just 3.14. So what is n? N is the principle quantum number. It can be any positive integer. One, two, three, and so on. Because H2 and Pi are all constants. The only variable in this entire equation is N. Oh, I see. Therefore the angular momentum of an electron changes only in discrete steps depending on its orbit. So you can't have an orbit of n equals 1.5, it's step one or step two. Bohr then took this quantized angular momentum and related it to the energy of the electron. This gives us one of the most critical equations in this entire chapter. The energy of the electron, E equals negative RH over N squared. Okay, E is energy. RH is the Rydberg unit of energy, which is 2.18 times 10 to the negative eighteenth joules. And N is our orbit number. But we really have to address the elephant in the room here. Why is there a negative sign in front of the Rydberg constant? How can an electron have negative energy? This is a major stumbling block for students. It really comes down to establishing a reference point for zero. Okay, where is zero? In physics, we define an electron that is completely separated from its nucleus, meaning it is infinitely far away at n equals infinity as having exactly zero energy. It is totally free. It feels no pull. So if it's sitting at infinity, its energy is zero. What happens when it gets captured by an atom and falls inward? As the negative electron is pulled closer to the positive nucleus, the electrostatic force does work on it. Think of it like a gravity well. A gravity well. Yeah, if you are standing on the surface of the earth, you are stuck in a gravity well. To leave the earth and float free in space, you have to spend energy. Therefore, while you are on earth, you are essentially in an energy debt relative to deep space. So when the electron falls closer to the nucleus, it releases energy, becoming more stable. And because it started at zero, releasing energy pushes its total energy into the negatives. Yes. Let's look at the math. If the electron is in the closest possible orbit, n equals one, the denominator is one squared, which is one. The energy is negative RH. Right. If we move further out to n equals two, the denominator is two squared, which is four. The energy is negative RH over four. And mathematically negative one fourth is a larger less negative number than negative one. Exactly. As N increases, the energy becomes less negative, meaning the total energy is increasing, creeping its way up towards zero at infinity. The lowest energy state, 'N' equals one, is defined as the ground state. It is where the atom is most stable. But the electrons don't just stay in the ground state forever. If you add heat or hit the atom with light, the electron can jump to a higher orbit, right? Yes, that is called an excited state. But because of Planck's staircase, the electron cannot absorb just any amount of energy. To jump from n=one to n=two, it must absorb a photon of light that possesses the exact specific energy difference between those two orbits. And what goes up must come down. Electrons in an excited state are inherently unstable. When they fall back down to a lower energy level, they must release that exact same energy difference in the form of an emitted photon. Okay, so if I'm an MCAT student and a question asks me to calculate the specific wavelength of light emitted when an electron falls, how do I actually do that? We use the electromagnetic energy equation which combines Planck's relation with the wave equation. It's E equals Hc over lambda. E is energy, H is Planck's constant, C is the speed of light, which is three times 10 to the eighth meters per second, and lambda, the Greek letter lambda, is the wavelength. Notice the math here, energy and wavelength are inversely proportional. Meaning if energy is big, wavelength is small. Right, if a transition releases a massive amount of energy, it will emit a photon with a very short wavelength, like ultraviolet light. A smaller energy drop emits a longer wavelength like red visible light. So if we combine Bohr's energy equation with this photon equation, we get the master formula for calculating atomic emission. Yes, the Rydberg equation for photon emission. It's E hc over lambda, which equals RH times the quantity of one over ni squared minus one over nf squared. I am looking at that fraction inside the parentheses. One over ni squared minus one over nf squared. Ni is the initial state, nf is the final state. Wait a minute. What's the issue? Every single physics equation I learned in undergrad like delta temperature, delta velocity is final minus initial. Why is this equation initial minus final? You have just identified one of the most common traps on the MCAT. You must remember this is initial minus final. Let's think through the logic of an emission event to understand why. Okay, let's say an electron falls from an initial state of n equals three down to a final state of n=one. If you plug that into the equation as initial minus final, you get one over three squared minus one over one squared. So that's one ninth minus one. Exactly. That results in a negative number. Multiply that by RH and the energy calculated is negative. But wait, a photon is a physical particle of light. It can't have negative energy. That is the crucial conceptual leap. The negative sign in the calculation refers to the electron's change in energy, the electron lost energy. But the photon itself carries that energy away into the universe as a positive value. The equation is mathematically structured so that a negative Delta E for the electron directly corresponds to the emission of a real positive energy photon. So if I mistakenly did final minus initial, I would get one one minus over nine which is a positive number. That would imply the electron gained energy while falling closer to the nucleus which violates everything we just learned about the gravity well. Exactly. If the math implies the electron gained energy by falling, you have the formula backwards. And because every element has a unique nuclear charge, the spacing between its n=one, n=two, and n=three orbits is totally unique. So the distances are different for every element. Therefore, the exact wavelengths of light an element emits when its electrons fall are totally unique. Like a chemical barcode. Yes. If you excite a tube of hydrogen gas, it emits a specific violet line at four ten nm, a blue line at four thirty four, and a red line at six fifty six. The Balmer series Right, these visible light transitions in hydrogen falling down to the n two state are called the Balmer series. By reading that barcode, an astronomer can look at a star a million light years away and know exactly what elements are burning inside it. That is the beauty of the Bohr model. It perfectly predicts the exact colors of light hydrogen emits. It's mathematically pristine. So, why is it in the history section? Why did we abandon it? Because Bohr's math is only pristine for hydrogen. It completely failed when applied to helium or carbon or literally any atom with more than one electron. Why did the planetary model fail? Because Bohr only accounted for the attraction between the negative electron and the positive nucleus. He completely ignored the fact that if you have multiple negative electrons in orbit, they are going to aggressively repel each other. Oh right, negative repels negative. That electrostatic repulsion completely warps the perfect circular orbits. We needed a new paradigm. And that new paradigm shifts us into Section 1.4, Quantum Mechanical Model of Atoms. Modern quantum mechanics completely throws out the idea of electrons traveling in perfectly defined circular planetary orbits. The principle that destroys Bohr's model is the Heisenberg uncertainty principle. Heisenberg. This is where things get philosophically weird. The Heisenberg uncertainty principle states that it is basically impossible to simultaneously determine both the momentum and the position of an electron. Is that just because our microscopes aren't powerful enough? Like if we build a better laser, could we eventually measure it perfectly? No, it is a fundamental limitation of the universe itself. It has to do with wave particle duality. To measure the exact position of an electron, you have to hit it with a high energy photon. But the very act of hitting it transfers energy to the electron, fundamentally changing its momentum. It's like trying to photograph a race car in the dark. Okay, let's hear it. If you use a super fast camera shutter and a bright flash, you get a crisp image of exactly where the car is at that split second. So you know it's positioned perfectly. Right. But the image is frozen. You have no idea how fast the car is moving. But if you leave the shutter open longer, you see a blurred streak. The blur tells you the speed and direction, the momentum, but you can no longer point to the car's exact position. That is a brilliant analogy. Because of this uncertainty, we can no longer say an electron is at a specific point on a circular orbit. Instead, we replace orbits with orbitals. An orbital is a three-dimensional probability cloud around the nucleus where we are 90% likely to find the electron at any given moment. So the atom is just a fuzzy cloud of probability. If electrons are just buzzing randomly in these clouds, how do chemists track of them? How do we map the atom? We use a system of four quantum numbers. Think of these four numbers as the ultimate address system for an electron. Like a physical address. Right. Just like a physical address has a state, a city, a street, and a house number. An electron has four quantum numbers that completely describe its energy state and location cloud. Okay, let's walk down the street. What is the first quantum number? The first is the principal quantum number, denoted by the letter M. This is the exact same N we saw in the Bohr model. It dictates the main energy level or shell the electron is in. N could be one, two, three, and so on. The larger the N, the higher the energy and the farther the shell is from the nucleus. Exactly. So N is our state. How many electrons can live in a given state? What is the capacity of a principal shell? The maximum number of electrons within a principal shell is calculated using the formula two, n squared. For the first shell, n one, one squared is one times two is two. So the first shell holds a maximum of two electrons. Correct. For the second shell, n two. Two squared is four times two is eight. The second shell holds eight electrons. For the third, three squared is nine times two is 18. That arithmetic is exactly right. Now we move to our second quantum number. The azimuthal, or angular momentum, quantum number, which is denoted by the letter L. So if N is the shell, what's L? L tells us the specific subshell. Crucially, L dictates the three-dimensional shape of the orbital cloud. So L is the city. What values can L take? The value of N limits the available values of L. For any principal shell N, the possible values for L range from zero up to N minus one. Okay, so if we are in the first shell, N equals one. One minus one is zero. So the only possible value for L is zero. Right. If we move out to N equals two, L can be zero or it can be one. Yes. And because referring to subshells by numbers gets confusing, chemists use letters. If L zero, we call it the S subshell. The S orbital is shaped like a perfect sphere. Why a sphere? Because an L of zero means the electron has zero angular momentum in this state. It isn't whipping around the nucleus in a specific direction, it's existing as a stationary symmetric three d standing wave. Thus, a sphere. What if L equals one? We call that the P subshell. The P orbitals are shaped like dumbbells with two distinct lobes on either side of the nucleus separated by a node where the probability of finding an electron is zero. And if L equals two? It's the D subshell. If L equals three, it's the F subshell. Okay, so SPDF. How many electrons can fit into these subshells? Maximum number of electrons in a subshell is 4L plus two. Let's test that. For an S subshell, L equals zero. Four times zero is zero plus two is two. The S subshell holds two electrons. Exactly. For a P subshell, L equals one. Four times one is four, plus two is six. The P subshell holds six. For D, L equals two, so four and the two plus two is 10. And for F, L equals three, which gives 14. Perfectly calculated. Which brings us to the third number, the magnetic quantum number, denoted by ML. This tells us the specific orbital within the subshell. It describes the orientation of that orbital in three d space. Our street address. Yes. The values of ML range from negative L to positive L, including zero. Let's trace the logic on that. If we are in the spherical S subshell L zero. So ML can only be zero. There is only one way to orient a sphere in three d space. It looks the same from every angle. Exactly. But if we're in the dumbbell shaped P subshell where L equals one, the values for ML can be negative one, zero, or positive one. Which means there are three distinct P orbitals. Yes. We usually refer to them as Px, Pi, and Pz because they align along the x, y, and z axes of a three d graph. And since every individual orbital can hold a maximum of two electrons, those three p orbitals together hold a total of six electrons. Which perfectly matches my earlier math of 4L plus two. It all ties together. The system is incredibly cohesive. What is the final piece of the address? The spin quantum number denoted by false. This describes the intrinsic spin orientation of the electron itself. There are only two possible values: positive one half and negative one half. We often just call them spin up and spin down. So this is the house number. Or keeping with the fact that each orbital holds two electrons, deciding who gets the top bunk and who gets the bottom bunk. I like the bunk bed analogy, and it highlights a fundamental law of physics, the Pauli exclusion principle. This principle states that no two electrons in a given atom can possess the exact same set of all four quantum numbers. So if two electrons live in the exact same principle shell N, the same subshell, and the exact same orbital ML, they are basically sharing a room. To avoid having the exact same four number address, they are forced to have opposite spins. One must be positive one half, the other negative one half. Exactly. That is the physical reason why a single orbital can only ever hold a maximum of two electrons. Okay, we've built the empty address system. We have the shells, the subshells, and the orbitals. If we start dumping electrons into an atom, what are the rules for how they pick their rooms? The primary rule is the Aufbau principle. Aufbau is German for building up. It dictates that electrons will fill lower energy subshells entirely before they begin to fill higher energy subshells. Nature is fundamentally lazy. It always seeks the lowest possible energy state. So they fill the one's subshell first, then the two's, then the two p. But as atoms get bigger, the energy levels start to overlap, right? How do we calculate which subshell actually has lower energy? We use the N plus L rule. The lower the sum of the principal quantum number N and the azimuthal quantum number L, the lower the overall energy of that subshell. Let's walk through an MCAT style example. Say we have to decide between placing an electron in the four's subshell or the third subshell. Let's run the N plus L calculation. For the four's subshell N is four and since it's an S subshell, L is zero. The sum is four. For the third subshell N is three and since it's a D subshell, L is two, the sum is five. Because four is less than five, the four's subshell has a lower total energy than the third subshell, even though its principal shell number is technically higher. So the four's fills first. Yes, the filling order goes Three's, three p four's, and then third. Okay, so off bau governs the macro level. But what happens on the micro level? When an electron enters a subshell that has multiple empty orbitals, like the T subshell with its three empty dumbbells, how does it decide which specific orbital to go into? That is governed by Hunn's rule. Hunn's rule states that within a given subshell, orbitals are filled such that there are a maximum number of half filled orbitals with parallel spins before any pairing occurs. Textbook definition is a bit dense, this is perfectly explained by the empty bus seat analogy. Let's hear it. Imagine you step onto a public bus. There are three empty rows of seats. You are gonna sit in an empty row all by yourself. At the next stop, a stranger gets on. They're not going to sit right next to you if there is another completely empty row available. Right, that would be weird. They will go take their own row. Only when every single row on the bus has one person in it will new passengers be forced to double up and sit next to a stranger. Human social dynamics perfectly mirror quantum mechanics here. Why do people sit apart? To minimize social discomfort. Why do electrons sit apart? To minimize electrostatic repulsion? They're all negatively charged. Exactly. Electrons are all negatively charged. They hate being close to each other. They would much rather occupy their own empty px, pi or pz orbital than be forced into close quarters with another electron. So in a P subshell, you put one electron in Px, one in pi, and one in Pz, all with parallel up spins, before you ever force a second down electron to pair up in Px. Nature just hates crowding. It does. However, the MCAT loves testing the elements that cheat these rules. You must memorize the electron configuration exceptions. Chromium and copper. Let's look at chromium, atomic number 24. If we follow off Bau and Hun blindly, the configuration should end with fours, two, third, four. We filled the fours and we put the remaining four electrons into the third subshell. But look at the structure of that third subshell. It has five orbitals. With four electrons, we have four half filled orbitals and one orbital sitting completely awkwardly empty. So chromium just moves an electron. Yes. An important corollary to Hun's rule is that half filled and fully filled subshells exhibit significantly lower energy and higher stability than other states. Interesting. This is due to complex quantum exchange energy and spherical symmetry. Chromium achieves this stability by promoting one electron from the four's subshell up to the third subshell. Changing the configuration from four's two, three' four to four's one, three' five. But wait, we just proved with the N plus L rule that third is higher energy than fourths. Promoting an electron up should cost energy. It does cost a small amount of initial energy to move the electron up. However, the thermodynamic payoff, the immense stability gained by having a perfectly half filled third subshell with five parallel spins, far outweighs that initial cost. The overall energy of the atom is lowered. It bends the rules to get a better thermodynamic deal. And copper does the same thing. Copper, atomic number 29. Following the rules, it would be argon core, fours two, third nine. It is agonizingly close to a full D subshell. So it borrows an electron from fours. And me comes. The actual configuration is argon core, fours one, third 10. It now has a completely full, highly stable D subshell. These exceptions also apply to the elements below them on the periodic table, like molybdenum below chromium and silver and gold below copper. Yes, the entire groups follow this behavior to achieve D5 or D10 stability. Okay, we've placed all our electrons. To wrap up the quantum model, we need to talk about how electrons behave in the presence of a magnet. Because whether they are paired up or sitting alone on the bus completely dictates their magnetic properties. This is a high yield physical property. Yeah. The terms the MCAT will test are paramagnetic and diamagnetic. Let's start with paramagnetic. Materials composed of atoms unpaired electrons. Yes. If an atom has unpaired electrons like nitrogen, which has three unpaired electrons sitting alone in its p orbitals, those unpaired electrons will orient their magnetic spins in alignment with an external magnetic field. In alignment. We call these materials paramagnetic, and they are weakly attracted to the magnetic field. The prefix para is a great memory hook here. You can think of parallel because the magnetic spins align parallel to the external field. That is an excellent mnemonic. And to anchor this in reality for our pre med listeners, think about an MRI machine, which stands for Magnetic Resonance Imaging. Oh! They often inject gadolinium contrast dye into patients before an MRI to make the blood vessel show up clearly. Yes. And why gadolinium? Because if you look at its electron configuration, gadolinium has seven unpaired electrons in its F subshell. It is highly paramagnetic, which makes it incredibly responsive to the MRI's powerful magnetic field. That is a brilliant application. So what about diamagnetic? Materials consisting of atoms that have only paired electrons. If every single electron has a partner, one spin up, one spin down, their individual magnetic fields perfectly cancel each other out. Canceling each other out. Right. These materials are actually slightly repelled by a magnetic field. We call them diamagnetic. Like the noble gases. Neon is one's, two's, two's, two p six. Every orbital is completely full. Every electron has a mate. They are completely paired, so they are diamagnetic and repel the magnet. Exactly. A quick memory trick: Di means two, as in a pair. If all electrons are in pairs, or if you prefer, they die in the presence of a magnet by moving away from it. It's It's diamagnetic. Both are great ways to remember it, and that comprehensively covers our map of the atom's internal architecture. Let's do a quick flyover of what we accomplished today. Before we sign off, I want to circle back to something you mentioned during the quantum section, the Heisenberg uncertainty Principle. It always felt to me like a failure of our technology. A frustrating barrier. That is how most people view it. We tend to view uncertainty as a glitch. But let's follow the mathematics of the Uncertainty Principle to its logical conclusion. What if we could perfectly pin down an electron? Like freeze it? Yes. What if we could freeze an electron exactly inside the nucleus so its uncertainty of position was strictly zero? Infinite. Exactly. If the electron were pinned to a single point, it would vibrate with such infinite localized momentum and infinite kinetic energy that it would instantly tear itself free from the nucleus and escape the atom entirely. Wait, so if we could pin it down, the atom would literally destroy itself? Yes. If the uncertainty principle didn't exist, atoms could not exist. Electrons would either spiral into the nucleus and freeze, ending all chemistry, or they would violently tear themselves apart. It is the very mathematical reality of uncertainty that keeps the electrons smeared out in a massive probability cloud. Wow. That cloud is what gives matter its physical volume. It prevents the positive and negative charges from collapsing in on themselves. The only reason a solid object feels solid, I mean, the only reason biology and the physical universe exist, is because of the fundamental, unbreakable uncertainty of the electron. It's not a glitch at all. It's the feature that holds the Lego blocks together. It is the defining magic of the quantum world. That is a staggering thought to end on. Meaning you can change the ratios. Exactly. And crucially, you can separate it using physical methods. Physical methods like what? Boiling?
