Okay, that makes perfect sense. So to understand the math of reaction speed, we first have to visualize what is physically happening inside that that crater. We need to zoom way down to the microscopic level, right into the beaker. What does a reaction actually look like? Let's get into it. So moving into Section 5.1, the molecular reality. It looks chaotic. I mean it literally looks like a battlefield. A battlefield? Yeah, because for a chemical reaction to happen, we rely on collision theory. Molecules do not just float near each other and politely swap atoms through wifi. Right, they don't just Bluetooth their electrons over there. Exactly. They must literally crash into each other. They have to physically collide in space. But wait, if the molecules are just drifting lazily in a liquid and they bump into each other, a reaction isn't guaranteed, is it? I mean, just bounce off one another like bumper cars. And that is the core of the entire issue. You can have trillions of collisions happening every single second in a tiny droplet of water, but only a microscopic fraction of them actually result in a chemical reaction. And we call those productive collisions, right? Yes, productive collisions. And for a collision to be productive, two very specific, very difficult conditions have to be met simultaneously. The first one has to be orientation. They have to hit each other in the exact right spot. We call it steric hindrance. Molecules aren't always just simple tiny spheres. Often they're massive complex three-dimensional shapes. Like massive proteins or long carbon chains. Right. And the actual atoms that need to bond might be hidden deep inside a tiny little pocket of that massive structure. Oh wow. So the probability of another molecule floating through the chaos and striking that exact millimeter wide pocket at the correct angle is astronomically low. Very low. It's like trying to put a key into a lock during a tornado. That's a great way to put it. Like it doesn't matter how many times the key hits the door. If it's hitting the wood, or the hinges, or the side of the handle, the door isn't opening. The orientation has to be absolutely flawless. Flawless. And even if you miraculously achieve that flawless orientation, you still face the second condition sufficient kinetic energy. So they have to hit hard. Yes. The molecules must collide with enough brute force to actually shatter their existing bonds. This minimum energy threshold, the exact amount of violence required in the collision, is called the activation energy or E sub A. E sub A. That's the lip of the crater holding our boulder back. That's it. So flawless aim and incredible speed. Only when you have both do you actually get a productive collision. Right. And I know there is an equation that captures the physical reality perfectly. The Arrhenius equation. Yes. The Arrhenius equation. I mean, it looks like a wall of alphabet soup when you first see it, but it actually tells a really elegant story. It's a, k equals a times e raised to the power of negative e sub a over RT. Let's pull that apart piece by piece. Because if you understand the variables here, you essentially understand the universe of kinetics. Let's do it. We start on the left side with K. That is the rate constant. It essentially represents the speed of our reaction. And generally, our goal in chemistry is to make K as large as possible. Right. We want things to happen fast. We want a big K. So on the other side of the equal sign, we have A, the frequency factor. Think of A as the baseline chaos. It represents how often the molecules are colliding regardless of whether those collisions are actually productive. Okay, so just total crashes. Right. So if you increase A, say, by vigorously stirring the beaker or cramming more molecules into a smaller space, you inherently increase the rate constant. Because it's purely statistical. Exactly. More total collisions just mean more chances for a correct collision to randomly happen. Which brings us to the terrifying part of the the e raised to the power of negative e sub a over RT. It looks scary, but let's demystify it. So e is Euler's number. But for the MCAT, we can just think of this entire exponential chunk as a mathematical fraction, fraction. Right. A fraction representing a physical reality. Yeah, that whole chunk just represents the percentage of all your colliding molecules that actually possess enough energy to clear the activation energy hurdle. Okay, let's look at the denominator of that exponent RT. So R is the ideal gas constant, which which never changes. And T is the temperature in Kelvin. Now watch how beautifully the math maps to the physics. The exponent is negative E sub divided by RT. If beaker, you increase the temperature T. That makes the denominator RT larger. And when a denominator gets larger, the overall fraction E sub A over RT becomes a smaller number. But wait, there is a negative sign in front of that whole fraction. That is the crucial key. Because of the negative sign, the exponent is becoming less negative. Oh, and mathematically, if an exponent becomes less negative, meaning it gets closer to zero, the overall value of that whole term actually increases. Exactly. Which means your rate constant goes up. Raising the temperature mathematically proves that the reaction speeds up. That's brilliant. And physically, temperature is really just a measure of average kinetic energy, right? Exactly. Heating at the beaker just means the molecules are moving faster. Because they are moving faster, a much larger percentage of them now hit each other with enough kinetic force to shatter that activation energy barrier. The math is just a mirror reflecting the physical reality of the collision. It really is. So we've got the run up, we've got the speed and the angle. But what is actually happening at the exact microscopic split second when two molecules achieve a perfect energy collision. That brings us to transition state theory. At the exact moment of a perfect collision, the molecules merge into something called a transition state. It's also known as an activated complex. This is the absolute highest point of potential energy on their entire journey. It's the very peak of the mountain. I always like to picture a gymnast doing the high jump for this. Oh, that's a good one. Yeah, like the collision theory is everything leading up to to the jump, the run up, the sprint, the speed, the angle of their approach. But the transition state theory focuses entirely on that single heart stopping split second where the gymnast is suspended completely horizontal, floating directly over the bar. It's a highly unstable moment. In a chemical transition state, the old bonds of the reactants are partially broken, and the new bonds of the products are partially formed. It's this theoretical hybrid. And the gymnasts can't just pause there, know, they can't just hang out levitating over the bar to catch their breath. No, they are maxed out on potential energy. They have to come down. They are either going to carry their momentum forward and fall onto the mat, turning into products, or they're going to fall backward onto the ground, reverting it entirely back to reactants. That makes sense. That analogy works perfectly because it highlights the fleeting nature of the transition state. It is not a real stable molecule. You cannot isolate a transition state. You can't put a lid on a jar and capture it. No. It exists for mere femtoseconds. And the height of that high jump bar, that is your activation energy. It's the total energy it took for the gymnast to get from the ground to that theoretical peak. Okay, visualizing a gymnast is one thing, but if you are a chemical engineer trying to scale this up or, you know, a student staring at an MCAT exam, you have to map this out scientifically. You have to literally graph the mountain. Yes. Moving into section 5.2, we need to translate this into reaction coordinate diagrams. These diagrams are the visual heartbeat of chemistry. Every chemist sees these in their sleep. So walk us through it. You have a standard graph. You have a standard graph. The y axis is energy, specifically free energy or potential energy. And the x axis is the reaction coordinate, which is really just a fancy way of saying the progress of the reaction over time moving from left to right. So you draw a flat horizontal line on the far left. Those are your starting materials, your reactants. Right. And way over on the right side of the graph, draw another flat line. Those are your final products. And connecting those two lines is a massive sweeping curve. A hill. Right. The vertical distance from your starting reactant line, drawing straight up to the very highest peak of that hill. That is your activation energy, your E sub A, that's the hurdle. But there is another measurement on this graph. And if you mix these two up, you're in trouble. What is the vertical distance between the starting reactant line and the final product line? Not the peak of the mountain, just the net difference between where you started and where you eventually parked. That net difference is your change in Gibbs free energy, your Delta G, your thermodynamics. Ah, so the graph shows both kinetics and thermodynamics. It absolutely does. If your final product line is lower on the Y axis than your starting reactant line, it means your bolder ended up lower than it started. Energy was released into the universe. That's an exergonic reaction, a negative Delta G. Exactly. But if the product line is higher than the reactant line, it means energy was absorbed. That's an endergonic reaction, a positive Delta G. Okay, the mountain peak is kinetics, the final destination is the thermodynamics. You got it. But we're assuming a simple single hill here. And most reactions, especially in biology, don't happen in one miraculous step, do they? They? No, they're usually multi step reactions. So the graph of a biological process won't look like a single hill. It'll actually look like a jagged mountain range with multiple peaks and multiple valleys. Okay, so if the peaks are the transition states, the gymnasts suspended over the bar with partial bonds, what are the valleys between the peaks? Ah, the valleys represent intermediates. An intermediate is a molecule that forms temporarily during a multi step reaction, but gets entirely consumed before the final product is reached. Wait, how is that different from a transition state? Because they both exist temporarily in the middle of the reaction. It comes down to stability and fully formed bonds. A transition state, like we said, has partial bonds. It's at the absolute peak of energy, highly unstable, impossible to isolate. An intermediate, however, sits in an energy valley. It is fully formed, actual chemical bonds. It is a local energy minimal. So it's actually a real molecule. Yes. Because of that, an intermediate is somewhat stable. If you were incredibly fast and had the right lab equipment, you could theoretically isolate an intermediate. You can never isolate a transition state. Got it. Valleys are intermediates. Peaks are transition states. Now, if I have a reaction mountain range with three peaks, meaning it's a three step reaction, which of those three steps determines how fast the overall reaction finishes? The speed of the entire overall reaction is dictated exclusively by the single step with the highest activation energy. The tallest peak on the entire graph. Yes, we call this the rate determining step. It's almost like driving on a multilane highway. How so? Well, you're taking a road trip that has three legs. Leg one, the highway is totally empty, you're driving 100 miles an hour. Nice. Leg two, suddenly there's massive construction. Three lanes merge into a single dirt road and you're crawling at like five miles an hour for hours. Ugh, the worst. Then leg three, the road opens back up and you're flying at 100 miles an hour again. It fundamentally doesn't matter how fast you drove on leg one or how fast you drive on leg three. The total time it takes you to reach your destination is almost entirely determined by that single, agonizingly slow bottleneck in the middle. That is a perfect analogy. The rate determining step is the kinetic bottleneck. You literally cannot produce the final product any faster than you can push your molecules over that single tallest energy barrier. Okay, so we figured out how to map the mountain and we know how to spot the bottleneck. But if you're a chemist in a lab or, you know, a biological cell just trying to survive, conceptualizing the bottleneck isn't enough. You need to manipulate the environment to speed things up. Absolutely. How do we push the pedal down? So moving into Section 5.3, Factors Affecting Reaction Rate. You have four primary levers you can pull, and they all trace directly back to the physical realities of collision theory we just talked about. What's the first lever? The first lever is concentration. Which feels pretty intuitive, adding more cars to the highway. Exactly. If you increase the concentration of your reactants in a solution, you are packing more molecules into the exact same volume of space. This drastically increases the baseline frequency of collisions. So going back to our Arrhenius equation, you are pumping up the A variable. Yes, the frequency factor. More collisions simply mean a greater statistical probability of a productive collision occurring. More lottery tickets, more chances to win. Exactly. What's What's the second lever? The second lever is temperature, which we briefly touched on with the math earlier. But to really grasp it visually, we need to look at it through the lens of the Maxwell Boltzmann distribution curve. Paint that picture for us. What does that curve look like? Imagine a graph. The x axis represents the kinetic energy of the individual molecules, from zero energy on the left to massive energy on the far right. The y axis is simply the number of molecules. At any given room temperature, if you plot your molecules, this graph forms a bell curve. A standard bell curve. Right. Most molecules have an average amount of energy sitting in the fat middle of the bell. A few are practically frozen on the left and a tiny, tiny fraction are zooming around with massive energy on the far right tail. And our activation energy, our E sub, a hurdle, is basically a vertical line drawn way, way off to the right side of this graph, right? Precisely. Only the tiny fraction of molecules in that far right tail, past the line, actually possess enough energy to react. So what happens when you turn on a Bunsen burner and increase the temperature? The curve doesn't just slide evenly to the right. The entire bell curve actually flattens out and spreads wider. The peak gets much lower, but the tail of the curve gets significantly fatter. Oh, I see. Because that tail is now fatter, a drastically larger area of the curve now sits to the right of your activation energy line. So you have exponentially increased the population of molecules capable of making the jump. Yes. It's not just making the existing fast molecules a little faster, it's graduating a massive chunk of average molecules into the high energy club. Okay, so concentration gives us more collisions, temperature gives us harder collisions. What about the actual physical state of the molecules? Does the medium matter? The medium matters immensely. Whether your reactants are in a solid, liquid, or gas state dictates how freely they can move and collide. Right, because gases move wildly and collide constantly. Exactly. But solids are locked in a rigid crystalline lattice, meaning only the molecules exposed on the very surface can actually react. And what about the liquid solvents we dissolve them in? Does it matter if I use water versus say, oil? It matters deeply. Let's look at polar solvents like water versus non polar solvents. Well, polar solvent has an uneven distribution of electrical charge. Right. Water is highly polar. So when you drop a reactant into water, the water molecules surround it and actually start pulling on its electrons via bevel dipole interactions. That pull on it. Yeah. This physical pulling can actually stretch and weaken the existing bonds of the reactant. Wait, so the water is doing some of the heavy lifting for you. It's weakening the bonds before the collision even happens. Exactly. By polarizing the reactant, the solvent raises the potential energy of the ground state, which effectively lowers the amount of activation energy you need to snap that bond. It is a brilliant way to accelerate a reaction. Okay, those are the physical adjustments: concentration, temperature, solvent. But there is a final lever: the ultimate cheat code of chemical kinetics. The catalyst. Yes. If you look at the most complex rapid reactions in industrial chemistry or even human biology, they don't just rely on heat, they use catalysts. Catalysts are the absolute holy grail of kinetics. A catalyst is defined as a chemical substance that dramatically increases the rate of a reaction without being consumed in the process. Without being consumed. Right. It enters the reaction, does its incredibly specific job, and exits completely unchanged immediately ready to grab the next molecule. So how does it actually pull this off? Because the common misconception, and I know this is a huge MCAT trap, is that a catalyst acts like a steroid. Yes, people think it pumps energy into the molecule so they can jump higher over the mountain. That is the exact misconception you need to purge from your mind. A catalyst does absolutely nothing to the energy of the reactant molecules. It does not give them a boost. And it does not flatten the existing mountain. What is it doing? What a catalyst does is provide a completely different chemical pathway, an entirely new molecular mechanism that inherently requires a lower activation energy. Okay, so it doesn't give you stronger legs to climb the mountain. It literally drills the tunnel straight through the rock bypassing the peak entirely. That is the perfect visualization. Let's look at a real world example. The catalytic converter in your car. Oh yeah. Your engine produces toxic nitrogen oxides and carbon monoxide. Thermodynamically, those gases want to react and turn into safe nitrogen and carbon dioxide. Kinetically? Kinetically, the activation energy is so incredibly high that they would just float in the atmosphere for years. The mountain is just too tall for them. So your car forces those exhaust gases through a honeycomb coated with platinum and palladium, the catalyst. The metal surface physically blinds the toxic gas molecules, holding them in the exact perfect orientation and stretches their bonds. It drills the tunnel. It drills the tunnel. Suddenly the activation energy plummets and the gases react almost instantly before leaving your tailpipe. That is incredible. But, and this is crucial, we have to establish what a catalyst does not do because you mentioned this is heavily tested. It is heavily tested. A catalyst does not change the starting energy of the reactants. It does not change the final energy of the products. So it has absolutely zero effect on the thermodynamics? Zero. It does not change the delta G and it does not change the equilibrium position of the reaction. A catalyst cannot make a non spontaneous reaction spontaneous. It just speeds it up. All it does is get a spontaneous reaction to its inevitable finish line much, much faster. Okay, but pushing back on the tunnel analogy for a second. If a catalyst drills a tunnel through the mountain to help the reactants become products faster, doesn't that inherently mean the final products now have a super easy open tunnel to walk backward and turn back into What a brilliant deduction. Emphatically yes. A catalyst lowers the activation energy for the forward reaction. But by the irrefutable laws of physics, it equally lowers the activation energy for the reverse reaction. A catalyst speeds up both the forward and reverse reactions by the exact same factor. It just helps the entire system achieve its equilibrium balance faster from both directions. Okay, so we figured out how to hack system drill a tunnel, turn up the heat, cram more molecules in. Right. But if you are a chemical engineer trying to scale this up to a multi million dollar factory, conceptual analogies aren't enough. You need to know exactly how much faster your factory is going to run. You need the math. We need to translate speed into mathematical rate laws. Which brings us to section 5.4. And the absolute foundation of that is defining what a rate actually is in a laboratory setting. Right. In kinetics, the reaction rate is expressed as the change in concentration of a specific substance over a given period of time. Mathematically, that's delta concentration divided by delta t. And the standard units for that? Molarity per second? Molarity being moles per liter. We are literally measuring how many moles of a substance are appearing or disappearing in a liter of water every single second. Correct. But we run into a major accounting problem here involving stoichiometry. The balanced equation. Oh, the stoichiometry Yes. Let's say we have a simple reaction where one molecule of reactant A turns into two molecules of product B. So A yields 2B. Okay, straightforward. But look at the flow of that. Every time one single molecule of A disappears, two molecules of B appear. So mathematically, product B is forming twice as fast as reactant A is disappearing. Oh, I see the problem. If a scientist walks into the room and asks, Hey, what is the rate of this reaction? The answer is totally ambiguous. Are we measuring the slow disappearance of A or the rapid appearance of B? Right, because you'd get two completely different numbers for the exact same beaker. Exactly. To fix this ambiguity, chemists created a general balanced rate expression. You simply take the change in concentration and divide it by the stoichiometric coefficient from the balanced equation. So if the balanced equation is little a plus little bb yields little cc plus little dd, then the general rate equals negative delta concentration of A over little a times delta T, which also equals negative delta concentration of B over little b times delta t, which equals positive delta concentration of C over little c times delta t. And the reactants have a negative sign because they are disappearing, right? And the products are positive because they are appearing. Exactly. By dividing by those stoichiometric coefficients, we mathematically ensure that the rate of the reaction is a single universally consistent number regardless of which specific molecule you choose to measure with your lab instruments. Okay, that tells us how to precisely measure the speed once the reaction is already happening. But how do we predict the speed before we even start? Like based entirely on what we put into the beaker? This brings us to section 5.5 and the most critical equation in the entire field, the rate law. The rate law. Rate equals K times the concentration of A to the power of X times the concentration of B to the power of Y. So rate equals K times A to the X times B to the Y. This equation dictates everything. We've seen K before. That is our rate constant. A and B are the starting concentrations of the reactants. And notice that products almost never appear in a rate law, right? Because the initial speed depends entirely starting materials colliding. Exactly. And then we have the exponents, X and Y. These are known as the orders of the reaction. And this right here, for anyone listening, is the biggest trap in all of chemical kinetics. Every textbook, every MCAT course warns about this and people still fall for it. It is an absolute minefield. The exponents x and y are almost never the same as the stoichiometric coefficients from the balanced equation. I feel like you need to repeat that because it goes against every instinct you develop in introductory chemistry. Say it again. If your overall equation is 2a plus 3b yields products, your rate law is almost certainly not T rate equals k times a squared times b3. Absolutely not. You cannot just pull the big numbers down from the equation and use them as exponents. You cannot. And the reason why, Doug, it goes back to our mountain range analogy. The balanced overall equation is just a summary. It just tells you the starting materials and the final products. It completely hides the complex multi step mechanism that actually occurs in the beaker. The overall equation doesn't tell you which step is the rate determining bottleneck. It's like looking at a summary recipe that just says flour plus sugar plus eggs cake. Boy, like that. That's your balanced equation. But that summary doesn't tell you the order of the kinetic steps or the speed of the individual steps. Do you mix the eggs first? Do you let the dough rest? You can't just look at the final ingredient list to understand the kinetic rate of the entire baking process. That's exactly it. You have to actually go into the kitchen, run the experiment, and observe. So the rate law exponents must be derived from physical observation, not just from reading the balanced equation. The only way to find X and Y is experimentally. Which means we need to talk about the method of initial rates. The method of initial rates because this is how you actually solve for those exponents on a test. Yes. We need to set up a hypothetical lab right now to walk through this logic. Let's say we have a reaction between reactant A and reactant B. We have no idea what the exponents are. How do we find out? We run a series of controlled experiments. Right. Let's imagine three separate beakers. In beaker one, we put in exactly one molar of reactant A and one molar of reactant B. We hit start on our stopwatch and we measure the initial rate of the reaction. Let's say our instrument tells us the initial rate is two molarity per second. Okay, me track this. Beaker one. A is one, B is one, grade is two. Now what? Now we prepare beaker two. This is where the deduction happens. We want to find the exponent for reactant A. To do that we must hold reactant B entirely constant so it doesn't interfere. Okay. So in beaker two we keep B at one molar but we double reactant A. We put in two molar of A. We hit the stopwatch. And let's say the instrument tells us the new initial rate is four molarity per second. What does that tell us? Think about the math. We held B constant. We doubled the concentration of A from one to two. As a direct result, the rate also exactly doubled from two to four. So that is a perfectly linear one to one mathematical relationship. Exactly. Two to the power of one is two. Therefore, the exponent for reactant A, its reaction order, is exactly one. Reactant A is first order. Okay, that was easy. Now we need to find the exponent for reactant B. So we set up beaker three. Yes. This time we hold reactant A constant, leaving it at one molar. But we double reactant B, putting in two molar. We hit the stopwatch. And this time, let's say the instrument says the initial rate is eight molarity per second. Now the logic gets really interesting. We held A constant, we doubled B from one to two, but the rate didn't double. Right, the rate went from our bigger line of two all the way up to eight. The rate quadrupled. We multiplied the concentration by two, but the rate multiplied by four. What exponent makes that mathematical statement true? Two raised to what power = four? Two to the power of two. Exactly. Therefore the exponent for reactant B is two. Reactant B is second order. So our final experimentally determined rate law is rate = k x a to the first power times b to the second power. We deduce the invisible exponents purely by watching how the system reacted when we poked it. That is the method of initial rates in a nutshell. You isolate one variable at a time, see how it affects the speed, and derive the exponent mathematically. Perfect. So moving to section 5.6, we've solved for the mysterious exponents X and Y. Let's unpack what these reaction orders actually tell us about the physical reality of the molecules. Let's start with the strangest one. Zero order reactions. In a zero order reaction the exponent is zero. Right, and anything raised to the power zero mathematically becomes one. So the concentration variable effectively cancels out of the equation entirely. So the rate law just simplifies to rate equals k. Exactly. But what does that mean physically? It means the rate of the reaction is entirely 100% independent of the reactant concentration. Whether you have a beaker with one molar of reactant or a beaker packed with 100 molar of reactant, the reaction proceeds at the exact same unrelenting speed. That feels deeply counterintuitive though. We established earlier that increasing concentration increases the frequency of collisions, which should increase the rate. Why does that rule suddenly break down here? It usually breaks down when the reaction is entirely dependent on a physical catalyst, like a biological enzyme. And that enzyme is fully saturated. Saturated? Yeah, it's working as fast as its molecular machinery possibly can. I think the human liver is the perfect physical analogy for this, specifically how we metabolize alcohol. Oh, that's a great one. Walk us through it. So the liver uses an enzyme called alcohol dehydrogenase to break down alcohol, but the liver only has so much of this enzyme and it can only process roughly one standard drink per hour. That is a brilliant example of a zero order process. Right. If you have one drink in your bloodstream, the liver processes it at a rate of one drink per hour. If you go to a party and suddenly have five drinks in your bloodstream, massively increasing the concentration of the reactant, your liver does not magically speed up. Occupied. The rate remains stubbornly fixed at one drink per hour. The rate is independent of the concentration. Which is exactly why drinking coffee or taking a cold shower doesn't sober you up faster. You can't spawn more toll booth. Exactly. You just have to wait for the constant rate to do its job. A phenomenal real world anchor. Now consider the graph of that zero order reaction. If you plot the concentration of alcohol in the blood over time, you get a perfectly straight downward sloping line. Because it's dropping by the exact same amount every hour. Yes. The concentration drops by the exact same mathematical amount every hour. The slope of that straight line is simply negative K. And what are the units for the rate constant K in a zero order reaction? The units are molarity per second, or minutes. Which makes sense because the rate law is simply rate equals K. Since rate is always measured in Ms, K must be Ms to balance the equation. Which brings up a really important concept before we move on. The units for K are not fixed. K is essentially a mathematical chameleon. It absolutely is. The overall reaction rate, the left side of the equation, must always, always be molarity per second. But the right side of the equation changes depending on how many concentrations are multiplying together. So the rate constant K has to constantly shift its own units to magically cancel out whatever is happening on the right side. So the final answer always perfectly equals mins. Let's see that chameleon in action. Level two, first order reactions. In a first order reaction the exponent is one. So the rate law is rate equals k times a to the first power. The rate is directly proportional to the concentration of a single reactant. If you double the concentration of a, the reaction goes exactly twice as fast. Physically, what does that imply about the mechanism? It strongly implies that the rate determining step involves just a single molecule undergoing a chemical change all by itself. It doesn't need to collide with a partner. So just one molecule popping? Yeah. The classic example is radioactive decay. A single unstable atom just decides, case on quantum probability, to decay. The more unstable atoms you have in the sample, the more total decays you get per second. And what do the units of our chameleon K become here? Well the equation is rate equals K times A. Rate is Ms. Mithist. The concentration of A is already providing molarity. Oh I see. So K doesn't need to provide molarity, it just needs to provide the per second part. Therefore, the units of K in a first order reaction drop the molarity entirely and become simply inverse seconds, S to the minus one. See, that makes so much sense when you explain why it shifts. It's just algebraic balancing. Now what about graphing a first order reaction? If you plot concentration versus time for a first order reaction, you do not get a straight line. You get an exponential downward curve. Because it's slowing down over time. Right. As the reactant is consumed, there is less of it available to react. So the rate continuously slows down. But scientists hate curves. We love straight lines. We do. So to force a first order reaction into a straight line, you have to apply a mathematical trick. You plot the natural log of the concentration, Ln of A, versus time. And that gives you a straight line? Yes. That manipulation gives you a straight downward line where the slope is exactly negative K. Alright, on to second order reactions. A second order reaction has a rate law that looks like rate equals K times A squared or a combination like rate equals k times a to the first times b to the first. The rate is proportional to the square of one reactant or the multiplied product of two different reactants. So if I double the concentration of A in a rate equals k times a squared scenario two squared is four, the reaction goes four times as fast. Exactly. And physically this maps perfectly to the classic collision theory we started with. It requires two physical bodies to find each other and slam together in three-dimensional space. Precisely. It is a true two body collision. And let's check our chameleon units for K here. The rate law is rate equals K times molarity squared. M squared. Right. So to make the final answer M's, K has to mathematically cancel out one of those molarities and add the seconds. So the units for K become inverse molarity, inverse seconds. M to the minus one, S to the minus one. And graphing it. Again, plotting concentration versus time gives you an incredibly steep exponential curve. The mathematical trick to get a straight line here is different. Have to plot one over the concentration, one over A, versus time. Okay, the reciprocal. Yes. This manipulation gives you a straight upward slipping line and the slope of that line is positive K. Okay, so we have the saturated toll booth of zero order, the solitary quantum decay of first order, and the violent two car collision of second order. But nature is rarely that clean. What happens when chemistry gets messy? What is a mixed order or broken order reaction? Well, sometimes the experimentally determined exponents aren't neat integers like one or two, they can be fractions. That is a broken order reaction. But more fascinating is a mixed order reaction which can actually change its kinetic identity over the course of time. I saw a terrifying equation in the sources for this. It was a rate law that looked like a massive fraction. Rate equals K1 times C times A squared, all divided by the quantity K2 plus K3 times A. If I see that in the textbook, my eyes just glaze over. It looks like a nightmare, I know. But you don't need to calculate it. You just need to understand the physical behavior hidden in the math. Math. Look solely at the denominator: K2 plus K3 times A. Think about the very beginning of the reaction. At time zero, your concentration of reactant A is massive. It's a huge number. Right, the beaker is full of it. So mathematically, the term K3 multiplied by A is going to be an incredibly large number. So large in fact, that the tiny constant K2 sitting next to it becomes mathematically negligible. You can effectively ignore K2. Okay, so if I cross out the K2 on the bottom, I am left with an a squared term on the top of the fraction and an a term on the bottom. Through basic algebra they cancel out, leaving just a to the power of one. Yes. So at the beginning of the reaction, when concentration is extremely high, the math simplifies and the reaction physically behaves like it is first order with respect to A. That's wild! But watch what happens at the very end the reaction. The reaction has been running for hours. The concentration of A is almost entirely depleted. It's a tiny microscopic number. Exactly. Now that K3 times A term in the denominator is virtually zero. You can completely ignore it. So the denominator just becomes the fixed constant, K2. You no longer have an A variable on the bottom to cancel anything out. You are left to the A squared term on the top of the equation. Which means the entire reaction suddenly behaves like it is second order. That is mind blowing. The reaction literally shifts its physical identity as it runs out of fuel, transitioning smoothly from a first order process into a second order process. You really see how the math maps beautifully to physical reality there. It is a profound piece of chemistry. But it also brings us to a crucial juncture, Section 5.7. Because when you start mixing all these concepts rates, equilibrium, thermodynamics, shifting orders Students and seasoned professionals alike start stepping on landmines. This is undoubtedly the deadliest trap in physical chemistry. The two expressions look mathematically similar. They both use brackets. They both have exponents, but they describe entirely different universes. The rate law rate equals k times a to the x tells you how fast you're moving along the journey. It almost exclusively includes only reactants. And crucially, as we hammered home with the beakers, the exponents x and y unless t be found experimentally. But the equilibrium expression, K e q. The equilibrium expression tells you where the reaction ultimately stops. It tells you the final destination. K e q equals the concentration of products divided by the concentration of reactants. It includes both sides of the equation. And for Keq, the exponents a exactly the stoichiometric coefficients from the balanced equation. You just pull the numbers straight down. So it's a domain error. If you use coefficients as exponents in a rate law, you're dead. If you don't use them in an equilibrium expression, you're dead. You have to know which domain you are in: the speed of the journey or the balance of the destination. Perfectly stated. Which leads directly to trap two. We've spent this entire time throwing around the term rate constant for K. But is K actually a constant? Based on the chameleon discussion earlier, I feel a trick question coming on. It is entirely conditional. The rate constant K is not a universal constant like the speed of light or the ideal gas constant R. It is highly malleable. K is only constant for one specific reaction, running at one specific temperature with a specific activation energy. If you heat up the beaker, K changes dramatically. Remember the Arrhenius equation. Right, temperature changes K. And if you drop in a platinum catalyst, changing the E sub A, K changes instantly. It is a constant only so long as you don't touch the environment. Okay, trap three. And this is perhaps the deepest, most philosophical concept in the entire field. The battle between kinetic and thermodynamic control. This phenomenon occurs when a single reactant sits at a crossroads. It has two different chemical pathways it can take to form two entirely different products. Which product wins the race? It depends on the temperature, right? It depends entirely on the temperature conditions. Yeah. And it exposes the fundamental tension between speed and stability. Let's call them product A and product B. Product A is what we call the kinetic product. Right. The pathway to form product A has a very low activation energy barrier. It is a very small hill. Because the hill is small, the molecules can cross it quickly, even at lower temperatures without much energy. It forms fast. However, the final valley it lands in, the thermodynamic stability of product A is relatively high. It is a shallow crater. Product A is not a very stable molecule. Fast but flimsy. Exactly, fast but flimsy. Now look at product B. Product B is the thermodynamic product. The pathway to form product B requires crossing a massive activation energy barrier. It is a towering mountain. So it's hard to get to. Very hard. At low temperatures, the molecules simply do not possess the kinetic energy to climb it. But if they do manage to climb it, the valley on the other side is incredibly deep. Product B sits in a massive thermodynamic crater. It is vastly more stable than Product A. Slow but incredibly sturdy. Right. So let's put me in the lab. If I'm an organic chemist and I am running this reaction in a beaker and I want the absolute most stable product possible, I want the sturdy product to be, what do I have to do? Do I just leave the beaker on the counter and wait longer? Weeding isn't enough. Because at room temperature the molecules simply lack the thermal energy to ever clear that massive mountain. They will all take the easy path and form the flimsy product A. To get the highly stable thermodynamic product, you have to do something that feels initially paradoxical. Crank up the heat. You have to crank up the heat. Because I need to graduate those molecules into the high energy tail of the bell curve. Yes. You have to provide enough thermal energy to overcome that massive E sub, a barrier. And you have to be willing to wait. Because even with the heat, climbing the big mountain takes statistically longer than climbing the small one. So if I'm in a rush and I just want the quick and dirty product, I keep the beaker cold, the molecules are sluggish so they are forced to take the easy path over the small hill, and I get the kinetic product extremely fast. But if I want the perfectly stable, highly engineered product, I have to blast the system with heat and let the slow thermodynamic process play out, allowing the molecules to slowly overcome the big hill and settle into the deep permanent valley. That is a beautiful paradox. Heat and patience yield true stability. It really is. It encapsulates the entire complex relationship between kinetics and thermodynamics. Thermodynamics dictates the depth of the valley. Kinetics dictates the height of the mountain. And temperature is the currency you use to negotiate between the two.
