Okay, so we've conceptualized this self actualized destination, but to actually succeed on the MCAT we have to quantify it. We have to do the math. Right. And that brings us to the core equation of this entire chapter: the Law of Mass Action, which gives us our equilibrium KEQ. The law of mass action is the mathematical bedrock of equilibrium. So let's visualize a generic reversible reaction. We have some number of moles of reactant A reacting with some number of moles of reactant B. Okay, got it. A plus B. And they are in a dynamic equilibrium with product C and product D. So A plus B yields C plus D. Correct. The equilibrium constant, Keq, is defined mathematically as a ratio. Specifically, it is the concentration of the products raised to the power of their stoichiometric coefficients. Divided by the concentration of the reactants raised to the power of their stoichiometric coefficients. Let me just break that down to make it super audio friendly. Because trying to visualize complex fractions in your head can be brutal while you're listening. Please do. Think of the Keq equation as a simple fraction. The winners, the products you're actually trying to make are always on the top of the fraction, in the numerator. Right. And the starting materials, the reactants, are always on the bottom, in the denominator. Products over reactants, that is the fundamental rule. Products over reactants. And then you just look at the balanced chemical equation, the big numbers in front of the molecules, the stoichiometric coefficients, those just become your exponents. Yes. So if your balanced equation makes two moles of product C, then in your fraction, the concentration of C is squared. It's raised to the power of two. Now this exact structural setup raises a really important question, and honestly it's one of the most common MCAT traps I've seen. Ah, comparing it to kinetics. Yes. The formula for the equilibrium constant looks mathematically identical to the rate law expression that you learn in the kinetics chapter. It looks identical, but they are derived in completely different ways and confusing them will absolutely cost you points. I have fallen for this trap so many times on practice tests. Let me try to explain the difference and tell me if I have it right. In a rate law expression which calculates the speed of the reaction, the exponents have absolutely nothing to do with the big numbers in the balanced chemical equation. You cannot just look at the equation and know the exponents. Unless the passage explicitly tells you it's a single step elementary reaction, yes, you are exactly correct. Right. For rate laws, those exponents are called reaction orders. And you can only find those orders by looking at a giant experimental data table, comparing different trials, and seeing how changing the concentration changes the initial speed. It requires empirical data. Right, but for the equilibrium expression. For the equilibrium expression, no experimental data tables are required at all. The exponents for Keqi are always exactly the stoichiometric coefficients from the balanced chemical equation. They just hand it to you. Yes. If the equation says three moles of hydrogen gas, the exponent for hydrogen in the Keqi expression is exactly three. Period. So much easier. Furthermore, there is a structural difference you need to remember. The rate law only includes the concentration of the reactants, whereas the equilibrium expression includes the concentrations of both the products and the reactants. Right, products over reactants, coefficients become exponents. Simple. But wait, there is a VIP list for this equation isn't there? A VIP list? Yeah, like not every molecule in the reaction gets to be part of the math. Pure solids and pure liquids are strictly excluded from the KEQ Ah yes, that is a critical rule. When you are looking at the state symbols in the balanced equation, any species annotated with an S for solid or an L for liquid is completely ignored. You just cross them out. The technical reason for this exclusion is that equilibrium expression is actually derived using the activities of the chemical species rather than just their raw molar concentrations. And by standard chemical convention, the activity of a pure solid or a pure liquid is defined as exactly one. Let's ground that in physical reality because activity sounds a little abstract. Okay. Imagine you have a solid block of copper sitting in an aqueous solution and a reaction is happening right on the surface of the copper. Okay, I'm picturing it. The density of that coppery block doesn't change as the reaction proceeds. The molar mass of copper certainly doesn't change. Therefore, its effective concentration, like how densely packed those copper atoms it remains totally constant throughout the entire reaction. Exactly. Since its concentration is essentially constant, plugging a value into the numerator or denominator that never changes is mathematically redundant. The math simplifies by just assigning it a value of one. And multiplying or dividing by one doesn't change the overall ratio. Exactly. Therefore we only include chemical species whose concentrations can actually fluctuate significantly during the reaction. Which means we only include gases and equase species because their concentrations are highly variable depending on the volume of the container or, you know, the amount of solvent. Correct. So to summarize where we are, Keq gives us the precise mathematical ratio of products to reactants when the system has successfully reached its final, stable, self actualized state of maximum entropy. But, what if the system isn't self actualized yet? What do you mean? What if you literally just mixed the chemicals together thirty seconds ago? They don't just instantly teleport to equilibrium, it takes time. So how do we mathematically describe where we are right now? That is where we introduce the reaction quotient, denoted by the letter Q. I love Q. I always think of Q as the GPS coordinate or like a snapshot timer for the reaction. That is highly accurate. The formula for calculating Q is identical to the formula for Keq. It is still products over reactants with coefficients as exponents. Pure solids and liquids are still completely excluded. So it's the exact same math? Yes. The vital defining difference is the data you plug into it. To calculate Q, you use the current concentrations at a specific instantaneous moment in time regardless of whether the system is at rest or wildly out of balance. Right. You take a snapshot of the beaker, measure concentrations, plug them into the fraction, and you get Q. And the whole point of finding Q is so you can compare it to the known target, Keq. It tells you which way the reaction has to drive to reach equilibrium. Exactly. Comparing Q to Keq yields three distinct scenarios. And the MCAT will absolutely test your ability to predict the shift based on this comparison. So let's walk through them. Let's do it. Scenario one, you calculate Q and it is mathematically less than Keq. Okay, let's visualize the fraction. If Q is smaller than Keq, it means the fraction is too small. The denominator is too big and the numerator is too small. In chemistry terms, you have way too many reactants and not nearly enough products. And how will the system resolve this imbalance? Well, the system wants to reach self actualization, right? So it has to shift to the right. It will spontaneously consume reactants and create products to make that fraction bitter until Q finally rises up to equal KE. And because this is the natural spontaneous direction the system wants to go, the Gibbs free energy, Delta G, is going to be negative. Precisely. The forward reaction is thermodynamically favorable. Now scenario two, you calculate Q and it is exactly equal to KEQ. The best scenario. System is perfectly at equilibrium. The forward and reverse rates are identical. The fraction is exactly where it wants to be. Delta G is exactly zero. The reaction has no drive to shift left and no drive to shift right. And finally, scenario three. Yeah. You calculate Q and it is greater than KEQ. Meaning the fraction is too top heavy, you've overshot the destination, you have way too many products and not enough reactants. To fix it, the system just throws it in reverse. It shifts to the left, consuming products and turning them back into reactants to shrink the fraction down to the KEQ target. And in the third scenario, the forward reaction is non spontaneous, meaning the forward delta G is positive. However, the reverse reaction moving left is spontaneous and has a negative delta G. Now I want to take this mathematical relationship and link it to another subject the MCAT tests heavily: statistics. Okay, statistics. Yeah, because KEQ isn't just a random chemistry number. It is fundamentally a statistical descriptor of a relationship. I am intrigued. How are you connecting chemical equilibrium to the behavioral science statistics? KS: Okay, think about inferential statistics in psychology or sociology research, right? When a researcher wants to know the relationship between two variables, say hours of sleep and MCAT score, they calculate the Pearson correlation coefficient. Which we denote as little r. Yes. The Pearson correlation coefficient r tells us the strength and direction of a linear relationship. It ranges from negative one to positive one. It does. Exactly. The little r is an inferential statistic that zooms out and tells you the fundamental structural balance between those two variables, and Keq is doing the exact same thing for a chemical system. Keq tells us the relationship and the balance between the products and the reactants. I follow your logic. The magnitude of Keq tells a very clear statistical story. If Keq is an incredibly large number, say ten ten, it means the numerator is massively larger than the denominator. Products heavily, overwhelmingly dominate at equilibrium. The relationship is heavily skewed to the right. Right. Conversely, if Keq is a tiny fraction like 10 to the negative ninth, it means reactants dominate. And the reaction barely proceeds forward at all before hitting equilibrium. Right. Is our inferential statistic. It gives us the big picture. And to push the analogy even further, the raw current concentrations we use to calculate Q, those are like our descriptive statistics. Big law data points. Exactly. Before you can run inferential stats to find the correlation, you have to look at the descriptive stats: the mean, the median, the mode, the standard deviation of your raw data. In chemistry, you measure your raw data, the instantaneous concentrations to find q, and then you infer how it relates to the big picture of KEq. That is a very sophisticated mental model. It reinforces the idea that math is really just a language used to describe fundamental relationships, whether those relationships are between human behaviors or colliding molecules. It's all connected. It is. And speaking of math, there is a very practical, highly necessary calculation shortcut involving KEQ that you absolutely must be comfortable with for the MCAT. Oh, thank goodness for this shortcut. Because without it, finding equilibrium concentrations requires the quadratic formula. And doing the quadratic formula by hand under extreme time pressure without a calculator? It's a nightmare. It is entirely unnecessary if you understand the underlying concepts. So let's set up a typical MCAT problem. You're given an initial concentration of a weak acid reactant, say 0.1 molar. Okay. And you're given a very, very small Keq value for its dissociation. Let's say 10 to the negative eight. Tiny. Very tiny. And you are asked to find the final equilibrium concentration of the products. Normally you'd set up an ICE table, right? Initial, change, equilibrium. The starting reactant is both 0.1. The change is that you lose unknown amount, which call minus X. So at equilibrium, the reactant is 0.1 minuteus X. Yes. The products start at zero and they each gain X. So if you plug that into the products over reactants fraction, you get X times X in the numerator, which is X squared. The And denominator is 0.1 minuteus x. Resulting the equation. X squared divided by 0.1 minuteus x equals 10 to the negative eight. Solving for x algebraically from this point requires the quadratic formula. But here is the massive conceptual shortcut. Look at the Keq value. It's ten-eight. That is microscopic. It tells us statistically that this reaction barely moves to the right at all. Reactants heavily dominate. So that value of X, the tiny, tiny amount of reactant that actually breaks apart to form products is practically zero. It is mathematically negligible compared to the starting concentration. Exactly. If you have a million dollars in your bank account and you drop a single penny down a storm drain, you don't go around saying you have $999,999.99 You basically still have a million dollars. The penny is negligible. So mathematically, one minuteus a microscopically tiny X is basically just point one. Which means we can completely drop the minus X from the denominator. Oh, it is such a relief. When you drop the minus x, the equation simplifies beautifully. It just becomes x squared divided by 0.1 equals 10 to the negative eight. You multiply both sides by 0.1, which gives you x2 ten-nine. And then you just estimate the square root. No quadratic equation required. It saves minutes of calculation time. Minutes. However, you must apply this rule judiciously. You can generally only use the 'x is negligible' approximation if the initial concentration is at least two or three orders of magnitude, meaning 100 to 1,000 times larger than the value of Keq. Oh, that's a good rule of thumb. If Ke is large, say point five, then a significant portion of the reactant is actually converting to product. So X is quite large and you cannot simply ignore it. Got it. Okay, so let's zoom out and summarize where we are. We've spent a lot of time defining the baseline. We know what it means for a system to be perfectly at rest. We know it strives for that maximum entropy, lowest free energy state. And we know the math we use to calculate exactly where it is. That foundational understanding is crucial because in biology and chemistry, life is rarely peaceful for long. Exactly. What happens when the universe moves the goalposts? What happens when we take a beautifully balanced, self actualized system and we brutally stress it. That conceptual leap brings us perfectly into the territory of Kaplan's Section 6.2, Le Chatelier's Principle and the Biology of Stress. I love Le Chatelier's Principle. It's basically the law of chemical rebellion. Chemical rebellion. I like that. It states that if a stress is applied to a system at equilibrium, the system will shift to relieve that applied stress and restore equilibrium. Whatever you do to the system, the system is going to violently try to undo it. It is the chemical equivalent of homeostasis. A system at rest wants to stay at rest. When perturbed, it compensates. Now since the MCAT is ultimately a medical exam, how does this chemical homeostasis translate to the human body? Let's connect this to If we look at the broader physiological picture, Le Chatelier's principle is perfectly mirrored by the peripheral nervous system, more specifically the autonomic nervous system or the ANS. The ANS is essentially the human body's macro level Le Chatelier mechanism. Okay, let's break this down. The autonomic nervous system controls all the involuntary automatic stuff, heart rate, digestion, pupil dilation, sweating. We don't consciously control it. Correct. So let's say a massive physiological stress is applied to the human body. You are hiking in the woods and a grizzly bear steps onto the trail. Instantaneously, the sympathetic division of your autonomic nervous system kicks in. This is your classic fight or flight response. It forcibly pushes your physiological system out of its resting baseline. It's prepping you. Exactly. To prepare you to survive, your pupils dilate rapidly to take in more light and increase your visual field. Your heart rate violently accelerates to pump highly oxygenated blood to your skeletal muscles. The adrenal medulla secretes a surge of epinephrine. And your liver starts dumping glucose into the bloodstream for fast, accessible ATP production. Right. And at the exact same time, it shuts down anything that isn't immediately useful, like digestion is actively inhibited. Because you really don't need to digest a turkey sandwich while you are sprinting for your life. Exactly. That massive sympathetic response is the stress pushing the system totally out of equilibrium. In chemical terms, the Q value of the human body just skyrocketed completely away from KEQ. Yes. But biologically, a human cannot exist in a state of maximal sympathetic arousal indefinitely. It is biologically unsustainable. The elevated heart rate and constant glucose mobilization are highly energetically draining and they will eventually cause tissue damage. So the system must relieve this stress. It has to compensate. Enter the parasympathetic nervous system. Precisely. To relieve the stress and return to the homeostatic baseline, the body fights back using the parasympathetic division. This is often called the rest and digest system. Once the bear is gone and the threat has passed, the parasympathetic division takes over to conserve energy. It slows the heart rate back down. It constricts the bronchi in the lungs. It stimulates the gallbladder and restarts the digestive process in the stomach and intestines. It is literally the body shifting entirely in the opposite direction to restore its physiological KEQ. That makes perfect sense. The sympathetic pushes it out of balance, the parasympathetic pulls it back. And just to make sure we keep our biology terms absolutely pristine, we should quickly contrast this autonomic, automatic process with the somatic nervous system. A vital distinction for the biology section. Yeah, the somatic nervous system is for voluntary conscious skeletal muscle control. When you see the bear and you actively choose to turn around and run away, that is your somatic nervous system firing motor commands from your brain, down your spinal cord, out to the striated muscles in your legs. It's a conscious choice. Exactly. But your heart rate's spiking and your pupils dilating. You didn't choose that. That's That's the autonomic system blindly following Le Chatelier's principle to manage the chemical and hormonal stress. An excellent differentiation. Now let's bring this macro level biological homeostasis back down to the molecular level in a beaker. For the chemistry section of the MCAT, there are three primary testable stresses that you can apply to a reaction that will force it to shift according to the Chattooye's principle. The big three, let's hit them. The first is a change in concentration. Right, Which links directly back to our discussion of the reaction quotient Q. Let's say we have a perfectly balanced reaction at equilibrium. If you suddenly take a pipette and dump in a massive amount of extra reactant, you have stressed the system. Mathematically what you just did is make the denominator of the Q fraction much, much bigger. And if the denominator increases while the numerator stays the same, the overall value of the fraction decreases. Therefore, Q is now mathematically less than K eq. And we know how the system fixes that. We do. If Q is less than K eq, the system shifts to the right. It will start aggressively consuming that extra reactant you just added, turning it into product. It does this until the fraction rises back up and Q perfectly equals K e again. Exactly. It shifts to consume the added stress. And the reverse is also true. If you add extra product, you swell the numerator. Q becomes greater than K eq and the reaction shifts left, turning that excess product back into reactant to shrink the fraction. Now here's my absolute favorite trick. It's how industrial chemists maximize their yield. Instead of adding things, what if you continuously take things away? Ah, yes. Like what if you design a system that constantly siphons off and removes the product the exact second it forms? By constantly removing the product, you are artificially keeping the numerator of the Q expression at zero or very close to it. So Q stays artificially, permanently low. It never gets a chance to reach Because Q is always less than KEQ, the reaction will just keep infinitely driving to the right. It will consume every last scrap of reactant trying to replace the product you keep stealing. You essentially force the reaction to 100% completion, even if it's a reversible equilibrium. It is a brilliant exploitation of Le Chatelier's principle. But here is the critical expert note, the trap that catches thousands of students. When you change the concentration, you're absolutely changing the value of Q. But the value of Keq itself stays exactly the same. This is so important. Keq is a constant. By adding more chemicals, you didn't change the destination. You just picked up the system and dropped it at a different starting location on the map. The GPS coordinate for the destination is identical. Beautifully articulated. Now let's move to stress number two. Changes in pressure and volume. And there's a massive caveat here that you must remember. Let me guess, the stress only applies to gases. Correct. Solids and liquids are, for all practical purposes, incompressible. Increasing the pressure on a beaker of liquid water does almost nothing to the volume or concentration of the water molecules. But gases are highly compressible, they're mostly empty space. So for pressure and volume stresses, we are relying on the principles of the ideal gas law. Let's visualize this. You have a chemical reaction taking place inside a sealed metal cylinder, and the top of the cylinder is a heavy movable piston. The reaction involves gases. If you physically push down on that piston, you are violently decreasing the volume of the container. And according to Boyle's law, volume and pressure are inversely proportional. By decreasing the volume, you are crowding the exact same number of gas molecules into a much smaller physical space. And they start bumping into things. Yes, they will collide with the walls of the container much more frequently, which significantly increases the total pressure inside the cylinder. That massive spike in pressure is the stress. The system absolutely hates it. It feels claustrophobic. It wants to relieve that pressure, but it can't push the piston back up. So what does it do? The system looks at the balanced chemical equation. To relieve the pressure, it will spontaneously shift to the side of the reaction that produces fewer total moles of gas. Wait, explain the physics of that. Why does fewer moles relieve the pressure? Well, pressure is caused by gas molecules colliding with the container walls. If you have fewer molecules bouncing around, you have fewer collisions and therefore less pressure. Okay, let's use the classic MCAT example: the Haber process. Fritz Haber figured out how to pull nitrogen out of the air to make ammonia, which revolutionized agriculture with fertilizer, but also allowed for mass production of explosives. Historically massive reaction. Very massive. So the balanced equation is one mole of N2 gas plus three moles of H2 gas reversibly yields two moles of NH3 gas, which is ammonia. Let's count the moles of gas on each side. On the reactant side, the left side, we have one mole of nitrogen plus three moles of hydrogen. That is a total of four moles of gas. On the product side, the right side, we only have two moles of ammonia gas. Okay, so four moles on the left, two moles on the right. If I slam that piston down and drastically spike the pressure, the system panics. It wants to reduce the pressure, so shifts to the right, toward the ammonia. Because existing as two moles of gas takes up way less physical space and creates way fewer wall collisions than existing as four moles of gas. Precisely. By shifting right, it fundamentally reduces the number of particles in the container, relieving the stress of the high pressure. And conversely, if you pull the piston up, vastly increasing the volume and dropping the pressure, the system feels a deficit. It wants more pressure. Right. It will shift to the side with more moles of gas in this case, shifting left to create four moles to try and replenish the lost pressure. Okay, wait, I have a major push back here because I actually got burned by this specific question on UWorld. What happened? What if I don't use a movable piston? What if I have a rigid solid steel tank where the volume absolutely cannot change? And I decide to increase the pressure inside that tank by hooking up a hose and pumping in a massive amount of an inert gas like helium or argon. The total pressure gauge on the tank goes through the roof. Does the reaction shift to the side with fewer moles to relieve that pressure? It does not. The reaction does not shift at all. But why? You just said high pressure makes it shift. The gauge says the pressure is huge. I don't get it. It is an incredibly devious trap. But it makes perfect sense when you look at the physics of partial pressures. When you pump in an inert gas like helium, you are absolutely increasing the total pressure inside the rigid tank. The gauge goes up. But equilibrium is not determined by the total pressure. It's not? No, it is determined by the specific concentrations or the specific partial pressures of the reacting gases themselves. Okay, break that down for me. Because the tank is rigid steel, the volume hasn't changed. The original nitrogen and hydrogen molecules still have the exact same amount of room to fly around. The addition of helium doesn't compress them, it just means there are helium atoms flying around with them. The nitrogen molecules are still hitting the walls with the exact same frequency they were before. Their partial pressure hasn't changed. And since their individual partial pressures haven't changed, the actual q ratio of the reacting gases hasn't changed. Oh! The reacting gases don't care that the helium is there, they are totally blind to it. Because helium is inert, it doesn't react with them, and because the volume didn't shrink, it isn't crowding them. So the ratio of products to reactants is identical. Q equals KEq, no shift. Exactly. Total pressure increased, but partial pressures remained constant. No shift. Do not fall for the inert gas trap at constant volume. Boom. That is why we break these concepts down. All right, that brings us to the final and most unique stress, changes in temperature. This requires your utmost attention. I cannot emphasize this enough for MCAT success. Temperature is the only stress that actually changes the numerical value of the equilibrium constant, Keq. Let me make sure I've got this absolutely locked in. If I change the concentration, Q changes but Keq stays the same. If I change the pressure or volume, Q changes but Keq stays the same. But if I change the temperature, I actually rewrite the fundamental rulebook of the reaction. I physically move the destination. That is correct. Changing the temperature changes the fundamental thermodynamic distribution of energy. Therefore, Keq itself is a temperature dependent constant. When you change the temperature, the initial concentrations in the beaker haven't magically vanished. So for one microscopic instant, Q is exactly what it was. But because Kq, the target, has moved, Q is suddenly no longer equal to this new Keq. Therefore, the reaction must shift to find the new target. And to figure out which way it shifts, we have to look at the enthalpy of the reaction, Delta H. We have to treat heat not as an abstract concept, but as if it is a physical, tangible molecule in the chemical equation. Precisely. Let's look at an exothermic reaction. Exo meaning out, thermic meaning heat. An exothermic reaction releases heat into the environment. Its delta H is negative. Because it produces heat, we can literally write the word heat on the right side of the balanced equation, belongs to the chemical products. So if the reaction is exothermic, heat is a product. Now let's apply Le Chatelier's stress. If I take a beaker of an exothermic reaction and I put it on a hot plate and crank up the temperature, I am adding heat. And since heat is a product Adding heat is exactly like adding a chemical product. Le Chatelier's principle says the system will shift to the left toward the reactants to consume that excess heat. Yes, increasing the temperature of an exothermic reaction forces a shift to the left and it actually decreases the new value of K8. Conversely, what if the reaction is endothermic? Endo meaning in. It requires heat to proceed. Its delta H is positive. Because it consumes heat to work, we write the word heat on the left side of the equation as a reactant. Correct. And if we apply stress? If we put an endothermic reaction on a hot plate and increase the temperature, we are adding a reactant. The system says, Woah, too much heat on the left side. And it shifts to the right toward the products to consume the heat. This makes the new Kaq larger. There is a stunning visual example of this temperature dependence in the Kaplan book that perfectly illustrates the concept. It involves the equilibrium between dinitrogen tetroxide (N2O4) and nitrogen dioxide (NO2). Oh, love this example because you can actually see the molecule shifting with your bare eyes. Yeah. N2O4 gas is completely transparent, just looks like empty air, but when it breaks apart into two moles of NO2 gas, the NO2 is highly toxic and has this very distinct, angry, reddish brown color. Now the specific forward reaction, transparent N2O4 breaking apart into reddish brown NO2, is an endothermic process. It requires energy to break the nitrogen bond. Therefore, heat is a reactant. So if you take a sealed glass bulb filled with this gas mixture and you plunge it into a beaker of boiling hot water, you are aggressively adding heat. You are adding a reactant. And the system shifts to the right to consume that heat. Exactly. As it shifts right, it produces massive amount of NO2. Suddenly, the gas inside the glass bulb violently changes from clear to a dark, opaque, reddish brown color. But the reaction is reversible. What happens if you take that same hot glass bulb and plunge it directly into an ice bath? Well, you are rapidly removing heat, you are stealing a reactant, the system panics. It wants to replace the heat you just stole to maintain its equilibrium. So the reaction shifts hard to the left to produce heat. As it shifts left, the reddish brown NO2 molecules fuse back together to form N2O4. And right before your eyes, the toxic brown gas completely fades away and the bulb becomes crystal clear again. It is the perfect, tangible demonstration of Le Chatelier's principle managing temperature stress. Now, about how chemists actually observe and prove these phenomena brings up a critical tie in to the psychology and sociology section of the MCAT, specifically regarding research methods. Research design is heavily tested. How are you connecting it here? If a chemist wants to definitively prove to a scientific journal that increasing pressure causes a specific shift in equilibrium, they can't just passively observe factories. They have to run a strict experimental study. Yes. The defining characteristic of an experimental study is the active manipulation of variables. The researcher must actively, purposefully manipulate the independent variable they must physically push down the piston to change the pressure. Simultaneously, they must ruthlessly control all other potential confounding variables, such as keeping the temperature perfectly constant. Only then can they observe and measure the effect on the dependent variable which is the final yield of ammonia. Right, because if they didn't manipulate anything and just went to five different chemical plants and measured the ambient pressure in the tanks and compared it to the yield, that is merely an observational or correlational study. They could only publish a paper saying pressure is associated with a higher yield. They could never legally say pressure causes the yield because maybe the high pressure factory also uses a better catalyst or operates at a lower temperature. You can't isolate the cause without an experiment. The mantra is, Correlation does not imply causation. And if we apply this exact same experimental rigor to our physiological example from earlier, say, testing a new pharmaceutical drug designed to act on the sympathetic nervous system to alter a patient's biological equilibrium, the hurdles for researchers become exponentially higher. External validity. Exactly. Internal validity means the experiment is designed so flawlessly with such tight controls that you can definitively say the drug and only the drug is causing the patient's heart rate to drop. You must eliminate all confounding variables. Which is incredibly hard with humans, because if you give a patient a pill and tell them it's going to lower their heart rate, their heart rate might drop just because they believe it will. The mind completely hijacks the autonomic nervous system. Yeah, the placebo effect is a real measurable physiological shift. Which is why double blind, placebo controlled trials are the gold standard. To protect internal validity, the control group must receive a placebo, an inactive sham pill that looks exactly like the real drug. This ensures that any physiological shift observed in the experimental group is due to the actual chemical mechanism of the drug binding to receptors and not merely the psychological expectation of healing. And then, even if you prove it works in the lab, you have to prove external validity. That just means making sure the results from your tightly controlled, perfectly sterile lab environment actually generalize to regular messy humans out in the real world who might be drinking coffee or take other medications. A drug with high internal validity but low external validity is practically useless to a physician. Okay, so we've covered how a system finds its happy place, how it mathematically describes that place, and how it violently fights back when we stress it out. But this raises a really fascinating deeper question. When we stress a system and force it to shift and make a product, what happens if the reactants actually have more than one option? More than one option. Yeah, like what if the chemical building blocks can be assembled in two different ways? Does the reaction take the easy fast path or does it take the hard stable path? That is a brilliant transition into the final major concept of this chapter, section 6.3, Kinetic and Thermodynamic Control. This is the intersection where raw chemistry meets the concept of pathway decision making. I love this section because it really gives molecules personalities. Many organic reactions can produce two entirely different structural products from the exact same starting materials. The product that forms depends entirely on the specific environmental conditions the chemist chooses, most notably the temperature and the available thermal energy. We define these two diversion options as the kinetic product and the thermodynamic product. The MCAT requires you to fundamentally understand the energy profiles of both pathways. Let's break down their defining characteristics. But flimsy. An evocative description. Let's look at the reaction coordinate graph. The kinetic pathway is characterized by having a significantly lower activation energy, which we denote as EA. The activation energy is the initial hill, the energy barrier, that the reactants must climb over to reach the transition state and start the reaction. And because this hill is relatively small, the kinetic product forms very quickly. It is the fast product. It's easy to make. Any molecule can casually bump over a small hill. But there's a massive catch. Yes. The final destination of the kinetic product is higher in free energy compared to the alternative. It does not drop very far down into the energy valley. Because it retains a higher level of free energy, it's fundamentally less stable. Flimsy as you put it. So it forms in a split second, but the resulting molecule is kind of unstable and reactive. Contrast that with the thermodynamic product, which I nickname slow but sturdy. Returning to our reaction coordinate graph, the thermodynamic pathway is characterized by a massive activation energy. The E A hill is incredibly steep and high. It requires a tremendous input of kinetic thermal energy for the reactant molecules to smash together hard enough to overcome that initial barrier. Consequently, the reaction proceeds much more slowly. It takes time. But the payoff is worth it. The payoff is substantial. While it takes immense effort to get over the hill, once the reaction proceeds, it drops deep down into an energy valley. The final thermodynamic product is significantly lower in free energy. It has a much more negative delta G, making the final molecule highly stable and unreactive. There is a fantastic physics and general chemistry crossover example for this in the UWorld question bank involving pure carbon. Specifically, a diamond versus a lump of graphite, like pencil lead. Both are just pure carbon atoms arranged in different three d lattice structures. The conversion of diamond to graphite is a classic illustration of kinetic versus thermodynamic control. Thermodynamically speaking, graphite is actually the more stable, lower energy form of carbon at standard atmospheric conditions. The change in Gibbs free energy, Delta G, for a diamond rearranging its bonds to turn into graphite is entirely negative. So it's a spontaneous thermodynamically favorable reaction. Yes. Wait, I have to stop you there. Are you telling me that my diamond ring fundamentally wants to turn into pencil lead? That diamonds aren't actually forever? Thermodynamically speaking, no, they are not forever. Your diamond is spontaneously trying to turn into graphite at this very moment. Okay, my mind is blown, To turn into graphite, the carbon atoms in the diamond must break their rigid, incredibly strong tetrahedral sp3 covalent bonds and rearrange themselves into the planar sp2 hybridized sheets of graphite. Which is hard to do. The activation energy, the EA, required to break those initial diamond bonds is so unimaginably massive that the reaction does not proceed at any observable rate at room temperature. So the destination is a beautiful, stable, deep valley, but the mountain you have to climb to get there is basically Mount Everest, and the diamond is at room temperature, it has no climbing gear. Precisely. The diamond is what we call kinetically trapped. It simply does not have the ambient thermal energy available from the environment to overcome the kinetic barrier, even though the thermodynamic destination on the other side is highly favorable. It is a spontaneous reaction that will take millions of years to occur. The diamond is trapped in a state of high energy because it can't afford the toll booth fee to get to the lower energy state. Now let's take this foundational concept of EA versus delta G and apply it directly to organic chemistry. Because this is where kinetic versus thermodynamic control shows up constantly on the MCAT. Specifically regarding the formation of enolates. Oh, enolate chemistry, the bane of every premed's existence. But it actually makes so much sense through this lens. The classic MCAT scenario is regioselectivity. You have an asymmetrical ketone, meaning the carbon chain on the left side of the carbonyl oxygen looks different than the right side. Usually one side is bare and exposed, and the other side is crowded with bulky methyl or ethyl groups. We call that crowding steric hindrance. The bulky electron clouds of the alkyl groups physically block access to the carbon atom. Right. So you introduce a strong base. The base's job is to rip off an alpha proton to form a carbon double bond creating the enolate. Because the ketone is asymmetrical, the base has a choice. It can rip a proton off the bare uncrowded side or it can fight its way into the crowded sterically hindered side. And this choice perfectly mirrors our kinetic versus thermodynamic pathways. I have an analogy for this to help visualize it because trying to picture three d molecules rotating in space is tough. Let's use a movie theater analogy. Imagine you're going to see a blockbuster movie. The previews are over, the lights are down, the movie is starting, and you rush late into a completely packed dark theater. Okay, I am visualizing the crowded theater. The kinetic pathway is like sprinting to the very first open seat you see, which happens to be right by the entrance door in the very front row. It is incredibly fast to get there. It takes almost no physical energy. There is no crowd you have to push through, which is the chemical equivalent of zero steric hindrance. You just drop into the seat. But the seat is highly undesirable. Extremely undesirable. You are in the very front row staring straight up at the screen. You have a terrible view. You get a stiff neck and you are generally miserable. It's a a high energy, unstable, uncomfortable position. That perfectly describes the microscopic reality of the kinetic enolate. The strong base attacks, the less substituted bare alpha carbon simply because it is the path of least resistance. There are no bulky alkyl groups blocking the way. Because there is no steric hindrance to fight through, the reaction requires almost zero activation energy. It happens incredibly fast. But the resulting carbon carbon double bond is on the end of the chain. It has very few neighboring carbon groups to stabilize it through hyperconjugation, making it a higher energy, highly unstable molecule. Exactly. Now let's reset. Same dark, crowded theater. But this time you decide to take the thermodynamic path way. You look around and you see the perfect dead center sweet spot seat right in the middle of the theater. But to get there you have to push past 12 people in the aisle. You have to step on toes, you spill someone's popcorn, you have to actively fight the crowd. You are fighting the steric hindrance. Yes. It takes way more time to get to the middle. It takes a massive expenditure of physical energy to push past all those people. You have to overcome a huge activation energy barrier. But once you finally sit down in that center seat, you are perfectly comfortable. You have the best view in the house. You are highly stable for the rest of the three hour movie. And chemically, that represents the base using significant kinetic thermal energy to literally smash through the bulky, repulsive electron clouds of the steric hindrance. It fights its way in to grab a proton from the more substituted, highly crowded alpha carbon. Which takes heat. It takes substantial heat energy to achieve this highly crowded transition state, making it a much slower reaction. But the payoff is immense. The resulting carbon double bond is internal. It is highly substituted with neighboring carbon groups. Which follows rule, right? Yes. Zaitsev's rule states that the more highly substituted an alkene is, the more thermodynamically stable it is due to electron donation from the neighboring alkyl groups. By forming the internal double bond, the molecule drops into a deep energy valley. It's the highly stable thermodynamic product. So as a chemist standing at a lab bench, how do you play God? How do you force reaction take the front row seat versus the center seat? You control the conditions, the temperature, and the physical size of the base you use. The choice of reagents and environmental conditions is paramount. The MCAT expects you to know exactly which conditions favor which pathway. Let's say a chemist wants to exclusively isolate the unstable kinetic enolate. They must intentionally run the reaction under conditions that favor the fast, low activation energy pathway while actively preventing the molecules from accessing the thermodynamic They literally have to freeze the thermodynamic pathway out. If you want the kinetic product, you drop the temperature of the reaction flask to -seventy eight degrees Celsius using a dry ice and acetone bath. It is freezing cold. At -seventy eight degrees, there is practically zero ambient thermal energy bouncing around in the solvent. The molecules are sluggish. When they collide, they simply kind of gently bump into each other. And because they are just gently bumping, they absolutely do not have violent kinetic energy required to smash through the bulky steric hindrance of the center seat. They can't overcome the high E barrier. They are physically barred from the thermodynamic pathway. So they take the easy way out, they grab the unhindered proton. Furthermore, the chemist will pair that freezing temperature with a massive sterically bulky base. The classic example is LDA lithium disopyramide. LDA is huge. It has these two massive bulky isopropyl groups hanging off of it because the base itself is so phenomenally large, it is like a giant trying to squeeze into a tiny car. The LDA literally bounces off the crowded, sterically hindered side of the ketone. Its electron clouds repel each other. It physically cannot fit. Conversely, if the chemist desires the highly stable thermodynamic enolate, they must actively provide this system with the tools to overcome the steric entrance and scale that massive activation energy mountain. So you turn up the heat, you run the reaction at room temp, or you even attach a condenser and actively boil it. That provides massive amounts of ambient thermal energy. The molecules are zooming around violently colliding with each other with enough force to smash right through the steric hindrance. And you pair that high heat with a very small, highly unhindered base. The classic MCAT example is sodium hydride A hydride ion is literally just a single hydrogen atom with an extra electron. It is microscopic. It is a tiny, highly aggressive dart. Because it is so small, the hydrogen ion easily threads the needle. It penetrates the steric crowd of the more substituted alpha carbon with minimal resistance to abstract the interior proton. Now, because there is so much heat in the system, the reaction is completely reversible. The fast forming kinetic products might form initially, but they have enough energy to reverse back into reactants. Over time, because the thermodynamic product drops into such a deep, stable energy valley, it gets stuck there. The system will equilibrate and overwhelmingly favor the highly stable thermodynamic product. Small base, high heat equals thermodynamic center seat. Huge base, freezing cold equals kinetic front row. It's just incredible how elegant organic chemistry is when you actually understand the underlying physical mechanisms. You aren't just looking at a flashcard trying to memorize LDA equals less substituted. You can actually visualize in three d space why the giant base and the lack of heat physically force the reaction to take the easy way out. And that profound level of three d mechanistic comprehension is exactly what the MCAT test writers are looking for. Rope memorization will absolutely fail you when the exam presents you with a novel, unfamiliar molecule you've never seen before. But if you deeply understand the push and pull of electron density, the physical reality of steric hindrance, and the thermodynamic drives of the universe, you'll be able to logic your way through any problem. So let's take a deep breath and bring this all home. If we look back at the incredible amount of material we've unpacked in this hour, starting from the escalator analogy of dynamic equilibrium, exploring the math of KEq and the GPS of Q, diving into the body's autonomic nervous system mirroring Le Chatelier's principle and finally visualizing the choice between fast, flimsy kinetic products and slow, sturdy, thermodynamic products. The overarching summary is that mastering equilibrium is not just an academic exercise to pass a general chemistry section, it is about understanding the fundamental balance that dictates life itself. Everything ties back to it. Everything. The concepts we've discussed today dictate how enzymes selectively bind to substrates, governed by their dissociation constant These principles govern the common eye effect, which determines the exact solubility of life saving medications as they circulate in the human bloodstream. Right. And let's ground this in what you will actually be doing in a few years as a physician. Let's say you are in the ER and a patient comes in hyperventilating in severe respiratory distress. You order a metabolic panel to check their blood pH and you see they're in severe lactic acidosis. Their blood is flooded with acid. Exactly. You aren't just looking at random numbers on a lab chart. You are looking at a living, breathing application of the reaction quotient, Q, violently pulled out of balance. The sudden influx of lactic acid is a massive concentration stress. It drops the blood pH, meaning there is an excess of H plus ions. Le Chalier's principle instantly takes over. The bicarbonate buffer system in the patient's blood shifts violently to the left, consuming the dangerous acid and converting it into CO2 gas and water. And the patient is hyperventilating rapidly blowing off CO2 gas from their lungs because the body is desperately siphoning off the product to drive the reaction further, attempting to return Q back to the physiological KEQ. It is the ultimate tug of war happening inside their veins. Which leaves me with a final, slightly provocative thought for you to mull over as you close your books and take a break today. We have just spent an entire hour talking about how every single chemical system in the universe naturally, spontaneously wants to reach equilibrium. It wants to maximize its entropy, minimize its free energy and reach that peaceful state Delta G equals zero. That is the inescapable thermodynamic destiny of the entire universe. But here's the wild part about biology and medicine. We, as living human organisms, spend our entire biological lives burning massive amounts of glucose, synthesizing endless ATP, just to purposefully keep our bodily systems out of chemical equilibrium. We run ion pumps constantly to keep sodium high on the outside of the cell and potassium high on the inside. Because in biology, if your cells actually reach true chemical equilibrium, equilibrium, if the forward and reverse reactions perfectly balance out and Delta G hits zero, you are dead. A profound biological truth. True equilibrium in a biological organism is clinical death. We must continuously, ceaselessly use metabolic energy to maintain a highly regulated, highly stressed state of dynamic disequilibrium. We fight the thermodynamic math every single second of our lives just to stay alive. We use energy to fight the math. I love that so much. Keep that in mind next time you're staring at a textbook reviewing these equations, you aren't just looking at numbers on a page. You are literally studying the physical forces your body is violently fighting against right now. This has been an incredibly deep dive. Three hours of real focus absolutely beats eight hours of fighting the urge to take a nap on your own couch. So, with that in mind, let's transition into the core of today's deep dive. Let's do it. We are opening General Chemistry Chapter seven: Thermo Chemistry. Broadly speaking, this is the study of the energy changes that accompany chemical and physical processes. It's the ultimate accounting system of the universe. We are tracking where the heat goes, where the energy comes from, and basically predicting whether a chemical reaction is going to happen on its own. Right, because energy is never created, and it is never destroyed. It is merely moved around and transformed from one state to another. And thermochemistry provides the mathematical and conceptual framework to track those movements with absolute precision. So let's start building that framework. Okay, so our first major area is Section 7.1 Systems and Processes. And we're also going to weave the Ziroth and First Laws of Thermodynamics in here, just because they set the ground rules. Yes. Because before we can study energy changing hands, the very first thing we have to do is draw a boundary. We have to explicitly define what we're looking at. Right. And that boundary separates reality into two distinct concepts: the system and the surroundings. By definition, a thermodynamic system is simply the matter that is being observed. It is the specific, localized part of the universe that you care about for a given calculation. And the surroundings, which are also called the environment, are literally everything else in the entire universe. And the fascinating nuance here, the thing that trips up a lot of students when they set up a physics or chemistry problem is that the boundary you draw is completely arbitrary. Like you, the scientist, get to decide where the system ends and where the surroundings begin depending entirely on what question you are actually trying to answer. A classic, tangible example of this is a hot cup of coffee sitting on a table. If your goal is to calculate the rate of heat transferring from the hot dark liquid into the physical ceramic material of the mug, then you must define your system as just the liquid coffee. In that specific calculation, the ceramic mug is part of the surroundings. The boundary is the inner wall of the mug. But what if someone asks a different question? Like what if the boundary shifts and the question asks how much heat the coffee and the mug acting together are radiating into the air of the room? In that scenario, your system expands. The system is now the liquid coffee and the ceramic mug, And the surrounding environment is the air in the room. Okay. And you can just keep pushing that theoretical boundary outward. You can define the system as the coffee, the mug, and the entire room measuring heat loss to the rest of the house. You can push it outward until the system encompasses the entire universe. At which point there are no surroundings left. Exactly. Okay, so once we have drawn our arbitrary boundary, we have to classify the system based on what we allow to cross that boundary. The sources detail three very specific types of thermodynamic systems. Isolated, closed, and open systems. Let's walk through these starting with the most restrictive. An isolated system is essentially a locked vault. In an isolated system, neither energy, which includes heat and physical work nor matter, can be exchanged with the surroundings. It is completely sealed off from the rest of the universe. What's a real world example of that? Like does a perfectly isolated system actually exist? A perfectly isolated system is a theoretical ideal, much like a frictionless surface in physics. The closest we get in a practical laboratory setting is a heavily insulated bomb calorimeter. The thick layers of thermal prevent heat from transferring in or out, and the sealed steel reaction chamber prevents any gases or physical matter from escaping. So for the duration of a short experiment, we treat it as an isolated system. Makes sense. Next Next on the list is a closed system. So we relax the rules just a little bit here. We do. A closed system allows for the exchange of energy, but it strictly prohibits the exchange of matter. Right. The system can absorb heat, it can radiate heat, it can expand and do physical work on the outside world, but absolutely no actual atoms or molecules are allowed to cross the boundary. I'm picturing an old school cast iron steam radiator in a cold apartment. Like the boiler pumps hot steam into the radiator. The energy radiates out through the iron into the room warming up the air. But the water molecules themselves stay trapped completely inside the closed loop of the pipes. So energy leaves, matter stays. That is a perfect illustration. Another classic example, and one you will see constantly in thermodynamics problems, is a gas trapped inside a cylinder by a movable piston. Oh yeah, the classic piston. You can hold a blowtorch to the bottom of the cylinder to add heat energy. The gas can expand, pushing the piston upward, which transfers mechanical energy work to the outside world. So energy is crossing the boundary? Yes. But the piston is tightly sealed, so no gas molecules can escape. It remains a closed system. And finally, we arrive at the open system, which is basically a free for all. It is. An open system actively exchanges both energy and matter with its surroundings. It is by far the most common type of system in the natural world. Like a pot of water boiling. Exactly. A simple pot of water boiling on a stove without a lid is an open system. Heat energy is entering the system from the burner below. Simultaneously, water vapor actual matter is leaving the system and floating away into the air of the kitchen. And maybe the most important open system for anyone studying for a medical exam is the human body. We are remarkably complex open systems. We absolutely are. We ingest physical matter in the form of food and oxygen. We expel physical matter as carbon dioxide and waste. That is a constant exchange mass. And at the same time, we are radiating thermal energy into the room, absorbing heat from the sun, and exerting mechanical work on our environment. The human body is a textbook open system. It's constantly fighting to maintain its internal balance despite that constant exchange. Now, to understand how all these systems interact with their surroundings, we need to lay down the foundational rules of the universe, starting with the zeroth law of thermodynamics. The zeroth law. I always found it funny that it's called the zeroth law rather than the first. Like, they discovered the first and second laws and then later realized there was an assumption so basic, so utterly fundamental to the math, that they had to go back and insert a new law before number one. It's true. It is the bedrock that allows thermal physics to exist. The zeroth law states that if System A is in thermal equilibrium with System B, and System B is in thermal equilibrium with System C Then System A must inherently be in thermal equilibrium with System C. Exactly. If I'm translating this into basic algebra, it's just the transitive property. If A equals B and B equals C, then A absolutely must equal C. But here we are applying that logic to temperature. That underlying logic is identical. We need to explicitly define what thermal equilibrium actually means physically. Okay, what does it mean? Two objects are in thermal equilibrium when there is absolutely no net flow of heat between them. They might be touching, microscopic energy might be bouncing back and forth, but the macroscopic net transfer of heat is zero. Got it. And the only condition under which net heat flow is zero is when both objects are at the exact same temperature. So why is this transitive property so crucial that it gets elevated to the status of Law zero? Because it is the ultimate validation for the existence and use of thermometers. Let's map it out visually. System B is your thermometer. You take that thermometer and submerge it into a hot cup of tea, which is system A. Okay. You wait. Eventually the tea and the thermometer reach thermal equilibrium. The liquid mercury inside the thermometer expands to a very specific line on the glass. You pull the thermometer out. Okay, so system A and system B are at the same temperature. Then you take that exact same thermometer, system B, and you submerge it into a completely different cup of tea across the room, which is system C. You wait again. Right. If the mercury expands to that exact same line on the glass, you have proven that system B is in thermal equilibrium with system C. And because of the Zeroth Law, I now definitively know that system A and system C are at the exact same temperature, even though those two cups of tea never physically touched each other. Exactly. The zeroth law mathematically guarantees that if you were to take those two cups of tea and mix them together in a giant bowl, no net heat would flow between the liquids. It proves that temperature is an objective, universal property that can be measured and compared across disconnected systems. It feels like common sense, but having that rigorous mathematical proof is what allows us to trust our instruments. So now that we trust our thermometers, let's step up to the first law of thermodynamics, which is arguably the most heavily tested and applied law in chemical calculations. The First Law is the formal statement of the conservation of energy. It dictates that the total energy of the universe is a constant. Energy can never be magically created from nothing, and it can never be destroyed into nothingness. It can only be transferred or transformed. Right. And for a closed system, we express this conservation with a very specific vital equation: delta U equals Q minus W. Let's stay in professor mode and break that equation down variable by variable. I am looking at Delta U equals Q minus W. Let's start with Delta U. What is that capturing? Delta U represents the change in the internal energy of your defined system. Conceptually, internal energy is the grand total sum of all the kinetic energies the microscopic vibrations, rotations, and translational movements of the molecules plus all the potential energy locked up in the chemical bonds and intermolecular attractions of every single particle contained within your boundary. Wow. Okay, so it is the complete energy inventory of the system. Exactly. So U is the energy bank account of the system And Delta U is the net change to that account. What about Q? Q represents the heat added to the system. This is thermal energy crossing your boundary, flowing from the surroundings into your system. And W. W represents the work done by the system. This is mechanical energy crossing the boundary flowing outward from your system into the surroundings. Right, the classic visual is our trapped gas expanding and physically pushing a heavy piston upward against the weight of the atmosphere. Notice the prepositions the professor used there. Because the sign conventions, like positive versus negative, are where the traps are laid out on exam day. The equation again is delta U equals Q minus W. Let me reason through this. If Q is a positive number, that means heat is being added to the system. Right. Energy is entering the bank account, which increases the internal energy, U. That makes intuitive sense. Now reason through the work variable, W. Okay, if W is a positive number, that means work is being done by the system. The system is expanding, it's pushing a piston, it is physically exerting effort on the outside world. And exerting effort costs energy. Exactly. The system is spending its energy to move that piston. So if this system does work, its internal energy bank account should drop. And looking at the equation delta U equals Q minus W, there's a minus sign right in front of the W. Yes. So if W is a positive 50 joules of work done, the equation subtracts 50 joules from the internal energy. The math perfectly matches the physical reality. That is the exact logical progression you must use. Let's flip it though. What if the surrounding environment does work on the system? Say, a heavy weight is placed on the piston, and it forcefully compresses the gas inside the cylinder. In that case, the system isn't doing the work, the work is being done to it. So W would be a negative number. Right. And if I plug a negative number into Delta, U equals Q minus W, I get a minus a negative. Subtracting a negative is the same as adding a positive, so delta U goes up. You are literally packing energy into the system by physically crushing it into a smaller volume. So the internal energy skyrockets. Okay, so that is the master equation for a closed system. But what happens to that equation when we put the system under specific controlled constraints? The sources highlight four fundamental thermodynamic processes. Yes, and these are usually visualized on what's called a PV graph, a pressure volume graph. So if you're visualizing this, pressure's on the I axis and volume is on the x axis. We need to walk through all four of these processes. For each one, we will visualize its shape on the PV graph. And we will see exactly how the first law equation, delta u equals q minus w, simplifies under that specific constraint. Let's begin with the first process, isothermal. Okay, I can decode the root words there. Iso means same and thermal relates to temperature. So an isothermal process is one that happens at a constant That is the defining constraint. In an isothermal process, the temperature of the system is artificially held completely constant. This is usually done by immersing the system in a massive water bath that absorbs or provides heat instantly. Right. Now recall our definition of internal energy. A massive component of internal energy is the kinetic energy of the molecules, and temperature is a direct measure of that average kinetic energy. If the temperature doesn't change, the average kinetic energy doesn't change. Therefore the internal energy, U, remains constant. Exactly. Which means the change in internal energy, delta U, is exactly zero. Okay, taking that a step further, let's plug that into the first law. Delta U equals Q minus W. If Delta U is zero, the equation becomes zero equals Q minus W. And if you move W to the other side of the equal sign, we get Q equals W. What does that mathematical simplification tell us physically? It tells you that any heat added to the system, Q, is immediately and completely used by the system to do an equal amount of work, W, on the surroundings. Energy flows in as heat and instantaneously flows out as work. It's a perfect pass through, which is why the internal temperature never fluctuates. And what does this perfect pass through look like on our pressure volume graph? On a PV graph, an isothermal process appears as a hyperbolic curve sloping downward from left to right. It is not a straight diagonal line. As the volume increases on the x axis, the pressure on the y axis drops off sharply at first and then levels out, forming a smooth curve. And crucially, in physics and chemistry, the area underneath the curve on a PV graph represents the physical work done by the gas. So the area under this hyperbolic curve is the work, W. Which we just proved is also exactly equal to the heat, Q, that entered the system. Okay, that's process one. The second process is called adiabatic. That's a word that sounds intimidating and doesn't immediately give away its meaning like isothermal does. Adiabatic refers to a process where there is absolutely no heat exchange between the system and the environment. The system is perfectly, flawlessly insulated. Think back to our bomb calorimeter wrapped in thick foam. So if no heat can cross the boundary, then heat transfer, Q, is exactly equal to zero. Right, let's go back to the first law equation. Delta U = Q W. If the insulation is perfect, Q zero. That leaves us with delta U = negative W. The implications of that simplified equation are profound. They really are. The change in the internal energy of the system is now entirely dictated by the physical work done. Imagine our insulated cylinder of gas. If the gas expands and pushes the piston up, it is doing work. W is positive. And according to our simplified equation, delta U equals negative W, the internal energy must drop. And if the internal energy drops, the temperature of the gas plummets drastically. Wow. The gas cools down rapidly as it expands, even though it hasn't lost a single joule of heat to the outside room. It cooled down because it spent its own internal kinetic energy to physically move the heavy piston. Visually on our PV graph, how do we spot an adiabatic process? It looks remarkably similar to the isothermal curve. It is also a hyperbolic curve sloping downward from left to right. However, an adiabatic curve is typically much steeper. Okay, what's the visual trick to remember that? Professor. The trick is to remember that an isothermal curve traces a single constant temperature line. An eight adiabatic curve, because the temperature is actively dropping as it expands, cuts across multiple temperature thresholds, diving down much faster. Okay, two down, two to go. The third process is isobaric breaking down the word. Iso is same, and baric relates to barometric pressure, constant pressure. This is the most straightforward process to identify on a graph. If pressure is plotted on the y and the pressure never changes, then the graph is a perfectly flat horizontal line. Makes sense. As the volume expands or compresses on the x axis, you just trace a straight horizontal line to left or right. Does an isobaric constraint simplify the first law equation to zero like the others did? It does not. In an isobaric process both heat transfer and work are actively occurring and the internal energy is changing. The equation remains delta U= Q W. Oh, okay. However, the math for calculating the work becomes incredibly easy. We said earlier that work is the area under the curve on a PV graph. And the area under a perfectly flat horizontal line is literally just a rectangle. Exactly. You don't need complex calculus to find the area of a rectangle. It's just base times height. The base is the change in volume, delta V. The height is the constant pressure, P. So the formula for work in an isobaric process is simply W equals P times delta V. Simple geometry. Which brings us to the fourth and final thermodynamic process, isovolumetric. It's also frequently called isocort constant volume. Picture our cylinder of gas, but this time someone has driven a steel pin through the piston, locking it immovably in place. The volume is completely restricted. It cannot change. If the volume cannot change, the gas cannot physically expand or compress. Right. And if it cannot expand or compress, it cannot move the piston. If the piston doesn't move, no physical work is being done. So W equals zero. Building on that, take it back to the first law equation: delta U equals Q minus W. If W is mathematically zero, the equation simplifies to delta U equals Q. What does that mean in the real world, like practically speaking? It means that if you take a blowtorch and add heat to this locked cylinder, every single joule of that heat energy goes directly into changing the internal energy of the gas. The molecules vibrate faster and faster, the temperature spikes dramatically, and the internal pressure builds to dangerous levels. Because none of that incoming heat energy is being siphoned off to do work moving a piston. It is all trapped as internal energy. Precisely. And on our PV graph, if the volume on the x axis is fixed and never changes, we just have a perfectly straight vertical line going straight up as the pressure builds or straight down as it cools. And what is the geometric area underneath a perfectly vertical infinitely thin line? It's zero. There is no width to calculate an area. Which is the perfect visual confirmation of what we just reasoned out mathematically. The work done is exactly zero. That thoroughly covers Section 7.1. We have defined our boundaries, established the transitive laws of temperature, and mapped out exactly how energy crosses those boundaries as heat or work under four specific constraints. Awesome. This transitions us perfectly into Section 7.2, which Kaplan titles States and State Functions. We now know how a system can undergo a process, how it can expand isothermally or heat up isobarically. But to fully map out thermodynamics, we need a way to differentiate between the winding path a system takes and the final destination it ultimately reaches. This distinction is paramount in chemistry. We divide all measurable properties into two distinct categories: state functions and process functions. Let's start with state functions. A state function describes a specific property of a system at a defined equilibrium state. And the absolute defining characteristic of a state function is that it depends only on the current state of the system, absolutely and completely, regardless of the path taken to arrive at that state. To make this tangible, my favorite analogy for state functions is hiking a mountain. Think of a state function like your final altitude on that mountain. Although that's a good one. Yeah. If you were standing at the summit and the summit is exactly 10,000 feet above sea level, your altitude is 10,000 feet. It does not matter one bit how you got there. Exactly. You could have rock climbed straight up the vertical sheer cliff face in two hours. Or you could have taken a gentle winding switchback trail that circled the mountain for three days. Regardless of the wildly different paths, your current state, your altitude is identical. Altitude is the state function. That is a flawless physical analogy. In stark contrast, a process function, which is also commonly referred to as a path function, describes the specific quantitative pathway taken to transition between states. Process functions are entirely 100% dependent on how you got there. Taking it back to the mountain hike. The process functions are the actual physical distance you walked and the total number of calories you burned along the way. The hiker who took the winding three day switchback trail walked dozens of miles and burned thousands of calories. The rock climber who went straight up the cliff face walked a much shorter distance and burned a completely different amount of calories. Exactly. The path taken drastically changed those numbers even though they both ended up at the exact same ten zero foot state. In thermodynamics, the two major process functions you need to constantly track are: work and heat Yes. The amount of physical work done by a gas it, depends entirely on whether you used an isothermal path, an adiabatic path, an isobaric path, and so on. We saw that visually with the different areas under the curves on the PV graphs, work and heat are path dependent. But there are many, many more state functions. Things that only care about the destination. To keep them all straight for exam day, the sources provide a classic mnemonic. It's a bit ridiculous, but that's why it sticks in your memory. Are you ready for this professor? Let's hear it. When I'm under pressure and feeling dense, all I want to do is watch TV and get hugs. It is deeply silly, yes. But let's systematically unpack the variables hidden in that sentence. Pressure obviously stands for pressure. P, dense, stands for density, which is often represented by the Greek letter rho. TV gives us temperature, T, and volume V. And the acronym HUGS at the end covers the heavy hitters. Internal energy, U, Gibbs free energy, G, and entropy, S. So pressure, density, temperature, volume, enthalpy, internal energy, Gibbs free energy, entropy, every single one of those is a state function. Path independent. Now because these state functions can change drastically depending on the environment like, the volume of a gas is vastly different in a freezing room versus a hot oven. Scientists desperately needed a universal baseline. Right, we need to compare apples to apples. We need a standardized way to compare the enthalpy of a reaction performed in a lab in Chile, London with the exact same reaction performed in a hot lab in Singapore. To achieve this, chemists defined a baseline called standard conditions. And here is where I need to issue a massive blinking red light trap warning for the listener. Listen carefully. Standard conditions are defined as a temperature of 25 degrees Celsius, which is two ninety eight Kelvin. The pressure is exactly one atmosphere. And if you are dealing with solutions, the concentration is one molar. Standard conditions are used specifically for measuring kinetics, equilibrium, and thermodynamics. When you look at an equation and you see a little degree symbol like standard enthalpy delta H naught or standard Gibbs free energy delta G naught, that little circle signifies that the measurement was taken under standard conditions. At a comfortable room temperature of 25 degrees Celsius. Right. But UT, standard temperature and pressure, which is always abbreviated as STP, is a completely different baseline created for a completely different purpose. Right. STP is defined as a freezing zero degrees Celsius, which is two seventy three Kelvin, and one atmosphere of pressure. STP is used exclusively, strictly only for ideal gas calculations. The trap there is understanding why there are two baselines. Ideal gas laws, like PV NRT, rely on standardizing the volume of a gas like one mole of the ideal gas at STP occupies exactly 22.4 liters. It's a fundamental physics baseline set at the freezing point of water. But real world bench chemistry, you know, the thermodynamics of mixing acids and bases, that happens at room temperature. You don't do most liquid chemistry in a freezing room. So they created standard conditions at 25 Celsius for thermodynamics. Precisely. If a question is asking you to calculate the heat of a chemical reaction, you must use two ninety eight Kelvin. If a question is asking you about the volume of an expanding balloon filled with ideal helium gas, you must use two seventy three Kelvin. Mixing those two baselines up will inevitably lead you to a trap answer choice designed to catch that exact mistake. Makes total sense. Now under those standard conditions, every element exists in its most energetically stable natural form, which we call its standard state. For example, the standard state of oxygen at room temperature is O2 gas. Calculations for the heat of formation later in the chapter. Speaking of states of matter we need to examine how substances transition between them. This brings us to phase diagrams. This is another crucial graph that you must be able to visualize and interpret in your mind. Let's paint the picture of a phase diagram. It plots pressure on the y axis against temperature on the x axis. It essentially provides a map showing specific combinations of temperature and pressure under which a substance will settle into being a solid, a liquid, or a gas. The general shape of a standard phase diagram like the one for water or carbon dioxide looks roughly like a capital letter Y leaning heavily to the right. Yes, and the three large empty spaces between the branches of the Y represent the pure phases. The region to the far left at low temperatures is the solid phase. The region in the middle, cradled in the top of the Y at higher temperatures and pressures, is the liquid phase. Phase. And the vast region to the right and bottom, at high temperatures and low pressures, is the gas phase. The solid black lines forming the Y are the actual boundaries.
