If you adjust the temperature or pressure and cross one of those lines, you trigger a phase change. You melt, or you boil, or you freeze. Those boundary lines are lines of phase equilibria. Like, if you adjust your conditions so that you are sitting exactly on the line between solid and liquid, the substance exists as both ice and water simultaneously in a state of dynamic equilibrium. Right. The microscopic rate of the ice melting is exactly perfectly balanced by the rate of the water freezing back into ice. But I want to zoom in on the specific visual landmarks of this graph, the critical intersections. First, let's look right in the middle where all the lines converge at the center of the Y. That intersection is called the triple point. The triple point is a fascinating thermodynamic anomaly. It represents the one specific, utterly unique combination of temperature and pressure at which all three phases, solid, liquid and gas, exist in thermodynamic equilibrium simultaneously. The boundary lines separating solid from liquid, liquid from gas, and solid from gas all crash into each other at this exact coordinate. It's wild to picture that in reality. If you had a sealed glass chamber at the exact triple point of water, you would see chunks of ice floating in liquid water, and the liquid water would be actively boiling into steam while the steam was simultaneously frosting back into solid ice on the glass. It's chaotic. All three chaotic processes happening in the exact same container at the same time, perfectly balanced. It is a remarkable state. Now let's trace a different path. Follow the boundary line separating the liquid phase and the gas phase. Follow it upward and to the right as temperature and pressure both climb higher and higher. Eventually that boundary line doesn't intersect anything, it simply stops. It abruptly terminates in the middle of the graph. That sudden end point is the critical point. The critical point. This is a concept that is hard to wrap your head around at first. What does it mean for the boundary between a liquid and a gas to just, well, end? It means that at temperatures and pressures above that specific point, the liquid phase and the gas phase become physically and mathematically indistinguishable from one another. Walk me through how that physically happens. How can a sloshing liquid and an invisible gas become the exact same thing? Think about what happens to density. As you heat a liquid up in a sealed container, the liquid expands. As it expands, its density steadily decreases. Simultaneously, that heat is boiling some of the liquid into a gas and the pressure inside the sealed container is skyrocketing. As the pressure of a gas increases, it gets compressed and its density steadily increases. So the liquid is getting less dense and the gas is getting more dense. Precisely. And at the exact coordinate of the critical point, those two densities meet in the middle. The density of the incredibly hot liquid and the density of the highly compressed gas become perfectly exactly equal. That's crazy. If you were watching this in a high pressure glass cell, the visible meniscus, the distinct line separating the sloshing liquid at the bottom from the empty space at the top would literally blur and disappear before your eyes. The distinct phases vanish, replaced by a uniform homogeneous substance called a supercritical fluid. Supercritical fluids sound like science fiction, but they are used in industry all the time. Like if you drink decaffeinated coffee, the caffeine was likely extracted from the beans using supercritical carbon dioxide. Oh really? Yeah. It acts like a gas penetrating deep into the microscopic pores of the coffee bean but it also acts like a liquid solvent dissolving and carrying away the caffeine molecules. Yeah. It's both things at once. But beyond the inductor application, what is the thermodynamic significance of crossing that critical point? The primary thermodynamic consequence is that because there is no longer a physical phase boundary to cross, the heat of vaporization, the latent energy required to transition a liquid into a gas, becomes exactly zero. You cannot boil something that is already fundamentally indistinguishable from a gas. The concept of boiling simply ceases to exist above the critical point. That is fascinating. Okay, we've established our arbitrary boundaries, our thermodynamic states, and mapped out the phases of matter. Now, we need to zoom in on the primary mechanism, the process function that drives almost all of thermochemistry. This brings us to Section 7.3, Heat. This section demands that we immediately clear up a major semantic confusion that plagues students. In everyday casual language, we use the words temperature and heat as if they are interchangeable synonyms. Yeah, do. We say it's really hot outside or we say turn up the heat on the stove to raise the temperature. In the strict language of thermodynamics, temperature and heat are fundamentally completely different concepts. Let's draw a hard impenetrable line between them. Let's start with temperature. Temperature, symbolized by a capital T, is a scaled quantitative measure of the average kinetic energy of the individual particles within a substance. Crucially, temperature is a state function. If you look at a substance microscopically, and the molecules are vibrating intensely and zipping around at high speeds, the temperature is high. If the molecules are sluggish and vibrating slowly, the temperature is low. Temperature is simply a measurement of internal molecular motion. Okay, so if temperature is just a speedometer for the molecules, what is heat? Heat symbolized by a lowercase or uppercase Q is a process function. It is the actual physical transfer of thermal energy from one object to another. And this transfer only ever occurs because there's a difference in temperature between those two objects. Heat is the movement, the transit of energy. Temperature is the static state of the particles. My favorite analogy for this is a highway system. Temperature is a measure of how fast the individual cars are driving on the highway. High temperature means the cars are going 80 miles an hour. I like that. Heat, on the other hand, is the actual flow of traffic moving from one city to another city. You can't measure traffic flow by just looking at one car's speed speedometer. You have to measure the movement between locations. That analogy holds up perfectly. And extending it, the laws of thermodynamics dictate the direction of that traffic. Heat always, universally, flows spontaneously from the object with the higher temperature to the object with the lower temperature. The fast moving molecules physically collide with the slow moving molecules. Yes, transferring kinetic energy until the speeds average out. Heat flows until thermal equilibrium is reached, which ties us beautifully right back to the Ziroff Law we discussed earlier. To mathematically calculate exactly how much heat is transferring in that traffic flow, we use what is arguably the most famous equation in introductory chemistry. It's the formula for a specific heat capacity. Q equals MC delta T. And delightfully, the classic mnemonic for remembering this formula is literally built right into the letters. Q equals MCAT. You look at the delta symbol, the triangle, it looks like a capital letter A. So Q equals MC delta T spells out Q equals MCK. It's perfect. It really is. Let's explicitly define the variables in that equation. Q is the heat energy absorbed or released by the substance, typically measured in joules or sometimes calories. M is the mass of the substance, usually in grams. C is the specific heat capacity. And delta T is the change in temperature, which can be measured in either Celsius or Kelvin. Right, because the size of one degree Celsius is identical to the size of one Kelvin. The change is the same magnitude. Let's talk about that little C, the specific heat capacity. This is an intrinsic property of the material itself. Different materials resist changing temperature in different ways. By definition, specific heat capacity is the exact amount of energy required to raise the temperature of exactly one gram of a given substance by exactly one degree Celsius. And there is one specific heat value that you absolutely must commit to memory. The MCAT will frequently expect you to know it without providing it in the passage. It is the specific heat of liquid water. In calories, it is a beautifully simple number. One calorie per gram kelvin. One calorie raises one gram by one degree. In Joules, which is the standard SI unit, it is 4.184 Joules per gram Kelvin. That is an incredibly high specific heat compared to metals or rocks. It is remarkably high, and that single physical property of water shapes our entire It is why a watch pot of water seems to take forever to boil on a hot stove. The water is absorbing massive amounts of heat energy without its temperature rising very fast. But on a macro scale, it is why the Earth's massive oceans act as a global thermal buffer, regulating the climate. The oceans absorb immense heat from the sun during the day and slowly release it at night, preventing extreme temperature swings. Now I want to push back on the math of the Q=MC delta T equation because this is a place where I and many other students have gotten tangled up in algebraic knots. Oh definitely. Let's go back to an earlier example. Pouring cold cream into hot coffee. Heat is going to transfer from the hot coffee into the cold cream until they reach thermal equilibrium. If a test question asks me to calculate the final temperature of the mixed drink, I try to set up the equation. For the coffee, I write MC times T final minus T initial. I do the same for the cream. Yeah, that makes sense. But the coffee is losing heat, so its temperature change is negative. And the cream is gaining heat, so its change is positive. And trying to keep track of where to put the negative signs in the algebra becomes an absolute nightmare. You are describing a very common, very frustrating algebraic trap. The formal textbook way to set up that problem is to state that the sum of all heat changes in an isolated system is zero. Qsystem plus Qsurroundings zero. But setting it up that way often leads to sign errors during the calculation. The MCAT shortcut, the far more reliable way to set up the math, is to use a simple equivalence statement: Q cold equals negative Q hot. Let's walk through why that works so much better. The physical reality is that every single joule of heat gained by the cold object is exactly equal to the heat lost by the hot object. Conservation of energy. So on the left side of your equal sign, you write out the mc delta t expression for the cold cream. On the right side of the equal sign, you write out the mc delta t expression for the hot coffee. But before you do any math, put a big explicit negative sign in front of the entire right side. And the T final variable will be exactly the same on both sides of the equation because we established via the zeroth law that they will reach thermal equilibrium at the same temperature. Correct. By explicitly setting it up as Q cold equals negative Q hot, the negative sign you placed on the right side will naturally cancel out the negative value that results from the coffee's temperature dropping. Right, because T final minus a higher T initial is a negative number. Exactly. The algebra resolves smoothly into positive numbers and you never have to blindly guess which side needs a negative sign to balance the equation. Okay, that makes complete sense for mixing two liquids. But I have another trap to ask about. What happens to the math if we are adding heat to a solid block of ice? Oh, phase changes. Yeah, we turn on the burner. We use our equation Q equals MC delta T. The temperature of the ice goes up from -ten to -five until it hits exactly And then the thermometer stops moving, the ice begins to melt into water. During that melting process, the thermometer stays rigidly locked at The change in temperature, delta T, is exactly zero. But wait, if I plug delta T=zero into the Q=MC delta T equation, the math tells me that Q=zero, that no heat is being added, but the burner is still on, heat is absolutely still pouring into the system. The equation is lying to me. The equation is not lying. You have simply pushed it past its boundary conditions. This is a critical concept regarding heating curves and phase changes. You have hit the fundamental limit of the Q equals MC delta T equation. That equation is only valid, only mathematically true, when the substance is remaining completely in a single stable phase. All solid, all liquid or all gas. And its temperature is actively changing. So during a phase change like melting or boiling, the temperature remains totally constant. The graph flatlines. Exactly. So if the heat energy entering the system isn't raising the temperature, what is it actually doing? It is performing the physical work of overcoming intramolecular forces. In our solid block of ice, the water molecules are locked into a rigid, highly organized crystal lattice by strong hydrogen bonds. Right, they're stuck. To transition that solid into a liquid, you don't need the molecules to vibrate faster in place, which is what a temperature increase would measure. Instead, you need to physically break those hydrogen bonds so the molecules can break free of the lattice and flow fluidly past each other. Oh, so every single joule of heat being added by the burner during that flat line is consumed entirely by the effort of snapping those intermolecular bonds? Yes. The kinetic energy, the temperature cannot rise again until every last bond is broken and the entire mass is melted into liquid. Once the last crystal of ice melts, the flat line ends. The temperature can start rising again as a liquid and we go right back to using the Q equals M cat equation. Correct. But if we need to calculate exactly how much heat was added during that flat line phase change, we have to abandon the specific heat equation and switch to a different formula entirely. During a phase change, we use the equation Q=mL. Let's break down those variables. Q is still the total heat added or removed. M is still the mass of the substance. But L is a new variable the latent heat of transformation. If the substance is melting from solid to liquid, L is specifically the latent heat of fusion. If the substance is boiling from liquid to gas, L is the latent heat of vaporization. Right, it is a constant value that tells you exactly how much energy is required to push one gram of that specific substance across the phase boundary. This concept of latent heat explains a very practical, very painful phenomenon in medicine and cooking. It's the reason why a burn from steam is vastly more severe and tissue damaging than a burn from boiling water, even if both the steam and the water are at the exact same temperature of 100 degrees Celsius. It is a brutal but perfect biological application of thermodynamics. When 100 degree liquid water hits your skin, it cools down, transferring its kinetic energy, its Q equals MC delta T heat, into your tissue. Okay, but what about the steam? When 100 degree steam hits your skin before it even begins to cool down, it undergoes a phase change. It condenses from a gas back into a liquid. During that condensation phase change, it must release all of the latent heat of vaporization, L, that it absorbed when it was originally boiled. Ouch. Yeah, it dumps a massive concentrated payload of latent energy directly into your skin cells and then the resulting 100 degree water starts to cool down causing even more damage. It's a double hit of thermal energy. Finally, to wrap up this section on heat, we need to touch on the laboratory equipment used to actually measure these heat transfers. This is the science of calorimetry. We briefly touched on the two primary types of calorimeters when we discussed thermodynamic systems earlier. Right. The first type is constant pressure calorimetry. In a typical undergraduate lab, this is your classic coffee cup calorimeter. It's literally just a nested pair of insulated Styrofoam cups with a lid, a stirrer, and a thermometer poking through the top. And the defining characteristic is that it is open to the atmosphere. The pressure pressing down on the liquid in the cup is just standard atmospheric pressure, It doesn't change during the course of the experiment. Because the pressure is constant, the heat measured by the thermometer, Q, is mathematically equivalent to the change in enthalpy of the reaction. The second type is constant volume calorimetry. This is the bomb calorimeter we mentioned earlier. It is a heavy duty sealed steel container. The bomb submerged in a precisely measured tub of water, all wrapped in thermal insulation. You ignite a reaction inside the steel bomb and measure the temperature change of the surrounding water. Because the heavy steel walls cannot flex or move, the volume of the reaction chamber is absolutely constant. It is an isovolumetric process. And if we remember from section 7.1 in an isovolumetric process, the physical work done, W is exactly zero. Because W is zero, delta U equals Q. Therefore, all the heat measured by a bomb calorimeter is exactly equal to the change in internal energy. So a coffee cup measures enthalpy, a steel bomb measures internal energy. Which provides a seamless transition into section 7.4 enthalpy. We just defined it operationally by how we measure it in the coffee cup. Enthalpy, represented by the capital letter H, is the heat transferred into or out of a system at a constant atmospheric pressure. Right, but operationally measuring it is different from conceptually understanding it. What is enthalpy actually representing inside the molecules? Conceptually, you should think of enthalpy as a measure of the potential energy stored within a system. That potential energy is found in the microscopic intermolecular attractions pulling molecules together, and in the incredibly strong chemical bonds holding the individual atoms together. Oh, okay. So when those bonds break and form during a chemical reaction, that potential energy is either released into the world as heat, or heat is absorbed from the world and locked away as potential energy. Exactly. That flow of heat gives us the signs for Delta H, the change in enthalpy. This is foundational vocabulary for anyone studying chemistry. Let's define the two directions. If the calculated delta H for a reaction is a positive number, it means that heat is being absorbed into the system from the surrounding environment. The chemical products end up with a higher total enthalpy more stored potential energy than the reactants started with. This is defined as an endothermic process. The prefix endo means inside. Heat is pulled inside. If you are physically holding a glass beaker in your hand where an endothermic reaction is taking place, the glass will feel noticeably cold against your skin. Right, the reaction is literally stealing thermal kinetic energy from the skin of your hand to fuel the creation of new higher energy chemical bonds. Conversely, if the Delta H is a negative number, it indicates that heat is being released by the system and pushed out into the surroundings. The chemical products end up in a lower, more stable energy state than the reactants. The excess energy that is no longer needed is expelled. This is an exothermic process. The prefix exo means outside. Heat is thrown outside. If you hold that beaker, it will feel hot, perhaps even dangerously hot, as the reaction blasts thermal energy into your hand. So how do we actually calculate delta H for a specific complex chemical reaction without having to run the experiment in a calorimeter every single time. The sources give us three primary mathematical methods. The first method uses standard heats of formation denoted as delta H naught F. The standard heat of formation is a defined lookup table value. It is the precise amount of energy required to synthesize exactly one mole of a specific compound directly from its raw constituent elements, assuming those elements are starting in their natural standard states. For example, forming one mole of liquid water by reacting raw hydrogen gas with raw oxygen gas. And there is a critical rule here that we touched on earlier: what is the heat of formation for an element that is already in its standard state, like O2 solid carbon graphite? By definition, the standard heat of formation for an element in its standard state is exactly zero, because it takes zero Joules of energy to form an element into the exact state it already naturally exists in. It's a thermodynamic starting line. To find the standard heat of an entire multi molecule chemical reaction, the formula is incredibly straightforward. You take the total sum of heats of formation of all the products and you subtract the total sum of the heats of formation of all the reactants. Products minus reactants. It's basic accounting. The second method for calculating enthalpy is Hess's law. This is often tested because it requires algebraic manipulation. I always loved Hess' law problems. They feel less like chemistry and more like solving a logic puzzle or a sudoku. They do. The underlying logic of Hess' law relies entirely on the fact that enthalpy is a state function. As we establish with our mountain climbing analogy, state functions are perfectly path independent. Because it doesn't matter how you get from the reactants to the products, we can calculate the enthalpy of a massive complex chemical reaction by breaking it down into a series of smaller, simpler known steps. Let's construct a mental puzzle. Let's say you want to know the delta H for a reaction where chemical A turns into chemical C. A goes to C. You don't know the answer. But you look in a reference textbook and you find the delta H value for A turning into a middleman chemical, B. And you find a second reaction showing B turning into C. The solution is simple addition. You take the delta H at the first step, A to B, and add it to the delta H at the second step, B to C. When you combine the equations algebraically, the B on the product side of the first reaction cancels out the B on the reactant side of the second reaction. You are left with the net equation A goes to C. And because enthalpy is a state function, the sum of the enthalpy changes of the parts equals the exact enthalpy change of the whole. But the puzzle aspect comes in because the textbook reference reactions almost never match what you need exactly. What if the textbook gives you the reaction for C turning into B? It's the right chemicals, but the arrow is pointing backward. The rules for manipulation are strictly logical. If you need to reverse a chemical equation to make the pieces of your puzzle fit, you simply take the delta H value and flip the mathematical sign. If the forward reaction C to B is exothermic and releases 50 kilojoules, a delta H of negative 50, then logic dictates that the reverse reaction, forcing B to C, must be endothermic and absorb exactly 50 kilojoules, a delta H of positive 50. And what if the coefficients are wrong? What if my puzzle needs two moles of B, but the textbook equation only shows one mole of B? Enthalpy is an extensive property, meaning it scales with the amount of material. If you multiply all the stoichiometric coefficients in a chemical equation by a factor of two, you must also multiply the delta H value by exactly two. Twice the chemicals means twice the heat. So the Hess's Law strategy is: Manipulate the puzzle piece equations by flipping them and multiplying them until they perfectly stack up to match your target equation. Flip the reactions, flip the signs. Multiply the reactions, multiply the values, then add all the new Delta H values together. It's a very satisfying process once you get the hang of it. The third and final method for calculating enthalpy requires a conceptual shift that frequently catch students off guard. It involves using bond dissociation energy. This is a massive trap and we need to spend time dismantling it. I want to talk about the analogy of a wooden stick versus a pair of magnets. Okay, let's hear it. In introductory biology classes, we are constantly taught about high energy bonds in molecules like ATP. We are told that breaking the terminal phosphate bond in ATP releases energy to power the cell. Yes. The language used creates a terrible, deeply ingrained misconception that chemical bonds are like little taut rubber bands or tiny pinatas filled with energy. And when you snap them, the energy spills out. That mental model is entirely 100% backwards from the perspective of physical chemistry. It violates the fundamental nature of chemical attractions. Think about your physical analogy of a strong wooden stick. If you hold that stick in your hands and you want to snap it in half, do you spontaneously receive energy from the stick or do you have to expend your own energy to break it? I have to exert physical force, I have to burn calories, it takes effort and energy to break the stick? Precisely. Breaking a chemical bond is universally an endothermic process. The atoms in a bond are resting in a stable, low energy state attracted to each other. You have to physically input external energy to rip those atoms apart against their mutual attractive forces. So bond breaking always requires an input of heat. It is always a positive delta H. Now let's picture the opposite scenario. Imagine you were holding two incredibly powerful rare earth neodymium magnets, one in each hand. If you bring them within an inch of each other, they will rip violently out of your grip and slam together with a loud, sharp clack. That loud noise is acoustic energy radiating outward. The collision might even generate a microscopic spark, releasing light and thermal energy. Forming a chemical bond is universally an exothermic process. When two separated high energy atoms come together and establish a stable molecular bond, they drop down into a lower, more comfortable potential energy well. So the excess energy they held when they were separated has to go somewhere so it is violently released into the surrounding environment as heat or light. Bond formation always releases heat. It is always a negative delta H. This is where the biology terminology causes so much confusion. When a biology professor says breaking the phosphate bond in ATP releases energy, what they're actually describing is the net result of a multi step process. Yes, breaking that initial ATP bond absolutely costs energy. It is an endothermic step. But immediately after it breaks, the exposed pieces rapidly form new, incredibly stable bonds with water molecules during hydrolysis. That subsequent bond formation releases a massive, overwhelming amount of energy. The energy released by the new bonds is far greater than the energy it costs to break the old bond. The net mathematical result is a massive release of energy, but the physical act of breaking the bond itself was an energy cost. That is the exact chemical reality. And that reality dictates the mathematical formula used for bond dissociation energies. To calculate the total delta H of a reaction using bond energies, the equation is: delta H reaction equals the sum of the bonds broken minus the sum of the bonds formed. Notice that this formula is the exact opposite of the standard rule we just learned. For heats of formation, the rule was products minus reactants. But because breaking bonds is a positive energy cost, and forming bonds is a negative energy release, the bond energy formula is bonds broken bonds formed. Which is essentially reactants minus products. That flip in the formula is an incredibly easy trap to fall into on test day. It is a trap that tests your conceptual understanding over your rote memorization. You must let the physical reality of the stick magnets guide your negative signs. We have spent a massive amount of time on enthalpy, dissecting the flow of heat and potential energy. But heat naturally wants to move. It naturally wants to spread out into the cold. Which brings us to the second great driving force of the universe and Section 7.5: Entropy. If enthalpy is the most calculated concept, entropy is perhaps the most fundamentally misunderstood concept in all of science. I know exactly what analogy you're going to target here. The messy room analogy. Literally every high school science teacher uses it. Your bedroom naturally gets messy over time. Your clothes end up on the floor. It takes active energy to fold them and put them away. Therefore, entropy is a measure of disorder and chaos. I am pushing back hard on this. Why is the messy bedroom analogy a trap for college level chemistry? Because a messy room is a subjective, macroscopic human judgment. It is not a quantifiable thermodynamic state. A pile of dirty laundry on the floor does not possess fundamentally more thermodynamic entropy than that exact same laundry folded neatly in a drawer. The disorder analogy is simplification that completely falls apart when you try to apply it mathematically to chemical systems. So if it's not chaos or messiness, what is the mathematically correct way to think about entropy? We must approach it through the lens of statistical thermodynamics. Entropy is the mathematical measure of energy dispersal. It is a statistical measurement of how widely and evenly a given amount of energy can be distributed throughout a system at a specific temperature. Let's unpack that dispersal. How exactly does energy spread out? It spreads out by occupying different microstates. A microstate is a specific snapshot in time microscopic arrangement of atoms and energy packets. To visualize this, imagine rolling a pair of six sided dice. Okay, rolling dice. If you want to roll a total of two, there is only one specific microstate available to you. You must roll a one on the first die and a one on the second die. That's it. It is a highly restricted state. But if you want to roll a seven, there are six entirely different microstates, different combinations of the two dice like three and four or five and two that all add up to that same macroscopic state of seven. So in statistical terms, rolling a seven has vastly higher entropy than rolling a two, simply because there are more possible ways for the system to arrange itself to achieve that state. Exactly. The brilliant physicist Ludwig Boltzmann defined entropy mathematically with a famously elegant equation: S equals Kb times the natural log of W. In that equation, Kb is the Boltzmann constant and W represents the total number of available microstates. As the number of available microstates, W, increases, the natural logarithm increases and the total entropy, S, increases. The fundamental truth of the universe is that energy naturally, statistically, wants to occupy as many microstates as possible. It wants to spread out. And this statistical probability is the foundation of the second law of thermodynamics. The second law dictates the direction of time itself. It states that for any spontaneous process to occur, the total entropy of the universe must strictly increase. The mathematical statement is Delta S universe equals Delta S system plus Delta S surroundings is greater than zero. Energy will always spontaneously disperse from being highly localized to becoming widely spread out unless a physical barrier actively hinders it from doing so. This statistical disbuzzle is exactly why a hot cup of coffee cools down in a cold room. The thermal energy is highly concentrated, trapped in the few available microstates of the hot liquid. The surrounding universe, the air in the room, has vastly, exponentially more microstates available. So simply due to the statistics of probability, the thermal energy naturally disperses from the few microstates in the coffee outward into the billions of empty microstates in the room until the energy distribution is completely uniform. And crucially, the statistics dictate that it never goes in reverse. You will never ever see a room temperature cup of coffee sitting on a table suddenly spontaneously absorb ambient heat from the room and start violently boiling, while the rest of the room simultaneously gets colder. That would require a massive, localized decrease in entropy, a spontaneous concentration of energy into fewer microstates, which statistically violates the second law. On an exam like the MCAT, we don't usually have to calculate the complex statistical microstate math of Boltzmann's equation. Instead, we need to be able to look at chemical reaction and intuitively predict the sign of the entropy change, delta S. The sources give us clear rules of thumb for this. First, we look at the phases of matter. Predicting entropy based on phase is very straightforward. Consider a solid block of ice. It is a rigid crystal lattice. The molecules are locked tightly in place, barely vibrating. Because they cannot move freely, they have very few microstates available to distribute their energy. Therefore, solids, by definition, have the lowest possible entropy. If we melt that solid into a liquid, the rigid lattice breaks apart. The molecules can now tumble, flow, and slide past one another. They have significantly more freedom of movement, which means more microstates. Entropy increases as you move from solid to liquid. And if you boil that liquid into a gas, the molecules break free completely. They fly wildly throughout the entire volume of their container. The volume a gas occupies is massively larger than a liquid, creating an exponentially vastly larger number of available microstates. Gases possess the highest entropy by a massive margin. So any phase change from a solid to a liquid or from a liquid to a gas represents a massive increase in entropy, which means a positive delta S. Sublimation is a great example. If you watch a block of dry ice turn directly from a solid chunk into a rapidly expanding cloud of carbon dioxide gas, you are watching an explosive, localized increase in entropy. Conversely, freezing liquid water into solid ice restricts the molecules, resulting in a decrease in entropy, a negative delta S. We can also reliably predict entropy changes in balanced chemical reactions by simply counting the moles of gas on each side of the equation. The sources provide a classic industrial example. N2 gas plus three H2 gas yields two NH3 gas, nitrogen gas reacting with hydrogen gas to synthesize ammonia gas. Let's do the accounting. Look at the reactant site on the left. We have one mole of nitrogen gas and three moles of hydrogen gas. That is a total of four independent moles of gas bouncing around. Now look at the product side on the right. We have synthesized them into exactly two moles of ammonia gas. We started with four moles of gas and we ended up with two moles of gas. Think about the microstates. The physical matter and thermal energy that was previously spread vastly across four independent freely flying molecules has now been chemically fused and confined into just two molecules. The number of possible the available microstates has been drastically reduced. Therefore, without doing any complex math, we know with absolute certainty that the entropy of the system decreased. Delta S is a negative number. The rule is simple: count the gas molecules. If there are fewer gas molecules on the product side than the reactant side, entropy went down. Another quick predictive rule: if a solid crystal dissolves into an aqueous solution like dumping table salt into a glass of water, the rigid crystal structure breaks apart into freely moving, independent sodium and chloride ions. Freedom of movement increases, microstates increase, so entropy goes up. Positive Delta S These predictive rules of thumb are absolutely essential because we need to know the positive or negative sign of Delta S to plug into our final and by far most important state function. But neither of those alone can tell us the ultimate fate of a reaction. To figure out if a chemical reaction will actually spontaneously happen on its own, we have to combine them. The physicist Josiah Willard Gibbs formulated the ultimate state function to answer this exact question. He synthesized the first and second laws into a single master equation: Delta G = Delta H T Delta S. Delta G is the change in Gibbs free energy. Before we do the math, what does the term free energy actually mean a physical sense? Physically, it measures the maximum theoretical amount of energy released by a process that is free or available to perform useful work on the surroundings. However, for the purposes of introductory chemistry and the MCAT, its primary function is serving as a predictive binary toggle for spontaneity. Let's lay out the golden rule of that toggle. The rule is absolute. If the calculated delta G is a negative number, the reaction is spontaneous. It will proceed in the forward direction entirely on its own without any continuous input of outside energy. If Delta G is a positive number, the reaction is strictly non spontaneous. It is a thermodynamic dead end. It will not happen unless you you constantly force it to happen by pumping in massive amounts of external energy. And if delta G is exactly zero, the system is perfectly balanced in a state of dynamic equilibrium. So if we want a reaction to happen, we are desperately looking for a negative delta G. Looking back at the master equation, delta G = delta H T delta S, we can build a logical matrix to see exactly how the positive or negative signs of delta H and delta S interact to give us that desired negative result. Result. Professor, let's walk through this matrix scenario by scenario. Let's begin with the best case scenario, the holy grail of spontaneous chemical reactions. Imagine a reaction that is highly exothermic, meaning it is releasing massive amounts of heat. Delta H is negative. Furthermore, this same reaction is drastically increasing entropy, spreading energy out into many microstates. Delta S is positive. Let me mentally plug those signs into the equation. Delta G equals a negative number, delta H minus temperature times a positive number, Delta S. Mathematically, a negative number minus a positive number will always universally result in an even larger negative number. That is the mathematical certainty. If Delta H is negative and Delta S is positive, Delta G will always be negative regardless of what the temperature is. The reaction is unconditionally spontaneous at all temperatures. A roaring campfire burning a solid log of wood into chaotic gases and ash is a fantastic example. It violently releases heat, and it turns a highly ordered solid into highly disordered gas. It is wildly unstoppably spontaneous. Okay, now let's look at the absolute worst case scenario. The reaction is endothermic, actively absorbing heat, so delta H is positive, and simultaneously it is decreasing entropy, taking chaotic molecules and locking them into a rigid structure, so delta S is negative. Let's plug those signs in. Delta G equals a positive number, delta H minus temperature times a negative number, delta S. Subtracting a negative is the same as adding a positive. The equation becomes a positive number plus a positive number. Therefore, delta G will always be positive. This reaction will never ever be spontaneous at any temperature. You will never in the entire lifespan of the universe see a cloud of smoke and a pile of ash spontaneously absorb heat from the air and reassemble themselves into a solid log of wood. Those two scenarios are the easy absolutes. But what happens when the enthalpy and the entropy are fighting against each other? One wants the reaction to happen, the other doesn't. This is where the temperature variable T acts as the tiebreaker. What if a reaction is endothermic but it increases entropy Let's look at the math. The equation is now a positive Delta H minus a positive T Delta S term. The final outcome for delta G depends entirely on the sheer mathematical size of that T delta S term. To get our desired negative delta G, we need the subtracted T delta S term to be much larger than the positive delta H term. Because the value of delta S is fixed by the specific chemistry of the reaction, the only way to make the total T delta S term larger is to radically increase the temperature T. So if delta H is positive and delta S is positive, the reaction is spontaneous only at AGH temperatures. The extremely high temperature provides the raw thermal energy necessary to overcome the stubborn endothermic barrier, allowing the highly favorable increase in entropy to take the wheel and drive the reaction forward. Melting a block of ice is the perfect physical example. It is endothermic, it requires heat to melt, but it increases entropy, turning a rigid solid into a flowing liquid. It only happens spontaneously when the temperature is high enough, specifically when the temperature rises above zero degrees Celsius. The final scenario in our matrix is an exothermic reaction, negative delta H, that unfortunately decreases entropy, negative delta S. A negative minus a negative, which mathematically becomes a negative number plus a positive number. To keep the final delta G negative in this scenario, we need that positive T delta S term to be as small and insignificant as possible. So we need the temperature multiplier to be a very low number. Correct. If Delta H is negative and Delta S is negative, the reaction is spontaneous only at low temperatures. Freezing liquid water into solid ice is the classic example here. It is exothermic. It actually releases heat as the strong hydrogen bonds form the crystal lattice, but it severely decreases entropy, locking the flowing liquid into a rigid solid. It only happens spontaneously when the temperature drops low enough, specifically below zero degrees Celsius. The exam will absolutely test your mastery of that matrix. You can try to memorize the four outcomes, but honestly, the far more reliable strategy on test day is to simply write down the equation delta G = delta H T delta S on your scratch paper, plug in the plus and minus signs given in the question, and logically deduce the mathematical outcome. Relying on the underlying logic is always safer than relying on rote memorization under pressure. Now there is one final incredibly critical distinction we must make in this section. It is a conceptual trap we alluded to at the very beginning of the episode. The fundamental clash between thermodynamics and kinetics. I am going to push back here with a very common, very intuitive student question. Let's say I do all the math. I plug in the delta H and the delta S and I calculate the delta G for a newly discovered chemical reaction. The math tells me that delta G is massively, incredibly negative, like negative 500 kilojoules per mole. Does that massive negative number mean the reaction happens instantly? Do I need to be wearing safety goggles because it's going to violently explode the second I mix the chemicals? No, absolutely not. And assuming that it will is a fundamental dangerous trap. Thermodynamics, which encompasses Delta G, Delta H and Delta S, tells you only about the final destination. It tells you if a reaction is energetically favorable, if the universe will allow it to eventually happen. It tells you absolutely nothing whatsoever about the speed or the time frame of the trip. The speed of the reaction is the entirely separate domain of kinetics. A chemical reaction can have a massively overwhelmingly negative delta G, but its kinetics can be so painfully slow that the reaction is imperceptible on a human timescale. We bring it back to the diamond turning into graphite. The conversion of crystalline diamond into graphite has a decidedly negative delta G. It is thermodynamically spontaneous. Diamonds are not forever, they were actively currently turning into pencil lead. But the kinetic rate of that reaction is so agonizingly slow that it takes billions of years to occur. If delta G doesn't dictate the speed, what physical mechanism does? The speed is dictated by the activation energy, abbreviated as Ea. To visualize this, picture a reaction coordinate You have the reactants resting at a specific energy level on the left, and the products resting in a lower energy valley on the right. The vertical difference between the starting plateau and the final valley is your thermodynamic delta G. But separating the reactants and the products is a massive energy mountain, a spike called the transition state. The total height of that mountain, measured from the starting line of the reactants up to the very peak, the activation energy. Even if the final valley of the products is incredibly deep and stable, the molecules still have to physically climb all the way over that mountain to get there. Yes. The reactant molecules must physically collide with each other with enough raw kinetic energy and in the exact correct geometric orientation to push themselves over that Ea barrier. The taller the mountain, the fewer molecules will have the energy to climb it and the slower the overall reaction rate will be. And this mountain analogy perfectly explains exactly how catalysts work. A catalyst, such as a metallic surface in an engine or a highly complex enzyme in biological systems, functions by artificially lowering the activation energy. It physically grabs the molecules and provides an entirely different, easier chemical pathway. It's like blasting a tunnel through the mountain or finding a much lower mountain pass. By lowering the barrier, an exponentially larger number of molecules can cross it, speeding up the reaction rate dramatically. But, and this is perhaps one of the most frequently tested concepts on the entire MCAT, does a catalyst change the delta G of the reaction? Never. A catalyst lowers the peak of the mountain, but it does absolutely nothing to change the starting elevation of the reactants, nor does it alter the final depth of the product valley. Because the start and end points remain identical, the delta G remains identical. A catalyst does not alter delta H and it does not alter delta S. It affects the kinetics of the trip, but it has no power over the thermodynamics of the destination. This profound clash between the speed of kinetics and the stability of thermodynamics leads us to our final concept: Reaction control. Sometimes a specific set of chemical reactants can actually form two completely different products depending entirely on the temperature of the beaker. We call this the battle between the kinetic product and the thermodynamic product. Let's visualize one final reaction coordinate diagram to explain this battle. From the reactant starting line, the pathway splits into two separate trails. Path A goes over a relatively small, easy hill, but it drops into a very shallow, high energy valley. Path B goes over a massive, intimidating, tall mountain, but it drops into a much deeper, vastly more stable energy well. Path A is the pathway that forms the kinetic product. It has a much lower activation energy, that small hill. Because the hill is so low, this pathway happens incredibly fast. Furthermore, because the energy requirement is so low, this pathway can easily occur at very cold temperatures. However, the trade off is the destination. The final product resting in that shallow valley is higher in overall energy, meaning it is significantly less chemically stable. It is the quick, easy, and dirty product. Path B, on the other hand, is the pathway that forms the thermodynamic product. It has a much higher activation energy, the massive mountain. Because the barrier is so high, this pathway is incredibly slow to occur. Most importantly, because the mountain is so tall, this pathway requires intensely high temperatures. You have to actively pump massive amounts of thermal heat into the beaker just to give the molecules enough kinetic energy to survive the climb. But if you provide that heat, once the molecules finally get over the peak, they plummet into that incredibly deep energy well. The final product is much lower in energy, meaning it is the absolute most stable product physically possible. So if you run this reaction in an ice bath at very low temperatures, the system simply doesn't possess the thermal energy required to scale the massive mountain of path B. It is physically trapped taking the easy way out, path A. We say the reaction is under kinetic control, and the beaker will rapidly fill up with the less stable kinetic product. But, if you take that same beaker and place it over a roaring Bunsen burner at extremely high temperatures, the system is now flush with thermal energy. The molecules can easily climb both energy barriers. Given enough time, when the molecules inevitably crest the taller mountain of path B, they drop into that deep, highly stable valley and they get trapped there. They don't have the energy to climb back out. Under these hot conditions, we say the reaction is under thermodynamic control. And the highly stable thermodynamic product will eventually dominate the beaker. Fast and unstable in the cold versus slow and highly stable in the heat. That is a brilliant visual way to conceptualize a very complex topic. Looking back, we have covered an immense, rigorous amount of scientific ground today. We defined the arbitrary boundaries of our systems, we mapped the paths of pressure and volume on our graphs, we calculated the flow of heat and the potential energy of enthalpy. We mathematically quantified the inevitable dispersal of entropy, and ultimately we brought it all together with Gibbs free energy to predict the grand thermodynamic destiny of chemical reactions. We have meticulously examined the engine of thermodynamics from first principles. But before we completely sign off and let our listeners get back to their study questions, Professor, I want to leave them with something a bit more philosophical. Something to mull over that builds directly on the strict mathematical rules we just discussed, but scales them up to the ultimate extreme. We talked extensively about the second law of thermodynamics and how the entropy of the universe must constantly, inevitably increase. Yes, Delta S universe is greater than zero. Energy must continuously disperse into available microstates. It is the fundamental law of probability. Let's connect that chemical law to modern astrophysics. The universe, in its entirety, is the ultimate isolated system. There are no surroundings for it to exchange energy with. But the universe is not a static, fixed volume container. The physical fabric of the universe is continuously actively expanding outward. That is a profoundly fascinating application of thermodynamics. Consider what physical expansion means for Boltzmann's equation: S kb natural log W entropy is intimately tied to the number of available microstates. As the physical three-dimensional volume of the universe continuously expands, the sheer amount of empty space available for energy and matter to occupy is actively, perpetually growing. Exactly. Because the ultimate container is getting larger, the total entropy of the universe isn't just increasing due to hot stars cooling down, the very capacity for entropy is expanding. This expanding capacity creates a mathematically irreversible arrow of time. The finite energy of the universe is pointing inexorably toward a distant final future where every single joule of heat is perfectly uniformly diffused across an unimaginably massive, ever expanding void a final state of maximum possible entropy. It's known as the thermodynamic heat death of the universe. And it is all driven by the simple, inescapable math of S kb natural log W. Is definitely something heavy to mull over while you're reviewing your flashcards in the coffee shop today. Thank you for studying with us today. Good place to start. It kind of strips away all the math and just defines the physical state. So what fundamentally makes a gas a gas as opposed to a solid or a liquid? Well, really comes down to two interlinked factors: intermolecular distance and intermolecular interaction. Okay, let's break this down. Right, so if we look at a solid, the molecules are locked into this rigid lattice structure. They are practically touching and they have these strong attractive forces holding them in place. Like a brick. Exactly. Now in a liquid, they have a bit more kinetic energy so they can sort of slide past one another. Right. They flow. Yeah. But they're still very close together. They're kept in check by those attractive forces. But in the gas phase, have to picture, well, it's a massive amount of empty space. Just void. Yeah, a vast void. The particles are moving incredibly rapidly and the distance between them is huge compared to their actual physical size. So if I'm picturing a room full of gas, it's mostly just like empty space. A vast, vast void. And because they are so far apart, the intermolecular forces, meaning the physical attraction or repulsion between individual molecules, they're incredibly weak. So they basically ignore each other? A lot of calculations, we actually treat them as if they're completely negligible. They're essentially moving too fast and they're too far apart to care about each other. Okay, that microscopic picture makes a lot of sense. But how does that translate to the macroscopic properties we actually see, you know, in the lab? Good question. Because of that vast empty space and the lack of interaction, gases exhibit three really unique macroscopic properties. Oh, okay, what's the first one? First, they expand to fill whatever volume you put them in. A solid rock has a defined shape and volume. A liquid has defined volume even if it takes the shape of its container. Right. But a gas has neither. If you release one mole of gas into a small box, it fills the box. Oh, that makes sense. And if you open that box inside, say, an empty warehouse, the gas will rapidly expand until it's evenly distributed throughout the entire warehouse. It just wants to spread out as much as physically possible. Yes, exactly. Second, because of all that empty space, gases are easily compressible. Because there's room to squeeze them. Right, you cannot easily compress a block of ice or a beaker of water, because the molecules are already touching. Yeah. But with a gas, you can physically force those distant particles closer together by applying external pressure. Okay, and the third property. Third, despite all this, gases are actually classified alongside liquids as fluids. Wait, really? A fluid? Yeah. They flow, they yield to external forces, and they conform to the shape of their containers, just like water. All right, so to measure and describe this flowing compressible void, the Kaplan text introduces four foundational variables. Yes, the big four. This is defined as the average atmospheric pressure at sea level on earth. It's literally the weight of the sky pushing down on you. Heavy stuff. Literally. Mathematically, one at more is equal to exactly seven sixty millimeters of mercury, which is abbreviated as MMHG. Okay, seven sixty. Furthermore, seven sixty millimeter HG is exactly equal to seven sixty torr. Oh, so they're the same. Yeah, MMHG and a torr are completely interchangeable. It's a perfect one to one ratio. Now this sounds incredibly archaic. Why in the world are modern medical professionals measuring columns of liquid metal? I know. It does sound like something out of a medieval alchemy lab. But there's a really profound historical reason, and it carries directly into modern medicine. I'm intrigued. Historically, atmospheric pressure was measured using a device called a mercury barometer. It was invented by a guy named Evangelista Torricelli way back in the sixteen hundred's. How does that actually work? Like physically? Hey, imagine taking a long glass tube, right? It's closed at one end and you completely fill it with liquid mercury. Then you invert that tube and place the open end into a shallow dish that is also full of liquid mercury. The weight of the mercury in the tube naturally wants to pull it down, emptying into the dish, gravity's pulling on it. Sure. But at the exact same time, the invisible weight of the earth's atmosphere is pushing down on the surface of the mercury in that open dish. Ah, so it's like a pushing contest. Gravity pulling the mercury down the tube versus the atmosphere pushing the mercury up into the tube from the dish. Exactly. It's a tug of war. And at sea level, the force the atmosphere pushing down on the dish is strong enough to support a column of mercury inside that tube at a height of exactly seven sixty millimeters. That's where the number comes from. Yes. And if a storm rolls in and atmospheric pressure drops, the pressure on the dish lessens and the column of mercury drops to say seven fifty millimeters. So it is quite literally a physical measurement of distance. A ruler measuring how high the atmosphere can push a heavy liquid. Exactly. But how does this connect to me sitting in a doctor's office today getting a physical?
