You need a solid grasp of thermodynamic theory to understand the fundamental why behind the interactions alongside the math to calculate the precise how much. Okay, let's get right down to the molecular level then. Following the Kaplan Progression, Section 9.1, Nature of Solutions. Right, the basic definitions. Yeah, because before we can measure anything, we have to define what we're even looking at. So what separates a true solution from just, I don't know, a random pile of mixed up stuff? Well, a solution is defined very specifically as a homogenous mixture of two or more substances that combine to form a single phase. And on the MCAT, that phase is basically almost always a liquid, right? Usually, yes. And a solution fundamentally consists of two parts. First, you have the solvent. That's the majority component. The stuff doing the dissolving. Right. It's the substance that typically does not change its phase when you mix them. And second, you have the solute, which is the minority component getting dissolved into the solvent. Okay, I want to push back on that single phase definition for just a second. Sure, go for it. Think about the air we breathe. I mean, it's a mixture of nitrogen, oxygen, argon, CO2. It's totally homogenous. And it's all in a single gas phase. So is gas mixed in gas considered a solution? That's a common trap actually. Gases dissolved in gases are technically just classified as mixtures, not true solutions. Wait, really? Why draw that line? If they're mixed perfectly evenly shouldn't they count? We have to look at the intermolecular interactions, or you know, the lack of them. Oh right, the kinetic molecular theory of gases. Exactly. That theory states that gas molecules are spread so incredibly far apart and they're moving so fast that they just don't engage in meaningful chemical interactions with one another. You're just sort of flying past each other. Right. But in a true solution, the solvent and solute molecules are intimately interacting. They're forming intermolecular bonds like dipole dipole interactions or hydrogen bonds. Okay, so because gases don't form those structural relationships, we'd just call them mixtures. Yep. So all solutions are mixtures, but not all mixtures are solutions. That distinction makes a lot of sense. The solvent and solute have to actually talk to each other chemically for it to be a solution. Exactly. So let's trace the mechanism of that conversation. If I, say, drop a solid crystal of table salt into a beaker of water, what are the actual mechanical steps of it dissolving? Well, the overarching process is called solvation or dissolution. And when the solvent is water, which is like the standard biological scenario, we call it hydration, right? Right, hydration. And the key insight here, which UWorld loves to test, is that solvation is not just a single instantaneous pop. It's a three step mechanical process. Okay, let me try to reason through those steps. If I have a solid salt crystal, all those sodium and chloride ions are locked really tightly together in an ionic lattice. A very stable lattice, yeah. So step one has to be breaking those salute particles apart from each other. And I mean, breaking bonds always requires an input of energy, right? That is step one. Overcoming the salute salute intermolecular forces, it takes energy. Okay, what's step two? Step two shifts the focus over to the solvent. The water molecules are currently happily hydrogen bonded to one another. Oh, so they have to make room. To make physical room for the incoming sodium and chloride ions, the water molecules have to break some of their own intermolecular forces. Which means step two also requires an energy input. Correct. You're breaking bonds again. Okay, so we've spent energy ripping the salt apart and we've spent energy pulling the water apart. Step three must be the payoff where we get something back. It is. Step three is the formation of new intermolecular interactions interactions between the solute and the solvent. So the water grabbing the ions? Yes. The partial negative oxygen atoms of the water molecules align themselves to surround the positive sodium ions. And the partial positive hydrogen atoms of the water surround the negative chloride ions. And forming these new bonds releases energy. Exactly. This brings us straight into the thermodynamics of the whole thing. Yeah, because we have a battle of energy going on here. Why do some things dissolve effort when you stir them into a glass while others just sit at the bottom as a stubborn clump? The entire process is governed by Gibbs free energy. Oh man, Delta G. Yeah, the formula to keep front of mind is Delta G equals Delta H minus T times delta S. Right, so the change in free energy is equal to the change in enthalpy minus the absolute temperature multiplied by the change in entropy. Let's look at the enthalpy piece first. The delta H. We just established that steps one and two require energy while step three releases it. Well, for most things we dissolve. The energy we spend breaking the initial bonds is actually greater than the energy we get back when the new bonds form. Wait, really? So the overall process for most solutes is actually endothermic? Yes. The delta H is positive. It pulls heat from the surrounding environment. Which is exactly how an instant cold pack works. You snap the inner pouch, ammonium nitrate mixes with water, and because the dissolution requires so much thermal energy, it sucks the heat right out of your skin. The cold pack is a perfect, tangible example. But, you know, this creates a thermodynamic paradox. How so? Well, if the process is endothermic, if it fundamentally requires an ongoing input of heat, why does it happen spontaneously at room temperature? Because for a reaction to be spontaneous, the delta G must be negative. Exactly. How do we get a negative Delta G from a positive Delta H? The secret has to be the second half of the equation, right? The temperature times the change in entropy, the T Delta S. The entropy is the driving force here. Entropy is a measure of the dispersal of energy. We often think of it as disorder. So when you take a highly organized, rigid, solid crystal lattice and shatter it. And scatter its constituent ions randomly throughout a vast volume of liquid, yeah, you create a massive increase in entropy. That huge positive delta S multiplied by the temperature in Kelvin creates a negative term that completely overwhelms the positive delta H. Exactly. The math dictates that delta G becomes negative, which drives the spontaneous dissolution. I love how this thermodynamic battle plays out in biology, especially when we look at like biochemistry and protein folding. Oh that's a great interdisciplinary connection. Yeah because a protein is essentially just a giant salute trying to exist in the watery environment of a cell, right? But it's made of amino acids and some of those amino acids, like phenylalanine or valanine, they have hydrophobic side chains. They actively repel water. Right. And when a hydrophobic residue is forced into contact with water, the water molecules cannot form hydrogen bonds with it. They just have nothing to grab onto. So what happens? The water just ignores it. Not quite. To stabilize the situation, the water molecules are forced to rearrange themselves into a highly structured, rigid cage around the hydrophobic area. Just to maximize their hydrogen bonds with each other? Exactly. This is known as a solvation layer. But wait, a highly structured, rigid cage means a massive increase in order. Right. And order means a decrease in entropy. That's bad. It is. A negative change in entropy is a severe thermodynamic penalty. It drives the delta G toward a positive non spontaneous value. So to avoid this penalty, the protein just spontaneously folds itself up into a three d shape that buries all those hydrophobic residues deep in its core. Shielded from the water, yes. And then the surrounding water molecules are completely free to tumble randomly, keeping entropy high and making the folded state thermodynamically favorable. That's wild. So protein folding isn't just about protein wanting a specific shape. It's about the surrounding water demanding chaos. It's a purely entropy driven process. It really is. It's fascinating. Okay, let's pivot from how things dissolve to the concept of solubility itself. Right, solubility is defined as the maximum amount of a specific solute that can dissolve in a specific solvent at a given temperature. And when a solution is holding the absolute maximum, we call it saturated. Exactly. Now the MCAT always tests solubility rules for aqueous solutions. And I remember my college textbook had this massive table with a dozen different rules and like a million exceptions. Do we really need to memorize the entire table for this test? Taking a strategic approach? No. The MCAT is a reasoning test, not a trivia exam. They will usually provide the obscure solubility data in the passage if you need it. Oh, thank goodness. However, there are a few absolute unbreakable rules that you are expected to have perfectly memorized. Okay, let's lay them out for everyone listening. Rule number one, all salts of group one metals, so that's the alkali metals like lithium, sodium, and potassium, Those are completely soluble in water, no exception. Perfect. Rule number two: all salts containing the ammonium ion (NH4 plus AD) are completely soluble. Okay, group one and ammonium. Rule number three: all nitrates (NO3) are completely soluble. And rule number four: all acetates (CH3COO) are completely soluble. Group one: ammonium, nitrates, acetates. I get that we have to memorize them, but why are they always soluble? What's the actual chemical reason behind it? It generally comes down to size and charge density. Take the nitrate ion for example. It's a relatively large polyatomic ion, right? Right. And its single negative charge is delocalized across three oxygen atoms through resonance. Resonance. So the charge is smeared out. Exactly. Because that negative charge is smeared over such a large area, its charge density is very low. It can't hold onto a positrification very tightly. Oh I see. So the water molecules can easily overcome those weak ionic bonds and pull the crystal apart? Yes. And the same exact logic applies to the large singly charged group one metals. That makes the memorization feel way less arbitrary. And you know, the MCAT loves using these completely soluble ions as spectator ions in reaction. They do, they show up all the time. Like if a passage gives you sodium formate reacting with something, the sodium is just a delivery vehicle. Because sodium salts are always soluble, the sodium instantly detaches, floats away, and just watches the reaction from the sidelines. Exactly. The smart test taker immediately crosses out the sodium and focuses all their attention on the formate ion. It's a critical time saving habit to develop. Awesome. Okay, before we move into the mathematics of solutions, we need to touch on complex ion formation, which is a really unique twist on solubility. It is. I always picture a complex ion as like a VIP entering a crowded club. A VIP, I like that. Yeah. Imagine you have a central metalcation, maybe transition metal like copper or silver. On its own, it's the VIP. It might not mix well with the general crowd, meaning it has low solubility. But then it surrounds itself with a dedicated entourage. In chemistry, that entourage consists of molecules or ions called ligands. Ligands act as electron pair donors. And they bond to the VIP? Yes. They bond to the central metal cation via coordinate covalent bonds. That's a specific type of bond where the ligand donates both of the electrons required to form the connection. Like it's doing all the work to hold hands. Exactly. Common ligands include water molecules themselves, ammonia or cyanide ions. So the entourage forms a protective shell around around the VIP. Because those ligands interact beautifully with the surrounding solvent, the entire complex, the VIP plus the entourage, suddenly becomes highly soluble. We take a metal that normally wouldn't dissolve and basically trick the water into accepting it. Forming a complex ion drastically increases the solubility of the central metal. Which is going to be incredibly relevant when we look at Le Chatelier's principle later, right? Like how to force stubborn compounds into solution. Exactly. We'll explore that heavily when we tackle equilibria. Speaking of tackling the next concept, section 9.2, concentration. We understand the physics of salvation now, but you know, a physician can't just order a fairly strong IV bag of saline. We need exact numbers. How do we quantitatively measure what's in our beaker? Right. Qualitative terms like concentrated or dilute are totally useless in a lab or a clinical setting. We must employ quantitative units of concentration. And the MCAT expects complete fluency in several different units, each serving a distinct purpose. Okay, let's go through them. Let's start with percent composition by mass. This unit is calculated by taking the mass of the solute, dividing it by the total mass of the solution, and multiplying by 100%. Sounds simple enough. It is, but the most common error students make is in the denominator. The denominator is the total mass of the solution, which means the mass of the solute plus the mass of the solvent. Ah, so if I dissolve 20 grams of sugar into 80 grams of water, my denominator isn't 80, it's 20 plus 80, which is 100. So the solution is 20% sugar by mass. Correct. Don't forget to add them together. Good tip. What's next? The next unit is parts per million, or PPM. This unit is reserved for trace amounts of solute. Like what kind of scenarios? You'll frequently see it in environmental chemistry passages discussing pollutants or maybe in toxicology reports. UWorld loves testing this. The actual formula is the mass of the solute divided by the mass of the solution, all multiplied by 10 to the sixth power. I mean, that feels like a nightmare to calculate without a calculator. Which is exactly why the MCAT relies on a specific logical shortcut. You shouldn't be doing that math. Oh really? What's the shortcut? Because PPM is used for incredibly dilute aqueous solutions, the density of the overall solution is essentially identical to the density of pure water. And one liter of pure water has a mass of exactly 1,000,000 mg. Oh, I see where this is going. Therefore, one part per million is effectively equal to one mg of solute per one liter of water. That is amazing. So if a passage states a town's drinking water contains four PPM of lead, I don't need to do any complex fraction map. We deliver? I instantly know there are exactly four milligrams of lead floating in every single liter of water. Exactly. That shortcut is absolute gold for timing. What about mole fraction? Mole fraction represented by the letter X is simply the number of moles of a specific substance divided by the total number of moles of all substances in the system. So if you have two moles of gas A and three moles of gas B, the mole fraction of A is two fifths or 0.4. Right, it's just a ratio. I know this comes into play heavily with gases and partial pressures, and we'll see it later when we look at how solutes block vaporization at the surface of a liquid. But let's look at the heavy hitters now, the units you see in almost every chemistry passage. Let's talk molarity versus molality. Yes, molarity, represented by a capital M, is the gold standard for most chemical equations. It's defined as the moles of solute per liter of solution. Again, noting the denominator there, it's the total volume of the solution, not the volume of the solvent you started with. Right. Molarity is the unit you plug into rate laws, the law of mass action, pH calculations, and osmotic pressure. And then we have molality, represented by a lowercase m. This is defined as the moles of solute per kilogram of solvent. Yes. Let's really pull these apart for the listener. Moles per liter of total liquid versus moles per kilogram of solvent. Since a liter of water weighs exactly a kilogram, aren't these essentially identical? Like why does chemistry demand two separate units? It's a great question. The distinction lies in how liquids respond to the physical environment. Specifically, thermal expansion. Thermal expansion. Okay. Molarity is a volume based measurement. What happens to the volume of water when you heat it in a flask? Well, the kinetic energy increases, the molecules push further apart and the liquid physically expands. The meniscus rises up the neck of the flask. Exactly. The volume increases. Now imagine you have sealed one molar solution of sodium chloride. You heat it from room temperature up to 90 degrees Celsius. Okay, then close to boiling. The liquid expands, increasing the denominator of your molarity fraction. But the number of moles of salt inside the flask hasn't changed at all. Therefore, just by heating the beaker, your molarity actually decreases. Wow. The concentration literally changes without adding or removing a single grain of salt or a single drop of water. That means molarity is totally unreliable if the temperature is fluctuating. Exactly. But mass is an absolute constant. A kilogram of water is a kilogram of water regardless of whether it's freezing solid, sitting at room temperature, or nearly boiling. So because molality is based on the mass of the solvent, its value is entirely independent of temperature. That's the key. That completely clarifies it. Whenever we are calculating a phenomena that involves drastic temperature changes, like boiling point elevation or freezing point depression, we are mathematically forced to use molality. Got it. And for everyday reactions on a lab bench at room temperature, we just use molarity. Right. Now we have one final concentration unit to define here. Normality represented by a capital N. I always think of normality as the molarity of the specific thing we actually care about. It cuts through the noise. That is a highly functional way to view it. Normality is the number of reactive equivalents per liter of solution. It's almost exclusively used in acid base chemistry or redox reactions. So if I have a one molar solution of hydrochloric acid, HCl, every molecule gives off one reactive proton. So a one molar solution is also a one normal solution. But if I have a one molar solution of sulfuric acid, H2SO4, every molecule can donate two reactive protons. I don't really care about the sulfate part, I only care about the protons. So that one molar solution of sulfuric acid is actually a two normal solution of protons. Exactly. You're just multiplying the molarity by the number of equivalents. Nice. Finally, this section, we need to address how to change concentrations in the lab, the dilution equation. Oh, the classic M1 V1 equals M2 V2. The initial concentration times the initial volume equals the final concentration times the final volume. Let's run a quick Mendel simulation for the listener to solidify this. Imagine you are a researcher. You have a highly concentrated stock solution of a drug at 100. Okay, M1 is 100. You need to run an assay and you need exactly 50 milliliters of the drug diluted down to ten millimolar. How much of your stock solution do you need to pipette up? Okay, I'll set up the algebra. M1 is my starting concentration, 100. V1 is my unknown, the amount I need to pull from the stock. M2 is my target concentration, 10. And V2 is my target final volume, 50 milliliters. So 100 times V1 equals 10 times And 10 times 50 is 500. Divide that by your initial concentration of 100 and your V1 equals five. So I need to pull exactly five milliliters of my stock solution. Right. You would then place that five milliliters into a flask and add pure solvent until the total volume reaches 50 milliliters. Perfect. So we figured out how to measure the exact amount of solute in our water. That moves us to section 9.3, solution equilibria. What happens when the water simply refuses to accept anymore? Right, the wall. Yeah, if you keep dumping salt into a beaker, eventually it stops dissolving and just piles up at the bottom. The system hits a wall. What's happening at that molecular wall? This introduces the concept of dynamic equilibrium. Up until now we have treated dissolution as a one way street. Salt goes into water, it vanishes into ions. But in reality it is a continuous two way street. Exactly. The instant you drop that salt chunk into the water, sodium and chloride ions start breaking off and dispersing. That's dissolution. But as the water gets more and more crowded with dissolved ions, they inevitably bump back into the solid chunk, right? Yes. And sometimes they reattach themselves to the crystal lattice. That's precipitation. So at first, the rate of dissolution is incredibly fast because the water is totally empty. The rate of precipitation is essentially zero. But as the concentration of dissolved ions steadily climbs, the rate of precipitation accelerates. And there comes a magical critical point where the rate of ions leaving the solid perfectly matches the rate of ions returning to the solid. Ions are jumping off and latching back on at the exact same speed. To the naked eye, the chunk of salt has completely stopped shrinking. The water looks totally still. But at the molecular level, it's absolute chaos. It is. That state of perfect balance is called the saturation point. The solution is holding the maximum possible amount of dissolved solute at that specific temperature. And because it is an equilibrium state, we can express it mathematically using an equilibrium constant. We call it the solubility product constant or KSP. Right. And every equilibrium constant is just a ratio of the products over the reactants. But there is a massive trap here for cannabis P. Oh yeah. The trap lies in the state of matter of the reactant. Pure solids and pure liquids are never included in an equilibrium expression because their bulk concentrations do not fundamentally change in a way that impacts mathematical ratio. And in a dissolution reaction, the reactant is always a solid crystal. Therefore, the denominator of the KSP expression is always omitted, or effectively treated as one. So the formula for KSP is the product of the dissolved ion concentrations raised to their stoichiometric coefficients. Let's trace the math on that. If you're dissolving silver chloride, AGCL, it yields one silver ion and one chloride ion. The KSP expression is simply the concentration of silver multiplied by the concentration of chloride. But what if I dissolve lead two chloride, PbCl2? That breaks down into one lead ion but two chloride ions. In that case, the Ksp expression is the concentration of lead multiplied by the concentration of chloride squared. You must raise the concentration of the ion to the power of its coefficient from the balanced chemical equation. Okay, got it. And like all equilibrium constants, Ksp is strictly dependent on temperature. You can't change the Ksp limit by dumping in more salt or pouring in more water. Only heat can change the fundamental limit. Exactly. For almost all solid ionic compounds, the dissolution process is endothermic. Therefore, raising the temperature shifts the equilibrium to the right, increasing the KSP. Yes. The gas molecules have to slow down and lose kinetic energy to get trapped in the liquid. Therefore raising the temperature actually shifts the equilibrium to the left, decreasing their solubility. Which explains why a warm soda goes flat much faster than a cold soda because the heat forces the carbon dioxide out of solution. Exactly. And for gases, pressure is a huge factor. This is governed by Henry's law. Henry's law states that the solubility of a gas liquid is directly proportional to the partial pressure of that gas directly above the liquid. Which is exactly what causes the bends in our scuba diver from the intro. Right. So at a depth of 100 feet, the pressure of the compressed air they're breathing is massive. This extreme pressure forces a huge volume of nitrogen gas to dissolve into their blood plasma. And as long as they stay at that depth, the system is at equilibrium. But when they rocket to the surface, the ambient pressure suddenly drops back to normal atmospheric levels. The partial pressure of nitrogen drops, which means the solubility limit, the KSP of the nitrogen gas, instantly plummets. The blood can literally no longer hold all that dissolved nitrogen. So the system violently corrects itself, forcing the excess gas out of solution. Bubbles form in the blood vessels, blocking blood flow to vital organs. Yet. To determine the current state of a system, we calculate the ion product, or IP. I really like to use a highway Drahagen analogy here. The KSP is the strict speed limit posted on the side of the road. It's a fixed law for that specific highway. Okay, I follow. The ion product, the IP, is what your speedometer currently reads. It's how fast you are driving right at this very second. That's perfect. The mathematical formula for IP is identical to Ksp. You multiply the current concentrations of the ions together. The power comes from comparing the IP to the KSP. So if your calculated IP is less than the KSP, you're driving under the speed limit. The solution is unsaturated. Right. The water has plenty of room. The thermodynamics are telling the system to keep dissolving more solute. And if your IP equals the KSP, you're driving exactly the speed limit. The system is saturated and in perfect dynamic equilibrium. But what happens if the IP is greater than the KSP? You're speeding? You're speeding! If the IP exceeds the KSP, the solution is supersaturated. How does that even happen? This usually happens if you heat a solution, dissolve a massive amount of solute, and then let it cool very very slowly and carefully. It is holding more dissolved solute than it theoretically should be able to at that temperature. So it's a highly unstable state. Very unstable. If you hit a bump in the road, you crash. If you scratch the side of the glass or drop in a single tiny crystal, the thermodynamics violently correct the imbalance. The excess solute instantly precipitates out of the water as a solid crystal until the dissolved concentration drops back down to the KSP limit. Exactly. Yeah. Which brings us to a phenomenon the M FAT tests constantly. The common ion effect. Oh yeah. What happens when we intentionally mess with an equilibrium system by introducing an outside ion? The common ion effect is really just Le Chatelet's principle in disguise. Imagine you have a saturated solution of silver chloride. It is perfectly balanced. Silver ions and chloride ions are floating around happily. Now you pour a beaker of hydrochloric acid into the mixture. Well, HCl completely dissociates into hydrogen ions and chloride ions. Hydrogen ions are spectators here, they don't interact with the silver. But you have just flooded the system with a massive wave of excess chloride ions. Chloride is common to both compounds. We just spiked the product side of our silver chloride equilibrium. And according to Le Chatelier's principle, a system at equilibrium will shift to relieve an applied stress. By drastically increasing the concentration of chloride, you've stressed the system. So the system will attempt to consume that excess chloride by shifting the reaction to the less. It shifts back toward the solid reactant. The excess chloride collides with the dissolved silver ions, forcing them to bond and precipitate out as solid silver chloride at the bottom of the beaker. So the practical result of the common ion effect is that the molar solubility of a salt is significantly reduced when it is dissolved in a solution that already contains one of its constituent ions. Precisely. I always picture it like trying to board a train car. If you are getting on an empty train, it's easy to find a seat that's dissolving in pure water. Right. But if you try to board a train that is already packed with people going to the exact same destination, there are very few seats left. You can't fit, your solubility goes way down. I love that analogy. But what if we want to go the other way? What if a physician or a chemist actually wants to force a stubborn solid to dissolve? Then we utilize complex ion formation. We mentioned this earlier, but now we can look at the thermodynamics of it. Forming a complex ion is the ultimate trick to force a solid into solution. It has the exact opposite effect of a common ion. Okay, walk us through the mechanism. Let's say we have that pile of solid silver chloride at the bottom of the beaker. We want it to dissolve. We add a bunch of ammonia. The ammonia molecules act as ligands. They seek out the very small amount of silver ions that are naturally dissolved in the water and bond with them, creating a Diamine Silver Complex ion. The VIP gets surrounded by the entourage. Exactly. But here is the critical part. By forming that complex, the ammonia essentially kidnaps the free silver ions out of the water. It removes them from the original AGCL equilibrium. And how does Le Chatelier's principle respond to the removal of a product? It shifts the reaction to the right to replace it. As the ammonia continuously consumes the free silver ions, the solid silver chloride is forced to continuously dissolve to try and replenish the missing silver. And the equilibrium constant for forming the complex, which is called the formation constant or KF, is typically astronomically larger than a KSP of the solid, right? Yes, it acts like an unstoppable thermodynamic vacuum, violently pulling the solid lattice apart and sucking it into solution. So to summarize the equilibrium levers for the Adding a common ion pushes the reaction left, decreasing solubility. Forming a complex ion pulls the reaction right, dramatically increasing solubility. That perfectly transitions us to the final major concept of the chapter, Section 9.4 Colligative Properties. Right. We've covered the mechanics of salvation, the math of measuring it, and the thermodynamic laws that dictate the absolute limits. Now, when you take pure water and dump a bunch of dissolved particles into it, the physical properties of that water fundamentally change. The liquid literally behaves differently. We classify these changes under the umbrella of collegative properties. And the strict definition is paramount here. Colligative properties are physical properties of solutions that depend only on the concentration of dissolved particles. Only on the number. Right, they depend entirely on the sheer number of particles and they absolutely do not depend on the chemical identity of the solute. The water does not care if you dissolve table salt, baking soda, glucose, or urea. It only counts how many pieces are floating around. Because the entire concept hinges on counting particles, we must introduce the Van Hoff factor represented by the lowercase letter I. Oh man, this is where so many points are lost on the MPAT. The Van Hof factor is simply a measure of how many individual particles a single molecule shatters into when it dissolves in water. Right. If you dissolve a covalent molecule, like glucose or sucrose, it does not dissociate. The molecule stays completely intact. One molecule of solid sugar dissolves into one molecule of aqueous sugar. Therefore the Van't Hoff factor, I, is one. But ionic compounds shatter. If you drop sodium chloride, NaCl, into water, the ionic bonds break. One unit of NaCl physically splits into one free floating sodium ion and one free floating chloride ion. One piece becomes two. The Van't Hoff factor is two. For calcium chloride, carry aCl, the lattice breaks into one calcium ion and two separate chloride ions. One piece becomes three, the Van't Hoff factor is three. And the golden rule for colligative properties is that you must remember to multiply the mathematical formulas by I to account for true concentration of particles causing the effect. Exactly. Let's explore the first colligative property: vapor pressure depression, which is governed by Raoult's law. Okay, to understand the depression, we must understand vapor pressure itself. Vapor pressure is the pressure exerted by gas molecules that have evaporated from the surface of a liquid, right? Yes. If you have a beaker of pure water, the entire surface area of the liquid is occupied by water molecules. They're constantly gaining enough kinetic energy to jump into the gas phase above the liquid. But what happens when we stir in a bunch of salute particles? The salute particles disperse evenly throughout the liquid, including at the surface. They act as physical obstacles. They take up space at the surface boundary. Which means there are simply fewer water molecules directly exposed to the air. Yes. I picture it like dumping a bucket of ping pong balls into a swimming pool. The balls float on the top and cover a large percentage of the water. Because there is less water actually exposed to the hot sun, less water can evaporate. That's exactly it. Because the evaporation rate drops, there are fewer gas molecules hovering above the liquid. Fewer gas molecules mean a lower vapor pressure. The vapor pressure is depressed strictly proportionally to the mole fraction of the solute added. And this vapor pressure depression is the dominator that knocks over the next colligative property, boiling point elevation. Why does throwing salt into water force the boiling temperature to go up? Well, we have to define the physical threshold of boiling. A liquid boils exactly when its internal vapor pressure rises enough to perfectly equal the atmospheric pressure pushing down on it from the room. Oh, and we just established that adding salt lowers the starting vapor pressure of the water. Precisely. The baseline vapor pressure is now further away from matching the atmospheric pressure. To force the depressed vapor pressure back up to the boiling threshold, you have to supply even more kinetic energy. You must heat the water to a higher temperature than normal. The math here is beautifully simple. The change in the boiling temperature is strictly proportional to the number of particles. The formula is delta TB equals I times Kb times M. You take the boiling point constant for the solvent, multiply it by the concentration, and multiply it by the Van Hoff factor. And notice the concentration unit, it's molality, the lowercase m, because we are dealing with massive temperature shifts. The opposite end of the thermometer gives us our third colligative freezing point depression. Why does adding salute cause the freezing point to drop? Let's look at the mechanical process of freezing. As liquid water cools, the molecules lose kinetic energy. They slow down until they can precisely align their hydrogen bonds to form a highly organized, rigid, crystalline solid ice. When you introduce salute particles into the liquid, they continuously move around and physically disrupt the formation of that crystal lattice. They wedge themselves between the water molecules, preventing them from achieving the perfect alignment needed to solidify. Right. They are like annoying hyperactive party guests constantly cutting through the middle of a group of people trying to form a synchronized conga line. That's a great visual. To force the water molecules to lock into the solid lattice despite these relentless obstacles, you must drain even more kinetic energy from the system. You have to slow the molecules down even further. Therefore, the freezing point depresses below the normal threshold. The formula mirrors boiling point elevation. Delta Tf I times Kf times M. And this is the exact mechanism behind the trucks that spray salt on icy winter roads. They aren't spraying hot chemicals to melt the ice. In fact, dissolving salt is endothermic. It actually makes the slush slightly colder. They are exploding collogative chemistry by dissolving huge amounts of salt into the moisture on the road. They drastically lower the freezing point of the water. They alter the physical properties of the puddles so that the water remains a liquid even when the ambient air temperature drops far below zero degrees Celsius. That leaves us with the final colligative property and arguably the most important one for biological systems: osmotic pressure. Imagine a U shaped glass tube. Right in the bottom curve of the U, there is a semipermeable membrane. This membrane has pores that are large enough to allow water molecules to pass through, but too small to allow large solute particles, like glucose, to cross. Okay, so you pour pure water into the left side of the tube, and you pour a highly concentrated glucose solution into the right side of the tube. The fundamental law of thermodynamics is that nature abhors a concentration gradient. The universe wants both sides of that tube to have the exact same concentration. But the glucose is trapped. It physically cannot cross the membrane to spread out into the pure water, so the water has to do the work. The pure water will spontaneously flow across the membrane into the concentrated side in a desperate attempt to dilute the glucose. And as water flows to the right, the water level on the right side of the U tube literally rises against the force of gravity. The highly concentrated side is essentially sucking the water across the membrane. Wow. Osmotic pressure, represented by the Greek letter pi, is a measure of that exact sucking force generated by the concentration gradient. The formula is pi equals I times M times R times T. So the osmotic pressure equals the Van't Hoff factor multiplied by the molarity times the ideal gas constant times the absolute temperature in Kelvin. Yeah. I notice it uses molarity, the capital M, instead of molality. Yes. Osmotic pressure is generally measured at a constant temperature, like the internal temperature of a human body, so thermal expansion of the volume isn't an issue. Molarity is perfectly acceptable here. Alright, we've covered a massive amount of ground through Chapter but the reality of the MCAT is that knowing the science is really only half the battle. Surviving the traps set by the test makers is the other half. So, let's bulletproof you against their favorite tricks. Based on thousands of practice questions and AMC materials, we have identified four major traps designed to punish students who rush through their calculations. Let's do it. Trap number one: molarity versus molality in phase change formulas. In the high stress environment of the exam, your brain naturally wants to take shortcuts. It is incredibly easy to read a passage, spot a concentration value with a capital M, and immediately plug it into the boiling point elevation equation. But the equation is delta Tv equals I times K times molality, the lowercase m. Right. If you use molarity, which is moles per liter of total solution, instead of molality, which is moles per kilogram of pure solvent, your final calculated temperature will be slightly off. And I can guarantee you that the test makers have already done the math using the wrong unit, and that incorrect answer will be sitting right there as choice A or B waiting for you to select it. Always double check the denominator of your concentration. Trap two Forgetting the Vant Hoff Factor We see this constantly on UWorld and Kaplan practice tests. They love to bait students with compounds like magnesium chloride, MgCl2, or calcium nitrate. A student will correctly identify the colligative property, correctly calculate the concentration, execute the multiplication perfectly, and feel incredibly confident about their answer. But they forgot that MgCl2 breaks into three distinct pieces. They forgot to multiply their entire calculation by an I of three. The math was flawless, but the chemistry was completely absent. A great habit is to always write the I variable on your scratch paper before you even look at the numbers. Trap number three involved KSP denominators. We touched on this during the equilibrium section, but it warrants repeating because the visual muscle memory from earlier chemistry chapters is so powerful. Yeah, for every other equilibrium constant you've learned, like kek, ka for acids, Kb for bases, it is always a fraction. It is always products divided by reactants. Your hand naturally wants to draw line and write a denominator. But KSP specifically describes the dissolution of a solid. And pure solids are excluded from equilibrium expressions. There is absolutely no denominator. KSP is strictly a multiplication problem. If you take the product of the ions and divide it by the mass of the solid chunk in the beaker, you have fallen headfirst into the trap. Trap number four: the wording around pH changes. This is an MCAT expertise tip directly from the text regarding complex biochemical passages like chemiosmotic coupling in the mitochondria. The trap here isn't mathematical, it's linguistic. When you read the phrase the pH of the inner membrane space drops, your brain instinctively associates the word drop with a decrease in quantity. But the pH scale is logarithmic and inverse. It operates backwards. A pH drop means the numerical value of the pH is getting lower, moving from seven down to five or four. This means the solution is becoming significantly more acidic. Therefore, a pH drop actually indicates a massive increase in the hydrogen ion concentration. Do not let the English word drop trick your intuition into thinking protons are disappearing. Active, deliberate reading is mandatory. Okay, we've warned you about the traps. Now let's see if you can navigate them. If you're listening to this right now, I want you to actively visualize these scenarios. Try to deduce the answer before we explain it. Applying the concepts in real time is the best way to solidify the neural pathways. Question one. Imagine a beaker sitting on a lab bench. Inside is a saturated aqueous solution of copper, sulfide, COS. Because it's saturated, there's a visible pile of solid COS resting at the bottom of the beaker. You take a pipette and slowly add aqueous ammonia to the beaker. The passage tells you that ammonia acts as a ligand and forms a complex ion with copper. What happens to the physical size of the pile of solid COS at the bottom of the beaker? Think about the two competing equilibrium systems here. Let's trace the logic. The ammonia forms a complex ion with the dissolved copper. This essentially kidnaps the free copper ions from the water, pulling them completely out of the standard CUS dissolution equilibrium. We are removing a product. According to Lachettelier's principle, the system must shift to the right to replace the missing copper. This thermodynamic vacuum forces more of the CUS lattice to break apart and dissolve. Therefore, the pile of solid CUS at the bottom of the beaker shrinks. It decreases. Excellent application of the complex ion vacuum effect. Question two. This is a fast reflex level question. What is the Van't Hoff factor for calcium nitrate? (Kanon03)two? Remember how polyatomic ions behave in water? The Van't Hoff factor I is three. When the ionic bonds break, you get one free calcium ion. But the two nitrate ions do not shatter into individual nitrogen and oxygen atoms. Covalent bonds don't break during simple dissolution. The nitrate ions stay together as two distinct free floating units. One calcium plus two nitrates equals three total particles. If you can smoothly navigate the distinction between ionic dissociation and covalent stability, your colligative property calculations will be rock solid. Which brings us to the final consolidation of our deep dive. Let's retrace our steps through the molecular landscape of Chapter nine. We began with the fundamental thermodynamics of solvation. We saw how the immense increase in entropy, the chaotic dispersal of a rigid crystal lattice into a vast liquid volume drives the dissolution process to be spontaneous, even when it requires an input of thermal energy. We moved past qualitative descriptions and locked down the precise units required for clinical calculations. We explored when to use the volumetric convenience of molarity, the temperature independent stability of molarity, and the rapid shortcut of one milligram per liter for parts per million. We examined the delicate dynamic balance of Ksp equilibrium. We learned that a saturated solution is not a static dead end but a buzzing high speed intersection where dissolution and precipitation are constantly occurring at the exact same rate. We saw how Le Chatelier's principle allows us to manipulate this balance, using a common ion to force a precipitate out of solution, or using a complex ion to tear a stubborn solid apart. Finally, we looked at how the sheer quantity of dissolved particles alters the physical identity of the solvent through colligative properties. So loose particles physically block the surface to lower vapor pressure, force the liquid to demand more heat to boil, physically obstruct crystal lattices to lower freezing points, and generate massive osmotic pressures to suck water across biological membranes. It forms a cohesive, interconnected narrative of physical chemistry. Before we sign off, we want to leave you with a final, provocative thought, something to mull over that applies the raw chemistry we just learned to a life or death clinical In the introductory framing of this text, there is a brief mention of diabetic ketoacidosis, or DKA. In a patient suffering from DKA, their body lacks the insulin required to pull glucose out of the blood and into the cells. As a result, massive amounts of excess glucose continuously build up in the bloodstream. The patient's blood plasma fundamentally transforms into a hyper concentrated solution of glucose. Now based purely on the mechanisms of osmotic pressure and colligative properties we just explored, What does that sheer number of dissolved glucose particles in the blood do to the water trapped inside the surrounding tissues and cells? Glucose is a covalent molecule. So it's Van Hoff vector is only one. It doesn't break apart. But if the overall molarity of the glucose in the blood gets astronomically high, the concentration gradient becomes extreme. The osmotic pressure formula tells us that this hyper concentrated blood will exert a massive sucking force across the semi permeable membranes of the body cells. The blood will relentlessly draw water out of the surrounding tissues to try and dilute the excess glucose. The patient's cells literally begin to shrivel up. It causes severe, dangerous cellular dehydration. The chemistry we just calculated on a theoretical lab bench is the exact mechanism causing physiological chaos on the hospital floor. It is a stark reminder that you aren't just memorizing formulas to pass a test. You are learning the molecular rules that govern human life. That is exactly why you are putting in the hours. MSAT General Chemistry Chapter nine: Solutions. Right, so we are diving straight into, the really foundational characteristics and behaviors of solutions today. Like we'll be looking at how they form, the mathematical languages we use to measure their concentration, and how to evaluate their dynamic equilibria. Yeah, and before we get into any of the textbook definitions, I want to pose a paradox. It's this real world scenario from the Kaplan text that completely threw me when I first thought of the thermodynamics of it. Oh, the cold pack one. Yes, exactly. Think about an instant cold pack, the kind you snap and shake when you sprain your ankle on a hike. That cold pack works by dissolving ammonium nitrate in water. Right. Now think about making a pitcher of sweet tea. You dissolve sugar in water. Both of these rely on the exact same physical process, right? Solution chemistry, solute goes into solvent. Yeah, conceptually they are identical processes. But one creates freezing cold temperatures, literally pulling heat out of the environment and chilling your skin while the other actually requires heat. Like you have to boil the water on the stove to get the sugar to dissolve effectively. How does the exact same physical mechanism result in such wildly different thermodynamic outcomes? Well, answer to that paradox lies in the invisible molecular tug of war happening inside the beaker or inside that plastic coal pack. It all comes down to the energy required to break bonds versus the energy released when new bonds form. And unlocking that tug of war is basically the entire mission of our deep dive today. Which makes me realize why this topic isn't just like middle school science fair material. We aren't just stirring salt into water. This is the ultimate biological problem. Oh, absolutely. If you think about it, almost all important reactions in the human body take place in solutions. Blood, cellular, cytoplasm, interstitial fluid, it's all solution chemistry. And you're hitting on the exact reason the MCAT cares so much about this chapter. I mean, understanding solutions is the only way to comprehend how molecules actually find each other and interact in living systems. Your enzymes can't function, your neurons can't fire, and your blood can't buffer acids without the principles we're about to cover. Right, which explains its placement in the Kaplan sequence. It's like the bridge to the really highly tested upcoming stuff like acid base chemistry and biological enzymes. It really serves as the absolute foundational bedrock.
