So if we jump into time machine back to like the late nineteenth century, the first framework we'd see is the Arrhenius definition. Yes, Vontae Arrhenius, he basically just said, look at what happens in water. Keep it simple. Simple. His concept was born out of the practical reality of laboratories back then, where water was just the universal solvent for everything. So under his framework, an arrhenius acid is basically any species that when you dissolve it in water dissociates to produce an excess of hydrogen ions, right? So dollars plus dollar. Correct. And the Arrhenius base is any species that dissociates in water to produce an excess of hydroxide ions. So, oh, Lollers. Got it. So if I take like hydrogen chloride gas, drop it into a beaker of water, it splits into bolyte plus plus and all dollars. It floods the water with protons. Boom, it's an acid. Boom, exactly. And if you drop sodium hydroxide in, it splits into nalar plus and all dollars. Floods the water with hydroxide. It's a base. But there's a pretty glaring limitation there, isn't there? A massive one. It strictly, uncompromisingly requires an aqueous environment. Arrhenius, you know, he just had no vocabulary for a reaction occurring in a gas cloud or say an organic solvent like benzene. I've actually always thought of the Ardennes definition like a really strict nightclub dress code. Oh, like that. How so? Well, if you're a molecule and you don't have an aller at the very front of your formula like HDL or HNO3D3, the bouncer isn't letting you in as an acid. And if you don't have an aller at the end, like an aller or two, you aren't getting in as a base. Right. Oh, and the nightclip itself is completely underwater. If the party isn't happening in an aqueous solution, the Arrhenius Bouncer completely ignores you. That's a perfect analogy. And that restrictive nature is exactly why the definition had to expand. Because what about things that don't fit that mold? Exactly. Take ammonia and H3A3 dollars If you dissolve ammonia in water, it actually reacts to generate OA3 dollars So in water, Arrhenius would nod and say, yes, that creates a basic solution. But what if you aren't in water? Right. What if you're running a reaction where liquid ammonia is the solvent itself? The Arrhenius definition suddenly goes completely blind. Chemists needed a framework that focused on the transfer of the particles themselves, regardless of the solvent. And that's where Brinstedt and Lowry come in, Yeah. Sometime around 1923. Exactly. The Brinstedt Lowry definition, they focused on the proton itself. So an acid is a proton donor, and a base is a proton acceptor. Which is so much broader. But wait, let's clarify what we mean by a proton here for the listener. Good call. A hydrogen ion in H plus dollars is essentially just a bare proton. It's a hydrogen atom that, well, it's been stripped of its one and only electron. So it's just a concentrated point of positive charge? Yes, with no electron cloud to shield it. Which means it is violently reactive. So a acid is a molecule willing to give away that highly reactive proton. And a base is willing to take it, which completely opens up the nightclub. You don't need water anymore. Not at all. If you have two gases reacting and one molecule throws a proton to the other, you have an acid base reaction. But, it introduces something new too. The conjugate pairs. Yes. The concept of conjugate acid base pairs is absolutely central to this entire chapter. Because Bernstedt Lowery is defined by a transaction, you know, a transfer, you literally cannot have an acid without a base. You can't throw a ball if there's no one there to catch it. Right. So let's look at that ammonia molecule, an H3 3A12er, dissolved in water. Ammonia has a lone pair of electrons on its nitrogen atom. It uses those electrons to reach out and grab a proton from a water molecule. So in that transaction, ammonia accepted the proton, making it the base. And water donated the proton, making water the acid. But now look at what they become after the transaction. Okay, so the NH33ha gained a proton and a positive charge, becoming NH33ha plus dollar, the ammonium ion. And that ammonium ion now has an extra proton it could potentially give away. Therefore, NH4 dollars plus dollars is the conjugate acid of the base NH33. So the conjugate acid is basically just the base wearing its new proton hat. I love that, yes. And the water molecule, TH2O dollars having lost its proton, is now $0, the hydroxide ion. It has a negative charge and is perfectly positioned to accept a proton back. So OHA is the conjugate base of the acid water. Exactly. Now for everyone listening, there is a massive conceptual trap here that UWorld points out and the MPAT loves to exploit. It's an inclusivity trap regarding these two definitions. The rule is absolute. Every single arrhenius acid is a acid. Because if a molecule's whole identity is dissociating in water to give up, it is by definition donating a proton. Right. But the reverse is false. Not every acid is an Arrhenius acid. Because you could be donating a proton in a non aqueous solvent. Which fails the underwater night club rule. Exactly. So the Bernsted Lowry circle completely swallows the Arrhenius circle. It covers far more territory. But even that wasn't broad enough, right? Especially for organic chemistry. No, it wasn't. Gilbert Lewis realized that by obsessing over the true currency of chemical reactions. Which is electrons, you always follow the money, and in chemistry, the money is the negative charge. Exactly. The Lieu's definition flicks the perspective. An acid is an electron pair acceptor, and a base is electron pair donor. So we're no longer tracking the positive nucleus, we're tracking the negative lone pair. Right. A Lewis base has an unshared pair of electrons it wants to share. A Lewis acy has vacant orbital, or a significant positive charge, that eagerly wants to accept those electrons. This is basically a bridge straight into organic chemistry. If you're reviewing organic reactions, you already know these terms. You do. Lewis acids are electrophiles. They are electron deficient, so they love electrons. Lewis bases are nucleophiles. They are electron rich, so they love finding a positive nucleus to attack. Can we visualize a classic organic reaction to make this concrete? Like, boron trifluoride reacting with ammonia. Perfect example. So boron trifluoride, BF3O2 there. Boron is in group three of the periodic table. It forms three subal bonds with three fluorine atoms. Which gives boron a share of only six valence electrons. Right. It does not have a complete octet. It possesses a completely empty orbital just sitting there wide open. It is a profound electrophile. Meanwhile, ammonia, NH three forty three, has that nitrogen atom with a big juicy lone pair of electrons just taking up space. So when they collide, the nitrogen atom donates both of those electrons straight into boron's empty orbital. They form a bond, but notice what didn't happen. No protons were transferred? None. A Brinsted Lowry observer would look at that and say absolutely nothing occurred. But the Lewis definition recognizes this as a fundamental acid base neutralization. And that resulting bond is called a coordinate covalent bond, right? Yeah. Because both shared electrons came from only one of the participating atoms. Now is this just an organic chemistry quirk? Or does this electron pair acceptance actually show up in biology? Oh, it is the foundation of human respiration. Wait, really? How? Look at hemoglobin in your red blood cells. At the center of the heme group is an iron cation, $5.02 plus dollars. That iron ion does not have any protons to donate. It completely fails the test. But it has emptied orbitals. Yes. So when you inhale, an oxygen molecule acting as a Lewis base donates an electron pair into the iron's empty orbital. The iron acts as a Lewis acid. Oh wow, that coordinate covalent bond is literally how oxygen is transported through your arteries. It's incredible, right? The iron is an acid, the oxygen is a base. So the Lewis definition is the ultimate, most inclusive circle. Every Arrhenius acid and every Brunstadt Lowery acid is also a Lewis acid, but it doesn't work the other way around. Spot on. Now before we move on to the next section, we have to address the chameleons, amphoteric and amphorrotic species. Ones that can't make up their minds. Basically. An amphoteric species can act as either an acid or a base depending entirely on the environment it finds itself in. So if you place it in a highly basic solution, it acts like an acid. If you place it in a highly acidic solution, it acts like a base. Right, and the word amphoproduct specifically ties back to the proton transfer. It means the molecule can either gain a proton or lose a proton. Water is the classic example of that. It can become 3H3 dollars plus already a dollar. But what about in the human body? What's the MSAT's favorite biological chameleon? Without a doubt, amino acids. The AAMC utilizes amino acids constantly to test this concept because their entire structure is fundamentally amphoteric. Because every amino acid has a carboxylic acid group, which is acidic and can donate a proton. And every amino acid possesses an amino group which is inherently basic and can accept a proton. I want to really dig into the how of this. Because students just memorize the word 'swaterian' without understanding the mechanism of why it happens in the blood. Yeah, let's break down the mechanism. It's all about the relationship between the pKa of the specific functional group and the pH of the surrounding environment. Okay, so the carboxylic acid group typically has a pKa around two, right. And the amino group typically has a pKa around nine. Let's say this amino acid is just floating along in human blood, which has a pH of 7.4. Okay, at a pH of 7.4, the environment is far more basic than the pKa of the carboxylic acid group, right? Because 7.4 is greater than two. Yeah. Because the environment is relatively basic, it strips the proton away from the carboxylic acid. The group becomes deprotonated, holding a negative charge a little of the lever. But what about the amino group on the exact same molecule? Well, the pH of the blood, 7.4, is more acidic than the pK of the amino group, because 7.4 is less than nine. Ah, so because the environment is relatively acidic compared to that specific group, the amino group retains its proton. Exactly. It stays protonated, holding a positive charge NH3 dollars plus dollars. So in the exact same bloodstream, the molecule has a negative end and a positive end. That is a suturium. The molecule is internally neutralized but it's highly polar. And you know if that amino acid travels to the stomach where the pH drops to like 1.5 That's a massive flood of environmental protons. Huge. It will force the car carboxyl group to pick its proton back up. The molecule suddenly shifts to having an overall positive charge. That chameleon like ability to shift states based on pH. That's literally how proteins fold and enzymes bind, isn't it? It is the driving force of biochemistry. Which perfectly transitions us into Section 10.2: Properties. Because if an acid's identity is based on throwing away a proton, we need to know what happens when we put it in water an environment full of molecules waiting to catch it. Right, how do they behave when left alone, and how do we measure them? First, we have to examine the baseline state of water itself. We tend to think of pure water as static, just, you know, intact, 20 work two hour molecules sitting peacefully. But it's actually incredibly dynamic. Extremely dynamic. The molecules are constantly vibrating, moving, colliding. And occasionally, two water molecules collide with the perfect trajectory and enough kinetic energy that one molecule violently rips a proton away from the other. So the water is acting as an acid in a base with itself. Yes, this is the auto ionization of water. The equation is 2H2O plus H2O in equilibrium with three 3O plus Hg plus in OH hydroxide. One bond breaks another forms. And because it's reversible, it establishes an equilibrium. Governed by the water dissociation constant. Which at standard room temperature two ninety eight Kelvin is exactly $10.14 dollars Grant, we calculate dollars by multiplying the concentration of the products 3H3 dollars plus plus tapascivers. But here is a fundamental thermodynamic rule the MCAT will test: $2 is only affected by changes in temperature. I really want the listener to grasp why. If I take a beaker of water and I compress it with massive pressure or dump a bucket of salt into it, Tdal's does not budge. Why only temperature? Because the autoionization of water involves breaking a covalent bond between oxygen and hydrogen, Breaking bonds always requires an input of energy. So the forward reaction is endothermic. Exactly. Heat is essentially a reactant. So according to Le Chatelier's principle, if you add a reactant, system shifts to the right to consume it. If you raise the temperature, you're adding heat. The system shifts right, breaking more water molecules apart. Creating more hydronium and hydroxide. Which means the product concentrations go up, so the equilibrium constant itself, codeine dollars, physically increases at higher temperatures. It's not ten fourteen anymore. Precisely. But returning to a standard two ninety eight Kelvin, tailey is locked at $10.14 dollars This means three c. Wallers plus nollers and waller riddollers are playing a zero sum game. If you add an external acid driving the hydronium up, the hydroxide must proportionally decrease so their product stays exactly $10.14 dollars They're perfectly inversely proportional. Now expressing these concentrations in scientific notation, like ten, five, 11, or ten, eleven solomarolar, is incredibly tedious for calculations, which is why the pH scale was invented. Right, because nobody wants to write out all those zeros. Exactly. In chemistry, the lowercase letter p is a mathematical operator. It just means take the negative base 10 logarithm of. So PaPO is simply the negative log of the hydrogen ion concentration and PUAK is the negative log of the hydroxide ion concentration. Yes. So if pure water at two ninety eight Kelvin has a hydrogen ion concentration of $10 in molar, the negative log of $10 is just seven, so the pH is seven. And the pH is also seven, which gives us that super useful formula. PH plus pOH is 14.4. If you know one, you instantly know the other. But the MCA rarely gives you a perfect power of 10. Your approximation of 4.6 is easily close enough to select the correct multiple choice answer. The options will be spaced out like 3.4, 4.4, 5.4. This shortcut will guide you flawlessly. That is such a time saver. So once we can measure the pH, we can categorize these molecules based on how aggressively they impact that pH, meaning strong versus weak acids and bases. Right, and we need to clarify that strong and weak here has nothing to do with how dangerous or corrosive the chemical is. It refers entirely to the degree of dissociation. So a strong acid completely, 100% dissociates it into its ions and water. Yes. If you carefully measure out exactly one mole of hydrogen chloride gas and dissolve it into a liter of water, when you look closely at that solution you will find zero intact light selenema molecules. None. Just one mole of water bills plus dollars one mole of the dollars, dollars. The reaction goes to absolute completion. Because of this, calculating the pH of a strong acid is direct. The molarity of the acid equals the molarity of the hydrogen ions. A 0.1 molar solution of YHCSL yields a 0.1 molar concentration of protons. You take the negative log and you have your pH. But the weak acids are trickier. Because they only partially dissociate. If you drop one mole of acetic acid into water, perhaps only 1% actually give up their proton, the other 99% remain intact. Establishing an equilibrium state, which means we have to use an equilibrium constant. Collis for acids, cattle for bases for bases. Right. The formula for collar is the concentration of the products over the reactants. Plus times the conjugate base, all dollars, all divided by the concentration of the intact acid cells. So a smaller collar means a weaker acid. A tiny collar means the numerator is tiny, very little dissociation happened. The intact molecule is holding on to that proton for dear life. Exactly. And now we must connect the strength of an acid to the stability of its conjugate base. This is, hands down, the single most important conceptual bridge between general chemistry and organic chemistry on the MCAT. Let's paint a picture here. I always think of an acid molecule like a parent holding a squirming, screaming toddler. Okay, the toddler is the proton. Yes. The parent desperately wants to put the toddler down. But the parent will only let go if the environment they're placing the toddler into, the resulting state of the molecule, the conjugate base, is incredibly stable and safe. That analogy maps perfectly to the thermodynamics. An acid's willingness to donate a proton is entirely dictated by how thermodynamically stable the resulting conjugate base is. Because if the conjugate base is highly stable, it represents a low energy state, and the universe always favors moving toward lower energy. The acid will gladly jettison the proton to reach that stable state. But what actually makes a conjugate base stable at the atomic level? You have to look at periodic trends and molecular structure. Let's look at why HClO is a strong acid. When it loses its proton, it becomes chloride ion. Pizziol, why is the chloride dollars so stable holding a negative charge? Well first, chlorine is highly electronegative, so its nucleus pulls strongly on electrons. Yes, and second, more importantly, the chlorine atom has a large atomic radius. It has a massive electron cloud. Exactly. That single extra electron isn't confined to a tiny space. Its negative charge is diffused and spread out over a large volume. This dramatically reduces electrostatic repulsion within the ion. So because the CLR dollars is so stable and comfortable with that extra electron, it has absolutely no desire to grab a proton back. It is inert. It is an incredibly weak base. Which brings us to the cardinal rule. Strong acids produce extremely weak inert conjugate bases. Each Clr produces the inert taxane. What about sulfuric acid? Th2SO42ton, that's a strong acid too. It is, but its stability comes from a different mechanism, resonance. Let's visualize that resonance. Because this is where organic chemistry concepts bleed right into gen chem. When CHSO4 Torr loses a proton, it becomes the hydrogen sulfate ion (HSO4 dollars ). That negative charge is initially left on a single oxygen atom. But oxygen is electronegative, and the adjacent sulfur is double bonded to other oxygens. The pi electrons can easily shift. So the negative charge doesn't just sit isolated on one atom, it delocalizes. Right. The electron cloud smears itself across multiple oxygen atoms in the molecule. By spreading the charge out, the electrostatic repulsion is minimized. The molecule achieves immense thermodynamic stability. Because the HSO4 taller tetra four adalars is so deeply stabilized by resonance, the original tetra four adalar interfer four molecule is incredibly eager to lose the proton, making it a very strong acid. So if you are staring at an M SAT question asking you to rank the acidity of four different molecules. You don't even look at the proton. You mentally rip the proton off of every single choice, draw a conjugate base and ask yourself, which of these four bases is most stable? Exactly. Look for resonance, look for electronegative atoms pulling charge via induction, look for large atomic radii. The most stable base came from the strongest acid. Phenomenal strategy. But you must also remember the corollary: weak acids produce weak conjugate bases. A common trap is assuming a weak acid produces a strong conjugate base. It does not. Like acetic acid producing the acetate ion. Acetate is a weak base stronger than the inert chloride ion, but definitely not a strong base like sodium hydroxide. Correct. Now, when you mix these acids and bases together, they neutralize. You get salt formation. HCl lever reacting with no feature undergoes a double displacement reaction to produce NOTCL, table salt, and water. And whether that resulting salt solution is acidic, basic, or neutral depends entirely on the relative strengths of the acid and base you mixed. Yes, which we will explore mathematically when we discuss titrations. But before we get there, section 10.3 introduces a complication: polyvalence and normality. Right, because up until now, we've basically talked about monoprotic acids one molecule, one proton to give. But what happens when a molecule is holding multiple screaming toddlers? That's a polyprotic or polyvalent acid. It has multiple acidic protons. Sulfuric acid 3D2SO4 sores is diprotic. Phosphoric acid 3D3PO4 is tri product. But they don't just dump all their protons at once. It happens sequentially, step by step. And each step is harder than the last. Think about the thermodynamics. The first proton leaving H2SO4 alerting Loller is relatively easy. It's leaving a neutral molecule. That first dissociation is a strong acid process, it goes to completion. Leaving the HSO4 or elastradiololr, which now has a negative charge. And it still has a proton it can lose. But that second proton is a positive charge trying to tear itself away from an already negatively charged molecule. Electrostatic attraction heavily resists this separation. It requires significantly more energy, so the second dissociation doesn't go to completion. No, it establishes a weak acid equilibrium. The KIq for the second proton is drastically smaller than the KI for the first. Because these molecules deliver multiple protons, we cannot simply rely on the molarity of the whole molecule to tell us the concentration of the reactive protons. We need a new measurement system. Which brings us to equivalence and normality. An equivalent is simply one mole of the specific reactive species we care about. In this chapter, the species we care about is the proton, dollar hearts plus dollar, or the hydroxide ion And normality, denoted by a capital N, is just the concentration of those equivalents. Equivalents per liter. I honestly think the bicycle shop analogy is the best way to lock this in. Let's hear it. Let's say you own a bicycle shop. Molarity is how you count whole, intact bicycles. If you have one mole of bicycles, your molarity is one molar. But when we do an acid based reaction, we don't care about the frame of the bicycle. The chemical reaction only interacts with the wheels. The wheels are the protons. Every single bicycle has two wheels. So if your shop has a one molar concentration of whole bicycles, you actually have a two normal concentration of wheels. You have twice as many reactive units as you have whole molecules. That translates perfectly to chemistry. If you have a two molar solution of 2SUSO4O4, what is the normality of the hydrogen ions? Well, H2SO4O4 is our bicycle. It is two protons, two wheels. So I take my two moles of bicycles per liter and multiply it by two wheels per bicycle. That gives me a four normal, or 4N solution of hydrogen ions. Perfect. Molarity times the number of equivalents per molecule equals normality. You also need to understand how to calculate the gram equivalent weight. Which answers the question: How heavy is the chunk of the bicycle that gives you exactly one wheel? To calculate it, you divide the molar mass of the entire compound by the number of equivalents it produces. The molar mass of H2SO443 is roughly 98 grams per mole. Because one mole yields two moles of protons, you divide 98 by two. 49 grams. So if I walk into the lab and weigh out exactly 49 grams of sulfuric acid, I know with absolute certainty that I am holding exactly one mole of reactive protons. Understanding normality is absolutely non negotiable for section 10.4 because when you begin mixing acids and bases in the laboratory or in the human body, they do not react mole for mole, they react equivalent for equivalent. One wheel matches with one axle. Which brings us to the ultimate application of all this theory: titration and buffers. The laboratory setup for a general titration is something you likely performed in undergrad. You have an Erlenmeyer flask sitting on the bench. Inside is a solution of unknown concentration. We call this the titrant. And suspended above the flask is a long glass burette filled with the solution of a completely known standardized concentration. That's the titrant. You open the stopcock on the burette and you slowly, drop by drop, add the titrant into the flask until the reaction is complete. You are waiting for the equivalence point. The mathematical climax of the titration. Exactly. The equivalence point is the exact moment when the number of acid equivalents present in the flask perfectly equals the number of base equivalents you have added from the burette. And the equation for this is beautiful in its simplicity. One dal x vy nen dal x v b day. The normality of the acid x its volume equals the normality of the base x its volume. A vital testing strategy for the MCAT here. The volumes in this equation do not strictly need to be in liters. As long as you use the exact same volume units on both sides of the equation, say milliliters on the left and milliliters on the right, the math will work out perfectly. That saves precious seconds by avoiding unnecessary metric conversions. It does. Now, when you perform a titration, you typically plot a curve of the pH as you add the volume of titrant. The shape of that curve, and specifically the location of the equivalence point on the y axis, tells you exactly what kind of acid and base are reacting. Let's start with a strong acid reacting with a strong base. Both molecules dissociate 100%. Because of this, they neutralize each other completely, forming water and a neutral salt. The equivalence point will be located at exactly pH seven. But the MCAT rarely makes it that easy. They love testing a weak acid being titrated by a strong base. In this scenario, the equivalence point will always be basic. It will be located at a pH greater than seven. To understand why, you must understand the molecular reality at the equivalence point. Okay, let's say you're titrating weak acetic acid with strong sodium hydroxide. At the exact equivalence point, all the acetic acid has reacted with all the added sodium hydroxide. What is actually floating in the flask at that exact moment? Well, you formed a salt. So you have water, sodium ions, and acetate ions. Exactly. The sodium ion is the conjugate acid of a strong base, no Y Car O A, so it is inert. But the acetate ion is the conjugate base of a weak acid. It is a weak base itself. So floating in the water is a weak base. And it reacts with the water. Yes, a process called hydrolysis. It pulls a proton off H. $2 generates a small amount of ood deserts. Because it generated hydroxide, the pH of the solution at the equivalence point is basic, Will be greater than seven, maybe around pH 8.5 or nine. You can also use a tug of war analogy. Imagine pH seven is the centerline of a tug of war rope. On one side you have your weak acid, on the other your strong base. They are pulling against each other to neutralize. Because the strong base is, by definition, stronger, it pulls the center of the reaction, the equivalence point across the centerline and into its own basic territory. So if we reverse it and titrate a weak base with a strong acid, the strong acid wins the tug of war. The equivalence point gets dragged down below pH seven into acidic territory. Exactly. Now, how do we actually know we've reached the equivalence point during the experiment? A digital pH meter will show the curve, but visually we rely on chemical indicators. Indicators are fascinating. I always wondered how a liquid just suddenly changes color from one drop to the next. Indicators are themselves weak organic acids or bases. They have a massive, complex structural an extended system of alternating double and single bonds, known as a conjugated pi system. And that conjugated system dictates what wavelength of light the molecule absorbs. Right. And because it's a weak acid, it can gain or lose a proton. Adding or removing a single proton from that massive molecule slightly alters the electronic structure of the entire conjugated system. That tiny structural shift is enough to change the wavelength of light it absorbs. So the protonated form might appear yellow and the deprotonated form might appear blue. The crucial rule for the EMSIKAT is how to pick the right indicator. You have a whole shelf of them. How do you choose? You match the PK of the indicator to the expected pH at the equivalence point, right? You must. The indicator changes color. It shifts from the protonated to the deprotonated state when the environmental pH crosses its specific pK value. If you are titrating a weak acid with a strong base and you calculate the equivalence point will be a pH nine, you must select an indicator with a pK around nine. Exactly. And this introduces a fundamental vocabulary distinction that students frequently conflate: the equivalence point versus the end point. They sound similar, but they aren't the same. Not at all. The equivalence point is the mathematical stoichiometric reality of the reaction. It is the exact molecular moment. The equivalents perfectly match. And the end point? The end point is the human visual observation. It is the moment the indicator actually changes color and the researcher turns the stopcock to halt the titration. If you've chosen the correct indicator, the visible endpoint occurs just a tiny fraction of a drop after the mathematical equivalence point. So they represent roughly the same volume, but conceptually they're vastly different things. Equivalence is the invisible math. Endpoint is the visible color change. Exactly. Okay, let's connect this laboratory technique back to human physiology because this is where the MCAT truly shines. We need to talk about buffers. A buffer is an aqueous solution that actively resists large fluctuations in pH when you add small amounts of strong acid or strong base to it. And to construct a buffer, you need a specific mixture. A weak acid combined with its conjugate salt, or a weak base combined with its conjugate salt? Like mixing acetic acid with sodium acetate? Yes. You need significant concentrations of both the intact weak acid and the conjugate base floating in the same solution. They act as a chemical shock absorber. If a metabolic process suddenly dumps a flood of H plus plus into the blood, the conjugate base component of the buffer absorbs it, forming more weak acid. And if a process dumps OHA tolerance, the weak acid component neutralizes it, forming water and more conjugate base. The pH barely moves. Let's tie this back to the patient we talked about at the very beginning of the episode. The hyperventilating patient in the ER. They are experiencing a crisis involving the most important buffer in the human the bicarbonate buffer system. The AAMC tests this system endlessly. Human blood must be maintained at a pH of approximately 7.4. Even a drop to 7.2 or a spike to 7.6 is a life threatening emergency. The primary mechanism preventing this is the equilibrium between carbon dioxide and bicarbonate. The equation is CO2 plus H2O dollars is equilibrium with carbonic acid which equals CO3 dollars which is in equilibrium with dollar plus plus bicarbonate H2O3 Let's apply Le Chatelier's principle to a pathological state, like diabetic ketoacidosis Consider a patient with DKA. Due to a lack of insulin, their body begins breaking down fats at a massive rate, producing ketone bodies. These ketone bodies are highly acidic. They dump a massive load of dollars plus dollars directly into the bloodstream. The H plus concentration spikes. The pH plummets. The enter metabolic acidosis. According to Le Chatelier's principle, the system must shift to consume that excess reactant. The massive influx of dollars plus dollars drives the equilibrium violently to the left. The H plus dollar forces itself onto the bicarbonate ion forming carbonic acid, which then rapidly dissociates into water and CO2 to lows. So the blood is suddenly flooded with excess carbon dioxide. Which triggers the immediate compensatory mechanism, the lungs. The respiratory center in the brain detects the acid and the excess CO2 to two and triggers hyper hyperventilation. The patient begins breathing deeply and rapidly to blow off the CO2 gas. Exactly. By constantly removing two two two two's from the system, Le Chatelier's principle is continuously pulling the reaction to the left, acting like a vacuum to suck up the dangerous dollars plus dollars dollars. The lungs act fast, like in a matter of minutes. But the kidneys play the long game, right? The kidneys provide the long term definitive compensation. While the lungs manipulate the CO2-two thousand side of the equilibrium, the renal tubules manipulate the O2 plus 1,000 and HCO3 palauicide. The cells lining the kidneys can actively secrete excess H plus directly into the urine to be excreted. And simultaneously, they can reabsorb new bicarbonate ions back into blood to replenish the buffer's capacity. Right. The kidneys take days to fully compensate, but they are the ultimate regulators of acid base homeostasis. Now, to quantify how well these buffers work, we rely on the Henderson Hasselbalch equation. For a weak acid buffer, the equation is PPH plus logfrig Pa. The pH equals the pKa of the acid plus the logarithm of the ratio of the conjugate base concentration to the weak acid concentration. This equation unveils a critical landmark on a titration curve: the half equivalence point. The half equivalence point occurs when you have added exactly half the volume of titrant required to reach the equivalence point. At this precise moment, exactly half of your starting weak acid has been neutralized and converted into its conjugate base. Meaning the concentration of the acid, the way catch elites, is exactly identical to the concentration of the conjugate base, eight. If you look at the Henderson Hasselbalch equation, if those two concentrations are equal, their ratio is one. The logarithm of one is zero. The entire log term vanishes. You're left with pH equals pcas. Exactly. At the half equivalence point, the pH of the solution is perfectly equal to the pKa of the weak acid. If you look at a titration curve, this point is located at the exact center of the long, flat, horizontal section of the graph. That flat section is the buffering region. The slope is nearly zero, visually demonstrating that the solution is fiercely resisting any change in pH despite the continuous addition of base. The buffering capacity is at its absolute maximum at half equivalence point. Wow. We have journeyed from the subatomic transfer of a single proton all the way to the physiology of a patient in the emergency room. It is time for our final consolidation. Let's ensure the listener doesn't fall into the traps the AMC sets. The MCAT preys on intuition that hasn't been rigorously tested. First is the pure water tract. Students are conditioned from middle school to believe that pure water is always pH seven. It is not. We talked about this with the thermodynamics of auto autosynization. Yes. Pure water is only pH seven at two ninety eight Kelvin. If a passage states an experiment is running at three fifty Kelvin, the added heat shifts the endothermic auto oxidization reaction forward. Dollars two increases. Of H plus increases. Because there are more protons, the negative log gets smaller. The pH actually drops below seven. So hot pure water has a pH of say 6.5. Does that mean it's acidic? No, it is perfectly neutral. Neutrality does not mean the pH is seven. Neutrality means the concentration of Oxus plus dollars is exactly equal to the concentration of Adderstellers. Because the reaction produced one hydroxide for every hydronium, the water remains neutral even though the pH number itself has changed. That is a brilliant trap. What's the next one? Forgetting normality during calculations. If you see a titration question involving a polyprotic acid like 2SO4D30 or a base like barium hydroxide baroHC22, you must convert their given molarities into normality before you plug them into the NAVA NBVB equation. If you use molarity, your volume calculation will be off by a factor of two or three, and I guarantee that incorrect number will be sitting there as answer choice A. And historically, science really struggled with this. The definition, it had to evolve over decades because chemists kept observing reactions that just completely defied their initial frameworks. So if we jump into time machine back to like the late nineteenth century, the first framework we'd see is the Arrhenius definition. Yes, Vontae Arrhenius, he basically just said, look at what happens in water. Keep it simple. Simple. His concept was born out of the practical reality of laboratories back then, where water was just the universal solvent for everything. So under his framework, an arrhenius acid is basically any species that when you dissolve it in water dissociates to produce an excess of hydrogen ions, right? So dollars plus dollar. Correct. And the Arrhenius base is any species that dissociates in water to produce an excess of hydroxide ions. So, oh, Lollers. Got it. So if I take like hydrogen chloride gas, drop it into a beaker of water, it splits into bolyte plus plus and all dollars. It floods the water with protons. Boom, it's an acid. Boom, exactly. And if you drop sodium hydroxide in, it splits into nalar plus and all dollars. Floods the water with hydroxide. It's a base. But there's a pretty glaring limitation there, isn't there? A massive one. It strictly, uncompromisingly requires an aqueous environment. Arrhenius, you know, he just had no vocabulary for a reaction occurring in a gas cloud or say an organic solvent like benzene. I've actually always thought of the Ardennes definition like a really strict nightclub dress code. Oh, like that. How so? Well, if you're a molecule and you don't have an aller at the very front of your formula like HDL or HNO3D3, the bouncer isn't letting you in as an acid. And if you don't have an aller at the end, like an aller or two, you aren't getting in as a base. Right. Oh, and the nightclip itself is completely underwater. If the party isn't happening in an aqueous solution, the Arrhenius Bouncer completely ignores you. That's a perfect analogy. And that restrictive nature is exactly why the definition had to expand. Because what about things that don't fit that mold? Exactly. Take ammonia and H3A3 dollars If you dissolve ammonia in water, it actually reacts to generate OA3 dollars So in water, Arrhenius would nod and say, yes, that creates a basic solution. But what if you aren't in water? Right. What if you're running a reaction where liquid ammonia is the solvent itself? The Arrhenius definition suddenly goes completely blind. Chemists needed a framework that focused on the transfer of the particles themselves, regardless of the solvent. And that's where Brinstedt and Lowry come in, Yeah. Sometime around 1923. Exactly. The Brinstedt Lowry definition, they focused on the proton itself. So an acid is a proton donor, and a base is a proton acceptor. Which is so much broader. But wait, let's clarify what we mean by a proton here for the listener. Good call. A hydrogen ion in H plus dollars is essentially just a bare proton. It's a hydrogen atom that, well, it's been stripped of its one and only electron. So it's just a concentrated point of positive charge? Yes, with no electron cloud to shield it. Which means it is violently reactive. So a acid is a molecule willing to give away that highly reactive proton. And a base is willing to take it, which completely opens up the nightclub. You don't need water anymore. Not at all. If you have two gases reacting and one molecule throws a proton to the other, you have an acid base reaction. But, it introduces something new too. The conjugate pairs. Yes. The concept of conjugate acid base pairs is absolutely central to this entire chapter. Because Bernstedt Lowery is defined by a transaction, you know, a transfer, you literally cannot have an acid without a base. You can't throw a ball if there's no one there to catch it. Right. So let's look at that ammonia molecule, an H3 3A12er, dissolved in water. Ammonia has a lone pair of electrons on its nitrogen atom. It uses those electrons to reach out and grab a proton from a water molecule. So in that transaction, ammonia accepted the proton, making it the base. And water donated the proton, making water the acid. But now look at what they become after the transaction. Okay, so the NH33ha gained a proton and a positive charge, becoming NH33ha plus dollar, the ammonium ion. And that ammonium ion now has an extra proton it could potentially give away. Therefore, NH4 dollars plus dollars is the conjugate acid of the base NH33. So the conjugate acid is basically just the base wearing its new proton hat. I love that, yes. And the water molecule, TH2O dollars having lost its proton, is now $0, the hydroxide ion. It has a negative charge and is perfectly positioned to accept a proton back. So OHA is the conjugate base of the acid water. Exactly. Now for everyone listening, there is a massive conceptual trap here that UWorld points out and the MPAT loves to exploit. It's an inclusivity trap regarding these two definitions. The rule is absolute. Every single arrhenius acid is a acid. Because if a molecule's whole identity is dissociating in water to give up, it is by definition donating a proton. Right. But the reverse is false. Not every acid is an Arrhenius acid. Because you could be donating a proton in a non aqueous solvent. Which fails the underwater night club rule. Exactly. So the Bernsted Lowry circle completely swallows the Arrhenius circle. It covers far more territory. But even that wasn't broad enough, right? Especially for organic chemistry. No, it wasn't. Gilbert Lewis realized that by obsessing over the true currency of chemical reactions. Which is electrons, you always follow the money, and in chemistry, the money is the negative charge. Exactly. The Lieu's definition flicks the perspective. An acid is an electron pair acceptor, and a base is electron pair donor. So we're no longer tracking the positive nucleus, we're tracking the negative lone pair. Right. A Lewis base has an unshared pair of electrons it wants to share. A Lewis acy has vacant orbital, or a significant positive charge, that eagerly wants to accept those electrons. This is basically a bridge straight into organic chemistry. If you're reviewing organic reactions, you already know these terms. You do. Lewis acids are electrophiles. They are electron deficient, so they love electrons. Lewis bases are nucleophiles. They are electron rich, so they love finding a positive nucleus to attack. Can we visualize a classic organic reaction to make this concrete? Like, boron trifluoride reacting with ammonia. Perfect example. So boron trifluoride, BF3O2 there. Boron is in group three of the periodic table. It forms three subal bonds with three fluorine atoms. Which gives boron a share of only six valence electrons. Right. It does not have a complete octet. It possesses a completely empty orbital just sitting there wide open. It is a profound electrophile. Meanwhile, ammonia, NH three forty three, has that nitrogen atom with a big juicy lone pair of electrons just taking up space. So when they collide, the nitrogen atom donates both of those electrons straight into boron's empty orbital. They form a bond, but notice what didn't happen. No protons were transferred? None. A Brinsted Lowry observer would look at that and say absolutely nothing occurred. But the Lewis definition recognizes this as a fundamental acid base neutralization. And that resulting bond is called a coordinate covalent bond, right? Yeah. Because both shared electrons came from only one of the participating atoms. Now is this just an organic chemistry quirk? Or does this electron pair acceptance actually show up in biology? Oh, it is the foundation of human respiration. Wait, really? How? Look at hemoglobin in your red blood cells. At the center of the heme group is an iron cation, $5.02 plus dollars. That iron ion does not have any protons to donate. It completely fails the test. But it has emptied orbitals. Yes. So when you inhale, an oxygen molecule acting as a Lewis base donates an electron pair into the iron's empty orbital. The iron acts as a Lewis acid. Oh wow, that coordinate covalent bond is literally how oxygen is transported through your arteries. It's incredible, right? The iron is an acid, the oxygen is a base. So the Lewis definition is the ultimate, most inclusive circle. Every Arrhenius acid and every Brunstadt Lowery acid is also a Lewis acid, but it doesn't work the other way around. Spot on. Now before we move on to the next section, we have to address the chameleons, amphoteric and amphorrotic species. Ones that can't make up their minds. Basically. An amphoteric species can act as either an acid or a base depending entirely on the environment it finds itself in. So if you place it in a highly basic solution, it acts like an acid. If you place it in a highly acidic solution, it acts like a base. Right, and the word amphoproduct specifically ties back to the proton transfer. It means the molecule can either gain a proton or lose a proton. Water is the classic example of that. It can become 3H3 dollars plus already a dollar. But what about in the human body? What's the MCAT's favorite biological chameleon? Without a doubt, amino acids. The AAMC utilizes amino acids constantly to test this concept because their entire structure is fundamentally amphoteric. Because every amino acid has a carboxylic acid group, which is acidic and can donate a proton. And every amino acid possesses an amino group which is inherently basic and can accept a proton. I want to really dig into the how of this. Because students just memorize the word 'swaterian' without understanding the mechanism of why it happens in the blood. Yeah, let's break down the mechanism. It's all about the relationship between the pKa of the specific functional group and the pH of the surrounding environment. Okay, so the carboxylic acid group typically has a pKa around two, right. And the amino group typically has a pKa around nine. Let's say this amino acid is just floating along in human blood, which has a pH of 7.4. Okay, at a pH of 7.4, the environment is far more basic than the pKa of the carboxylic acid group, right? Because 7.4 is greater than two. Yeah. Because the environment is relatively basic, it strips the proton away from the carboxylic acid. The group becomes deprotonated, holding a negative charge a little of the lever. But what about the amino group on the exact same molecule? Well, the pH of the blood, 7.4, is more acidic than the pK of the amino group, because 7.4 is less than nine. Ah, so because the environment is relatively acidic compared to that specific group, the amino group retains its proton. Exactly. It stays protonated, holding a positive charge NH3 dollars plus dollars. So in the exact same bloodstream, the molecule has a negative end and a positive end. That is a suturium. The molecule is internally neutralized but it's highly polar. And you know if that amino acid travels to the stomach where the pH drops to like 1.5 That's a massive flood of environmental protons. Huge. It will force the car carboxyl group to pick its proton back up. The molecule suddenly shifts to having an overall positive charge. That chameleon like ability to shift states based on pH. That's literally how proteins fold and enzymes bind, isn't it? It is the driving force of biochemistry. Which perfectly transitions us into Section 10.2: Properties. Because if an acid's identity is based on throwing away a proton, we need to know what happens when we put it in water an environment full of molecules waiting to catch it. Right, how do they behave when left alone, and how do we measure them? First, we have to examine the baseline state of water itself. We tend to think of pure water as static, just, you know, intact, 20 work two hour molecules sitting peacefully. But it's actually incredibly dynamic. Extremely dynamic. The molecules are constantly vibrating, moving, colliding. And occasionally, two water molecules collide with the perfect trajectory and enough kinetic energy that one molecule violently rips a proton away from the other. So the water is acting as an acid in a base with itself. Yes, this is the auto ionization of water. The equation is 2H2O plus H2O in equilibrium with three 3O plus Hg plus in OH hydroxide. One bond breaks another forms. And because it's reversible, it establishes an equilibrium. Governed by the water dissociation constant. Which at standard room temperature two ninety eight Kelvin is exactly $10.14 dollars Grant, we calculate dollars by multiplying the concentration of the products 3H3 dollars plus plus tapascivers. But here is a fundamental thermodynamic rule the MCAT will test: $2 is only affected by changes in temperature. I really want the listener to grasp why. If I take a beaker of water and I compress it with massive pressure or dump a bucket of salt into it, Tdal's does not budge. Why only temperature? Because the autoionization of water involves breaking a covalent bond between oxygen and hydrogen, Breaking bonds always requires an input of energy. So the forward reaction is endothermic. Exactly. Heat is essentially a reactant. So according to Le Chatelier's principle, if you add a reactant, system shifts to the right to consume it. If you raise the temperature, you're adding heat. The system shifts right, breaking more water molecules apart. Creating more hydronium and hydroxide. Which means the product concentrations go up, so the equilibrium constant itself, codeine dollars, physically increases at higher temperatures. It's not ten fourteen anymore. Precisely. But returning to a standard two ninety eight Kelvin, tailey is locked at $10.14 dollars This means three c. Wallers plus nollers and waller riddollers are playing a zero sum game. If you add an external acid driving the hydronium up, the hydroxide must proportionally decrease so their product stays exactly $10.14 dollars They're perfectly inversely proportional. Now expressing these concentrations in scientific notation, like ten, five, 11, or ten, eleven solomarolar, is incredibly tedious for calculations, which is why the pH scale was invented. Right, because nobody wants to write out all those zeros. Exactly. In chemistry, the lowercase letter p is a mathematical operator. It just means take the negative base 10 logarithm of. So PaPO is simply the negative log of the hydrogen ion concentration and PUAK is the negative log of the hydroxide ion concentration. Yes. So if pure water at two ninety eight Kelvin has a hydrogen ion concentration of $10 in molar, the negative log of $10 is just seven, so the pH is seven. And the pH is also seven, which gives us that super useful formula. PH plus pOH is 14.4. If you know one, you instantly know the other. But the MCA rarely gives you a perfect power of 10. You aren't going to get $10 you'll get something messy. Like $4 times $10.05 a molar. And you don't have a calculator. I know students panic at logs without a calculator. They do, but do not panic. The AAMC doesn't expect you to be a human calculator. They expect you to approximate. This is where the Kaplan math shortcut, GenDemollers, is invaluable. Let's walk through that slowly so people can follow the math mentally. Okay. If your concentration is written in scientific notation as one molar times 10 dol, the approximate pH is simply exponent dollar minus $0 Let's use that $4 times 10 phi example. Our exponent dol is five. Our coefficient is four. Right. So you take the not value, which is five, and you subtract $0.4 which is $0.04 So $5.4 soar equals $4.6 The approximate pH is 4.66 And the true mathematical value is about 4.4. Your approximation of 4.6 is easily close enough to select the correct multiple choice answer. The options will be spaced out like 3.4, 4.4, 5.4. This shortcut will guide you flawlessly. That is such a time saver. So once we can measure the pH, we can categorize these molecules based on how aggressively they impact that pH, meaning strong versus weak acids and bases. Right, and we need to clarify that strong and weak here has nothing to do with how dangerous or corrosive the chemical is. It refers entirely to the degree of dissociation. So a strong acid completely, 100% dissociates it into its ions and water. Yes. If you carefully measure out exactly one mole of hydrogen chloride gas and dissolve it into a liter of water, when you look closely at that solution you will find zero intact light selenema molecules. None. Just one mole of water bills plus dollars one mole of the dollars, dollars. The reaction goes to absolute completion. Because of this, calculating the pH of a strong acid is direct. The molarity of the acid equals the molarity of the hydrogen ions. A 0.1 molar solution of YHCSL yields a 0.1 molar concentration of protons. You take the negative log and you have your pH. But the weak acids are trickier. Because they only partially dissociate. If you drop one mole of acetic acid into water, perhaps only 1% actually give up their proton, the other 99% remain intact. Establishing an equilibrium state, which means we have to use an equilibrium constant. Collis for acids, cattle for bases for bases. Right. The formula for collar is the concentration of the products over the reactants. Plus times the conjugate base, all dollars, all divided by the concentration of the intact acid cells. So a smaller collar means a weaker acid. A tiny collar means the numerator is tiny, very little dissociation happened. The intact molecule is holding on to that proton for dear life. Exactly. And now we must connect the strength of an acid to the stability of its conjugate base. This is, hands down, the single most important conceptual bridge between general chemistry and organic chemistry on the MCAT. Let's paint a picture here. I always think of an acid molecule like a parent holding a squirming, screaming toddler. Okay, the toddler is the proton. Yes. The parent desperately wants to put the toddler down. But the parent will only let go if the environment they're placing the toddler into, the resulting state of the molecule, the conjugate base, is incredibly stable and safe. That analogy maps perfectly to the thermodynamics. An acid's willingness to donate a proton is entirely dictated by how thermodynamically stable the resulting conjugate base is. Because if the conjugate base is highly stable, it represents a low energy state, and the universe always favors moving toward lower energy. The acid will gladly jettison the proton to reach that stable state. But what actually makes a conjugate base stable at the atomic level? You have to look at periodic trends and molecular structure. Let's look at why HClO is a strong acid. When it loses its proton, it becomes chloride ion. Pizziol, why is the chloride dollars so stable holding a negative charge? Well first, chlorine is highly electronegative, so its nucleus pulls strongly on electrons. Yes, and second, more importantly, the chlorine atom has a large atomic radius. It has a massive electron cloud. Exactly. That single extra electron isn't confined to a tiny space. Its negative charge is diffused and spread out over a large volume. This dramatically reduces electrostatic repulsion within the ion. So because the CLR dollars is so stable and comfortable with that extra electron, it has absolutely no desire to grab a proton back. It is inert. It is an incredibly weak base. Which brings us to the cardinal rule. Strong acids produce extremely weak inert conjugate bases. Each Clr produces the inert taxane. What about sulfuric acid? Th2SO42ton, that's a strong acid too. It is, but its stability comes from a different mechanism, resonance. Let's visualize that resonance. Because this is where organic chemistry concepts bleed right into gen chem. When CHSO4 Torr loses a proton, it becomes the hydrogen sulfate ion (HSO4 dollars ). That negative charge is initially left on a single oxygen atom. But oxygen is electronegative, and the adjacent sulfur is double bonded to other oxygens. The pi electrons can easily shift. So the negative charge doesn't just sit isolated on one atom, it delocalizes. Right. The electron cloud smears itself across multiple oxygen atoms in the molecule. By spreading the charge out, the electrostatic repulsion is minimized. The molecule achieves immense thermodynamic stability. Because the HSO4 taller tetra four adalars is so deeply stabilized by resonance, the original tetra four adalar interfer four molecule is incredibly eager to lose the proton, making it a very strong acid. So if you are staring at an MCAT question asking you to rank the acidity of four different molecules. You don't even look at the proton. You mentally rip the proton off of every single choice, draw a conjugate base and ask yourself, which of these four bases is most stable? Exactly. Look for resonance, look for electronegative atoms pulling charge via induction, look for large atomic radii. The most stable base came from the strongest acid. Phenomenal strategy. But you must also remember the corollary: weak acids produce weak conjugate bases. A common trap is assuming a weak acid produces a strong conjugate base. It does not. Like acetic acid producing the acetate ion. Acetate is a weak base stronger than the inert chloride ion, but definitely not a strong base like sodium hydroxide. Correct. Now, when you mix these acids and bases together, they neutralize. You get salt formation. HCl lever reacting with no feature undergoes a double displacement reaction to produce NOTCL, table salt, and water. And whether that resulting salt solution is acidic, basic, or neutral depends entirely on the relative strengths of the acid and base you mixed. Yes, which we will explore mathematically when we discuss titrations. But before we get there, section 10.3 introduces a complication: polyvalence and normality. Right, because up until now, we've basically talked about monoprotic acids one molecule, one proton to give. But what happens when a molecule is holding multiple screaming toddlers? That's a polyprotic or polyvalent acid. It has multiple acidic protons. Sulfuric acid 3D2SO4 sores is diprotic. Phosphoric acid 3D3PO4 is tri product. But they don't just dump all their protons at once. It happens sequentially, step by step. And each step is harder than the last. Think about the thermodynamics. The first proton leaving H2SO4 alerting Loller is relatively easy. It's leaving a neutral molecule. That first dissociation is a strong acid process, it goes to completion. Leaving the HSO4 or elastradiololr, which now has a negative charge. And it still has a proton it can lose. But that second proton is a positive charge trying to tear itself away from an already negatively charged molecule. Electrostatic attraction heavily resists this separation. It requires significantly more energy, so the second dissociation doesn't go to completion. No, it establishes a weak acid equilibrium. The KIq for the second proton is drastically smaller than the KI for the first. Because these molecules deliver multiple protons, we cannot simply rely on the molarity of the whole molecule to tell us the concentration of the reactive protons. We need a new measurement system. Which brings us to equivalence and normality. An equivalent is simply one mole of the specific reactive species we care about. In this chapter, the species we care about is the proton, dollar hearts plus dollar, or the hydroxide ion And normality, denoted by a capital N, is just the concentration of those equivalents. Equivalents per liter. I honestly think the bicycle shop analogy is the best way to lock this in. Let's hear it. Let's say you own a bicycle shop. Molarity is how you count whole, intact bicycles. If you have one mole of bicycles, your molarity is one molar. But when we do an acid based reaction, we don't care about the frame of the bicycle. The chemical reaction only interacts with the wheels. The wheels are the protons. Every single bicycle has two wheels. So if your shop has a one molar concentration of whole bicycles, you actually have a two normal concentration of wheels. You have twice as many reactive units as you have whole molecules. That translates perfectly to chemistry. If you have a two molar solution of 2SUSO4O4, what is the normality of the hydrogen ions? Well, H2SO4O4 is our bicycle. It is two protons, two wheels. So I take my two moles of bicycles per liter and multiply it by two wheels per bicycle. That gives me a four normal, or 4N solution of hydrogen ions. Perfect. Molarity times the number of equivalents per molecule equals normality. You also need to understand how to calculate the gram equivalent weight. Which answers the question: How heavy is the chunk of the bicycle that gives you exactly one wheel? To calculate it, you divide the molar mass of the entire compound by the number of equivalents it produces. The molar mass of H2SO4-four 33 is roughly 98 grams per mole. Because one mole yields two moles of protons, you divide 98 by two. 49 grams. So if I walk into the lab and weigh out exactly 49 grams of sulfuric acid, I know with absolute certainty that I am holding exactly one mole of reactive protons. Understanding normality is absolutely non negotiable for section 10.4 because when you begin mixing acids and bases in the laboratory or in the human body, they do not react mole for mole, they react equivalent for equivalent. One wheel matches with one axle. Which brings us to the ultimate application of all this theory: titration and buffers. The laboratory setup for a general titration is something you likely performed in undergrad. You have an Erlenmeyer flask sitting on the bench. Inside is a solution of unknown concentration. We call this the titrant. And suspended above the flask is a long glass burette filled with the solution of a completely known standardized concentration. That's the titrant. You open the stopcock on the burette and you slowly, drop by drop, add the titrant into the flask until the reaction is complete. You are waiting for the equivalence point. The mathematical climax of the titration. Exactly. The equivalence point is the exact moment when the number of acid equivalents present in the flask perfectly equals the number of base equivalents you have added from the burette. And the equation for this is beautiful in its simplicity. One dal x vy nen dal x v b day. The normality of the acid x its volume equals the normality of the base x its volume. A vital testing strategy for the MCAT here. The volumes in this equation do not strictly need to be in liters. As long as you use the exact same volume units on both sides of the equation, say milliliters on the left and milliliters on the right, the math will work out perfectly. That saves precious seconds by avoiding unnecessary metric conversions. It does. Now, when you perform a titration, you typically plot a curve of the pH as you add the volume of titrant. The shape of that curve, and specifically the location of the equivalence point on the y axis, tells you exactly what kind of acid and base are reacting. Let's start with a strong acid reacting with a strong base. Both molecules dissociate 100%. Because of this, they neutralize each other completely, forming water and a neutral salt. The equivalence point will be located at exactly pH seven. But the MCAT rarely makes it that easy. They love testing a weak acid being titrated by a strong base. In this scenario, the equivalence point will always be basic. It will be located at a pH greater than seven. To understand why, you must understand the molecular reality at the equivalence point. Okay, let's say you're titrating weak acetic acid with strong sodium hydroxide. At the exact equivalence point, all the acetic acid has reacted with all the added sodium hydroxide. What is actually floating in the flask at that exact moment? Well, you formed a salt. So you have water, sodium ions, and acetate ions. Exactly. The sodium ion is the conjugate acid of a strong base, no Y Car O A, so it is inert. But the acetate ion is the conjugate base of a weak acid. It is a weak base itself. So floating in the water is a weak base. And it reacts with the water. Yes, a process called hydrolysis. It pulls a proton off H. $2 generates a small amount of ood deserts. Because it generated hydroxide, the pH of the solution at the equivalence point is basic, Will be greater than seven, maybe around pH 8.5 or nine. You can also use a tug of war analogy. Imagine pH seven is the centerline of a tug of war rope. On one side you have your weak acid, on the other your strong base. They are pulling against each other to neutralize. Because the strong base is, by definition, stronger, it pulls the center of the reaction, the equivalence point across the centerline and into its own basic territory. So if we reverse it and titrate a weak base with a strong acid, the strong acid wins the tug of war. The equivalence point gets dragged down below pH seven into acidic territory. Exactly. Now, how do we actually know we've reached the equivalence point during the experiment? A digital pH meter will show the curve, but visually we rely on chemical indicators. Indicators are fascinating. I always wondered how a liquid just suddenly changes color from one drop to the next. Indicators are themselves weak organic acids or bases. They have a massive, complex structural an extended system of alternating double and single bonds, known as a conjugated pi system. And that conjugated system dictates what wavelength of light the molecule absorbs. Right. And because it's a weak acid, it can gain or lose a proton. Adding or removing a single proton from that massive molecule slightly alters the electronic structure of the entire conjugated system. That tiny structural shift is enough to change the wavelength of light it absorbs. So the protonated form might appear yellow and the deprotonated form might appear blue. The crucial rule for the EMSIKAT is how to pick the right indicator. You have a whole shelf of them. How do you choose? You match the PK of the indicator to the expected pH at the equivalence point, right? You must. The indicator changes color. It shifts from the protonated to the deprotonated state when the environmental pH crosses its specific pK value. If you are titrating a weak acid with a strong base and you calculate the equivalence point will be a pH nine, you must select an indicator with a pK around nine. Exactly. And this introduces a fundamental vocabulary distinction that students frequently conflate: the equivalence point versus the end point. They sound similar, but they aren't the same. Not at all. The equivalence point is the mathematical stoichiometric reality of the reaction. It is the exact molecular moment. The equivalents perfectly match. And the end point? The end point is the human visual observation. It is the moment the indicator actually changes color and the researcher turns the stopcock to halt the titration. If you've chosen the correct indicator, the visible endpoint occurs just a tiny fraction of a drop after the mathematical equivalence point. So they represent roughly the same volume, but conceptually they're vastly different things. Equivalence is the invisible math. Endpoint is the visible color change. Exactly. Okay, let's connect this laboratory technique back to human physiology because this is where the MCAT truly shines. We need to talk about buffers. A buffer is an aqueous solution that actively resists large fluctuations in pH when you add small amounts of strong acid or strong base to it. And to construct a buffer, you need a specific mixture. A weak acid combined with its conjugate salt, or a weak base combined with its conjugate salt? Like mixing acetic acid with sodium acetate? Yes. You need significant concentrations of both the intact weak acid and the conjugate base floating in the same solution. They act as a chemical shock absorber. If a metabolic process suddenly dumps a flood of H plus plus into the blood, the conjugate base component of the buffer absorbs it, forming more weak acid. And if a process dumps OHA tolerance, the weak acid component neutralizes it, forming water and more conjugate base. The pH barely moves. Let's tie this back to the patient we talked about at the very beginning of the episode. The hyperventilating patient in the ER. They are experiencing a crisis involving the most important buffer in the human the bicarbonate buffer system. The AAMC tests this system endlessly. Human blood must be maintained at a pH of approximately 7.4. Even a drop to 7.2 or a spike to 7.6 is a life threatening emergency. The primary mechanism preventing this is the equilibrium between carbon dioxide and bicarbonate. The equation is CO2 plus H2O dollars is equilibrium with carbonic acid which equals CO3 dollars which is in equilibrium with dollar plus plus bicarbonate H2O3 Let's apply Le Chatelier's principle to a pathological state, like diabetic ketoacidosis Consider a patient with DKA. Due to a lack of insulin, their body begins breaking down fats at a massive rate, producing ketone bodies. These ketone bodies are highly acidic. They dump a massive load of dollars plus dollars directly into the bloodstream. The H plus concentration spikes. The pH plummets. The enter metabolic acidosis. According to Le Chatelier's principle, the system must shift to consume that excess reactant. The massive influx of dollars plus dollars drives the equilibrium violently to the left. The H plus dollar forces itself onto the bicarbonate ion forming carbonic acid, which then rapidly dissociates into water and CO2 to lows. So the blood is suddenly flooded with excess carbon dioxide. Which triggers the immediate compensatory mechanism, the lungs. The respiratory center in the brain detects the acid and the excess CO2 to two and triggers hyper hyperventilation. The patient begins breathing deeply and rapidly to blow off the CO2 gas. Exactly. By constantly removing two two two two's from the system, Le Chatelier's principle is continuously pulling the reaction to the left, acting like a vacuum to suck up the dangerous dollars plus dollars dollars. The lungs act fast, like in a matter of minutes. But the kidneys play the long game, right? The kidneys provide the long term definitive compensation. While the lungs manipulate the CO2-two thousand side of the equilibrium, the renal tubules manipulate the O2 plus 1,000 and HCO3 palauicide. The cells lining the kidneys can actively secrete excess H plus directly into the urine to be excreted. And simultaneously, they can reabsorb new bicarbonate ions back into blood to replenish the buffer's capacity. Right. The kidneys take days to fully compensate, but they are the ultimate regulators of acid base homeostasis. Now, to quantify how well these buffers work, we rely on the Henderson Hasselbalch equation. For a weak acid buffer, the equation is PPH plus logfrig Pa. The pH equals the pKa of the acid plus the logarithm of the ratio of the conjugate base concentration to the weak acid concentration. This equation unveils a critical landmark on a titration curve: the half equivalence point. The half equivalence point occurs when you have added exactly half the volume of titrant required to reach the equivalence point. At this precise moment, exactly half of your starting weak acid has been neutralized and converted into its conjugate base. Meaning the concentration of the acid, the way catch elites, is exactly identical to the concentration of the conjugate base, eight. If you look at the Henderson Hasselbalch equation, if those two concentrations are equal, their ratio is one. The logarithm of one is zero. The entire log term vanishes. You're left with pH equals pcas. Exactly. At the half equivalence point, the pH of the solution is perfectly equal to the pKa of the weak acid. If you look at a titration curve, this point is located at the exact center of the long, flat, horizontal section of the graph. That flat section is the buffering region. The slope is nearly zero, visually demonstrating that the solution is fiercely resisting any change in pH despite the continuous addition of base. The buffering capacity is at its absolute maximum at half equivalence point. Wow. We have journeyed from the subatomic transfer of a single proton all the way to the physiology of a patient in the emergency room. It is time for our final consolidation. Let's ensure the listener doesn't fall into the traps the AMC sets. The MCAT preys on intuition that hasn't been rigorously tested. First is the pure water tract. Students are conditioned from middle school to believe that pure water is always pH seven. It is not. We talked about this with the thermodynamics of auto autosynization. Yes. Pure water is only pH seven at two ninety eight Kelvin. If a passage states an experiment is running at three fifty Kelvin, the added heat shifts the endothermic auto oxidization reaction forward. Dollars two increases. Of H plus increases. Because there are more protons, the negative log gets smaller. The pH actually drops below seven. So hot pure water has a pH of say 6.5. Does that mean it's acidic? No, it is perfectly neutral. Neutrality does not mean the pH is seven. Neutrality means the concentration of Oxus plus dollars is exactly equal to the concentration of Adderstellers. Because the reaction produced one hydroxide for every hydronium, the water remains neutral even though the pH number itself has changed. That is a brilliant trap. What's the next one? Forgetting normality during calculations.
