MCAT General Chemistry Chapter 12 Electrochemistry. Right now, literally as you listen to this, your brain is relying on, what, trillions of microscopic water towers just to process my work. Your very ability to comprehend this sentence is governed by an invisible, relentless flow of ions across the membranes of your neurons. So we are diving into nature's invisible engine. And as the grand finale of our general chemistry journey, this deep dive covers about 6% of the MCAT Gen Chem questions. We're going to apply everything we've learned about redox reactions to real world electrochemical cells. Exactly. It is the ultimate bridge between the abstract and the physical. I mean, up until this point in your study of general chemistry, oxidation reduction reactions, the transfer of electrons from one species to another, they've largely been treated as abstract concepts occurring in a void. Yeah, like we track the numbers, we balance the equations, but we rarely discuss the physical container in which those reactions actually happen. Right. But today, we put those reactions into physical devices. We solve the biological of how a neuron maintains a resting potential. We solve the physical problem of how a battery powers a circuit. And we solve the chemical problem of how to force an entirely non spontaneous reaction to occur against its own thermodynamic will. So we're basically talking about capturing work. And usually when we hear the word work in a physics or chemistry context, we picture like a piston compressing a gas or someone pushing a heavy box up a ramp. Sure, mechanical work. Yeah, pressure, volume, mechanical displacement. But in living systems, and in a battery for that matter, the volume doesn't really change. The pressure is constant. So we are doing a completely different kind of work here. We are doing electrical work. And to really understand this, we need to frame it through the first law of thermodynamics, dictates that the change in the internal energy of a system is equal to the heat transferred minus the work done by the system. If we have a chemical reaction where one molecule simply bumps into another molecule in a beaker and transfers an electron, the energy of that transfer is almost entirely lost to the surrounding environment as heat. Oh, because it's chaotic little collision. Exactly. It is a highly localized chaotic event. And we cannot harness heat efficiently to run a smartphone or fire a muscle fiber. So the goal is to stop them from bumping into each other directly. We have to separate the chemical that wants to give away the electron from the chemical that wants to take it. That physical separation is the foundational premise of an electrochemical cell. By creating a physical barrier between the oxidation half reaction and the reduction half reaction, we create a spatial gradient. The electrons can't just jump across the gap. Right, they're stuck. Yeah, so we build up potential energy. The species that wants to lose electrons is trapped on one side, and the species that wants to gain them is trapped on the other. It totally brings to mind a water tower. I mean, pumping water up from the ground into a massive tank suspended 100 feet in the air, that requires an input of energy. You're fighting gravity. You are. But once that water is trapped up there, you've separated the mass from the ground, you've established a tremendous amount of potential energy. The water desperately wants to fall, but it can't because of the physical barrier of the tank. And how do you extract work from that suspended water? Well, you detach a pipe and open a valve. You provide a specific restricted pathway for the water to fall. And as it rushes down that pipe, you put a turbine in its path. The falling water spins the turbine and boom, you're generating power. Which is the perfect framework. The electrochemical cell operates on that exact thermodynamic principle. The separation of the chemical species is the water trapped in the tower. The potential energy is the desire of the electrons to move from the species being oxidized to the species being reduced. So the wire is the pipe. Exactly. When we connect those two separated compartments with a conductive wire, we are opening the valve. We are providing a specific pathway for the charge to flow. And that flow of charge is an electric current. We can put our own turbines in its path a light bulb, a motor, or, you know, a cellular protein. And if the water is flowing down the tower naturally, that implies a spontaneous process. In thermodynamic terms, the change in Gibbs free energy delta G is negative. Correct. A negative delta G means the system can perform work on its surroundings as it moves toward equilibrium. But, the water tower analogy holds up in the reverse direction as well. What if you have an empty water tower and you want to fill it? I mean, water isn't going to flow up the pipe on its own. You have to plug in a mechanical pump, draw electricity from the grid and force the water up against gravity. So that is a non spontaneous process. Diligi is positive. Right. And every electrochemical cell we analyze falls into one of these two thermodynamic categories. It is either the spontaneous release of energy, so water flowing down, or the forced input of energy reaction backward pumping water up. Makes sense. To analyze these devices, we have to establish the universal anatomy that applies to all of them. So, Kaplan introduces three fundamental types of electrochemical cells: galvanic cells, electrolytic cells, and concentration cells. Let's isolate the anatomy first before we even get into the functional differences. Every single one of these cells contains electrodes. They do. Electrodes are solid, conductive materials. They are usually strips of metal or sometimes graphite that are submerged in an electrolyte solution. They serve as the physical docking stations where the electron transfer actually takes place. Okay, so docking stations. Yeah. And the electrode where oxidation occurs is called the anode. The electrode where reduction occurs is called the cathode. This is a rigid universal rule. So anode equals oxidation. Cathode equals reduction. Wait, anode and oxidation both start with vowels. Cathode and reduction both start with consonants. And ox red cat, that has to be the standard mnemonic for this. It is. An Ox in Red Cat is the most reliable memory tool you can bring into test day. Oxidation is always the loss of electrons. That means the anode is the physical site where electrons are being stripped away from a chemical species. Once those electrons are liberated at the anode, they push into the conductive wire and travel over to the cathode. At the cathode, those electrons are handed off to a different chemical species, which undergoes reduction of the gain of electrons. Electrons flow from the anode to the cathode, A to C. Also alphabetical, which is nice. The physical electrons absolutely travel from the anode to the cathode. But here we must introduce efact that causes endless frustration. Oh no. Yeah. If a physics passage asks you for the direction of the electrical current, which is denoted by the letter I, the current flows from the cathode to the anode. Wait, I have to challenge this convention. If the electrons are the physical particles moving through the wire from A to C, why would we mathematically define the current as moving in the exact opposite direction? That seems deliberately designed to confuse anyone trying to understand the actual mechanics. I know, I know. It is entirely a product of history, specifically Benjamin Franklin's early theories on electricity. Ben Franklin? Seriously? In the eighteenth century, long before the discovery of the electron, scientists observed the phenomenon of electrical charge. Franklin hypothesized that electricity was a single, invisible fluid present in all matter. He arbitrarily decided that an object accumulating this fluid was positively charged, and an object losing it was negatively charged. Therefore, he defined the flow of electricity as moving from a surplus of positive charge to a deficit of positive charge. He defined current as the flow of positive charge. But he was guessing. He didn't know what the moving particle actually was. He was completely guessing. And his guess became the foundational convention for all of electromagnetism. A century later, J. J. Thompson discovered the electron and proved that in a solid metal wire, the positive charges, the protons, are locked in place in the atomic lattice. They don't move at all. Right, because if the protons moved, the metal would disintegrate. Exactly. The only things moving are the negatively charged electrons and they move from the negative surplus to the positive deficit. So the physical reality was exactly backward from Franklin's convention. Yes. But by the time the electron was discovered, entire fields of physics and engineering had already been built on Franklin's mathematics. Maxwell's equations, circuit diagrams, the definitions of voltage and resistance all of it assumed a positive current. That is wild. Rather than rewrite every textbook and mathematical proof in existence, the scientific community just accepted the contradiction. We track the flow of theoretical positive charge. So for the sake of the exam, the flow of electrons and the flow of current are equal in magnitude. If a wire carries a flow of one mole of electrons per second to the right, the current I is mathematically treated as an equivalent flow of positive charge to the left. Electrons anode to cathode current cathode to anode. Keep those isolated in your mind, and you won't fall into the trap. Let's look at how this anatomy functions in a spontaneous system. The galvanic cell, also known as a voltaic cell. The classic historical model is the Daniel cell. This is the water tower with the open valve. Delta G is negative. We are generating energy. What does a Daniel cell actually look like on a physical workbench? Two separate glass beakers. In the left beaker, you place a solid strip of zinc metal you fill that beaker with an aqueous solution of zinc sulfate. The zinc sulfate completely dissolves into positively charged zinc ions and negatively charged sulfate ions floating in the water. So just a zinc stick sitting in a zinc ion bath. Exactly. Now in the right beaker, you place a solid strip of copper metal, this is our cathode. You fill this beeper with an aqueous solution of copper sulfate, so copper metal sitting in a copper ion bath. Finally, you connect the zinc metal to that copper metal with a copper wire. But nothing happens yet, right? Because they are just two isolated beakers. Well, we need one more component, but let's trace the thermodynamics first to understand why the reaction starts. Zinc, chemically speaking, has a lower electron affinity than copper. Copper wants electrons more than zinc does. It's pulling harder. Yes. So at the surface of the zinc anode, neutral zinc atoms begin to undergo oxidation. A zinc atom gives up two electrons into the metal strip. Because it lost two negative charges, it becomes a xenon two plus supercation. And a solid metal needs neutral atoms to hold its crystalline structure together. Once it becomes an ion, it dissolves. Exactly right. The xenon two plus ion falls off the solid strip and dissolves into the surrounding aqueous solution. The solid zinc anode literally slowly disintegrates, losing physical mass. Meanwhile, those two liberated electrons push their way up the electrode into the wire and travel across to the copper cathode. They arrive at the copper strip in the right beaker, but the solid copper atoms in the strip already have all their electrons. They don't need more. The solid copper atoms don't, no. But remember, what is floating in the water around that strip? Aqueous Cu2 plus cations from the dissolved copper sulfate. Those Cu2 plus ions bump into the surface of the cathode. And they want electrons. Very much so. They violently pull those two newly arrived electrons off the metal. By gaining two electrons, the CO2 plus ion undergoes reduction and becomes a neutral solid atom of copper. And because it's now solid, it physically attaches to the electrode. Yes, it plates onto the surface. The copper cathode physically grows and gains mass over time. We have electrons flowing, zinc dissolving and copper plating. We are doing electrical work. That's awesome. But, as we mentioned, if this is all we have, two beakers and a wire, the battery will die almost the instant it begins. Let's dig into the physical mechanism of why it dies. Yeah. We are ripping neutral zinc atoms apart, sending the electrons away, and jumping positive Zn2 plus ions into the left beaker. Very quickly, that left beaker is going to become highly concentrated with positive charge. And in the right beaker, the opposite crisis is occurring. We are pulling positive Cu2 plus ions out of the water to turn them into solid metal. But the negative sulfate ions that were originally paired with them are left behind. The right beaker rapidly develops a massive net negative charge. Nature abhors a localized charge imbalance. It does. The physics of the situation forcefully halts the chemistry. If the left beaker becomes incredibly positively charged, those positive ions will physically pull back on the negative electrons, preventing them from traveling up the wire. And if the right beaker becomes incredibly negatively charged, it will physically repel incoming negative electrons. The counter voltage generated by the solutions perfectly opposes the voltage of the battery, and the electron flow drops to zero. This is where the pressure release valve is required. We need a way to dissipate that localized charge buildup without mixing the two reactive solutions together. We need a salt bridge? Yes, the salt bridge. It's typically an inverted U shaped glass tube that dips into both beakers. Inside the tube is an inert electrolyte gel, usually containing something like potassium chloride, so KCl, or maybe ammonium nitrate. But it has to be inert. It must be inert, meaning the potassium and the chloride will not react chemically with zinc, copper, or sulfate. They are strictly there for crowd control. So how do they neutralize the beakers? Well, as the zinc anode oxidizes and dumps positive xenon two plus into the left beaker, the local positive charge physically attracts the negatively charged chloride ions from the salt bridge. The Cl anions migrate out of the gel and drop into the anode beaker, perfectly neutralizing the newly formed Zn2 plus ions. And in the cathode beaker, where the positive copper ions are being depleted and leaving behind a swamp of negative sulfate, the positively charged potassium ions from the salt bridge migrate out and drop into the solution, neutralizing the sulfate. Let's lock in the physical rule you just described. The negatively charged chloride ions, the anions, flow to the anode beaker. The positively charged potassium ions? The cations flow to the cathode beaker. Anions to anode. Cations to cathode. A to AC to C. That mechanism is absolute. Regardless of the type of cell, the ions in the salt bridge or the internal solution will always migrate this way. Anions to anode. Cations to cathode. By constantly neutralizing the solutions, the salt bridge prevents the counter voltage from forming, allowing the electrons to continue flowing through the wire until the zinc anode is completely dissolved or the copper ions in the cathode beaker are completely depleted. That paints a perfectly clear picture of the galvanic cell. The chemicals are the active participants. But, bringing in a point from our secondary source material, UWorld, what if the reaction we want to capture doesn't involve solid metals? What if we want to build a battery using hydrogen gas and oxygen gas? You can't attach a wire to a cloud of gas. No, you definitely can't. And that requires the use of inert electrodes. The zinc and copper we just discussed are active electrodes they physically participate in the redox reaction, losing and gaining mass. But if your reactants are gases, or both the oxidized and reduced forms of a species remain dissolved in solutions, say converting aqueous iron to aqueous iron two, you still need a solid one way for the electrons to enter or exit the wire. You need a neutral middleman. Exactly. We use unreactive, highly conductive materials like solid platinum graphite. An inert electrode simply sits in the solution. It provides a physical surface area where the aqueous or gaseous molecules can dock, drop off an electron into the platinum lattice, and swim away. So the platinum doesn't dissolve. Right, and it doesn't gain mass, it just ferries the electrons from the chemical reaction into the external circuit. That makes perfect sense. Now let's flip the thermodynamic script. Let's talk about the electrolytic cell. This is the empty water tower. We want to force a reaction that has a positive Delta G. We want to force a non spontaneous event. Because the reaction is non spontaneous, the chemical species involved will never naturally transfer electrons in the direction we want. Therefore, an electrolytic cell must be driven by an external energy source. We have to splice a powerful external battery into the wire connecting the two electrodes. This process is called electrolysis. Okay, let's take our spontaneous Daniel cell and force it backward. Normally zinc wants to give electrons to copper. Zinc is the anode, copper is the cathode. But what if we hook up a massively powerful external battery and force the electrons to go the other way? Pump. Its positive terminal is attached to the copper strip. The external battery's positive pole physically rips electrons out of the copper metal regardless of copper's high electron affinity. Because electrons are being removed from the copper, the copper is undergoing oxidation. Wait, and oxidation always occurs at the anode. So by forcing the reverse reaction, the copper electrode is now the anode. Precisely. The chemical identity of the anode has changed. The external battery pumps those ripped electrons across the wire and violently shoves them into the zinc strip. The zinc is forced to accept the electrons. Because it is gaining electrons, it is undergoing reduction. Wow. So the zinc is now the cathode. The golden rule hasn't changed but the chemical players have swapped roles because the external pump is dictating the terms. And this forced thermodynamic reversal has massive industrial and biological Consider the chlor alkali process, which is how the modern world produces chlorine gas. If you take standard table salt, sodium chloride, and melt it at incredibly high temperatures, you get a liquid pool of freely moving Ney plus cations and Cl anions. Molten salt. But sodium is incredibly stable as an anion, and chloride is incredibly stable as an anion. They have complete octets. They have zero desire to change. Exactly. The spontaneous delta G is entirely in favor of remaining molten salt. But if we insert two inert electrodes into that molten pool and hook up external power source, we create an electrolytic cell. And it forces the change. Yes. The external battery pulls electrons out of the chloride anions. It forces them to oxidize into toxic, highly reactive chlorine gas bubbling up at the anode. It pumps those electrons over to the other electrode and forces them onto the sodium cations, reducing them into volatile, pure liquid sodium metal at the cathode. We are forcing nature into a high energy state to harvest pure elements. And looking at some biological applications, we use this exact same force pump mechanism in the laboratory technique called isoelectric focusing. Isoelectric focusing is a brilliant application of the electrolytic cell used to separate proteins. Proteins are composed of amino acids, which have varying acidic and basic side chains. This means a protein's overall electrical charge changes depending on the pH of its environment. Right, at a very low pH, an acidic environment with lots of protons, the protein gets heavily protonated and takes on a positive charge. At a high pH, it loses protons and becomes negatively charged. And every protein has a specific pH at which it's perfectly electrically neutral, its isoelectric point. In isoelectric focusing, you create a gel with a stable pH gradient. Acidic on one side, basic on the other. You place your mixture of proteins in the gel. Then you set up an electrolytic cell by applying a high voltage across the gel. You create a cathode on one end and an anode on the other. If a protein is in a region of the gel where the pH makes it positively charged, it will feel the electrical pull and migrate toward the negatively charged electrode. But as it moves through the gel, it enters regions of changing Oh, I see where this is going. The moment the protein hits the exact pH that matches its isoelectric point, it loses its net charge. And an uncharged molecule doesn't feel an electrical field. It literally stops dead in its tracks. The electrical field is still pumping, but the protein stops stops migrating. By forcing this non spontaneous migration, you can separate hundreds of different proteins based purely on their specific neutral points. That is a phenomenal transition from industrial metallurgy to biochemistry. Which brings us to the third type of cell, the concentration cell. Honestly, this one feels like a trick question. A concentration cell is a specialized subcategory of a galvanic cell. So it is spontaneous, delta G is negative, it generates a voltage. But the trick is that the anode and the cathode are made of the exact same chemical material. Okay, hold on. If they're the exact same material, say, two identical copper strips in two identical copper sulfate solutions, how can there be any voltage? Neither side wants electrons more than the other. The tug of war is perfectly matched. There should be zero potential energy. If the two beakers were entirely identical, you would be correct. The voltage would be precisely zero. But in a concentration cell, the potential energy does not come from differing chemical identities. It comes from a difference in concentration. The beaker on the left might have a copper ion concentration of 0.1 molar, while the beaker on the right has concentration of two point zero molar. Then the second law of thermodynamics dictates that entropy must increase. The universe wants those concentrations to be equal. The system has a thermodynamic drive to reach equilibrium. To achieve this, a spontaneous redox reaction occurs specifically to alter the concentrations. In the beaker with the low 0.1 molar concentration, the solid copper electrode will spontaneously oxidize, dropping more Cu2 plus ions into the water to raise concentration. Because it is oxidizing, this is the anode. And the electrons from that oxidation travel up the wire to the other beaker, the two point zero molar side. Those electrons are grabbed by the abundant Cu2 plus ions in the water, reducing them into solid copper metal. This lowers the concentration of ions in the right beaker. This is the cathode. Exactly. This chemical current will flow spontaneously until the concentrations in both beakers are exactly equal. The moment the concentrations balance, the thermodynamic drive vanishes and the voltage drops to zero. We brought this up in the very first minute of the deep dive. The human body uses concentration cells. It is the foundation of neurophysiology. The cell membrane of a neuron is an insulating physical barrier, just like the glass separating our two peakers. And the body invests massive amounts of ATP energy to run the Na plus K plus pump. The NATE plus K plus ATPase pump. Let's detail its mechanism to see the concentration cell being built. The pump grabs three sodium cations from inside the cell and physically shoves them out into the extracellular fluid. Then it grabs two potassium cations from the outside and pulls them inside. By doing this continuously, the pump creates a massive imbalance. The outside of the cell becomes a highly concentrated sodium environment. The inside becomes a highly concentrated potassium environment. You have literally created two chemical concentration cells simultaneously across a barrier. The sodium desperately wants to flow back inside to reach equilibrium. The potassium desperately wants to flow outside. And that desperation is potential energy. It creates a measurable voltage across the neuronal membrane known as the resting membrane potential. When you decide to move a muscle, your neuron receives a signal that opens specific ion channels in the membrane. It is opening the valve on the water tower. Exactly. The ions instantly rush down their concentration gradients, generating a massive spike in electric current, the action potential that travels down the nerve. Your thoughts are simply biological concentration cells discharging to achieve equilibrium. It's breathtaking. Now, before we move into calculating the precise math of these voltages, we must confront the most notorious point of confusion in this entire subject: the positive and negative signs assigned to the electrodes. Oh, this breaks more students than almost any other concept. We have established that oxidation is always at the anode and reduction is always at the cathode. The chemistry does not change. But the actual positive or negative label painted on the physical metal strip flips depending on whether the cell is galvanic or electrolytic. Let's take it slowly. Start with a spontaneous galvanic cell, the Daniel cell. In a spontaneous galvanic cell, the anode is the source of the electrons. The zinc is naturally oxidizing, releasing its negative electrons, which then push into the wire. Because the anode is the origin point of the negative electrons, the anode in a galvanic cell is defined as the negative electrode. That tracks perfectly with logic. The electrons are born there, so it's the negative pole. And those electrons travel across the wire towards the cathode. Electrons are negatively charged, so they are naturally attracted to a positive environment. Therefore, the cathode in a galvanic cell must be the positive electrode. Exactly. In a galvanic cell, anode is negative, cathode is positive. Electrons move from negative to positive. Now, we shift to the non spontaneous electrolytic cell, the driven pump. Okay, but you defined the anode as the physical location where oxidation occurs. Loss of electrons. How can the label change? We must look at why the oxidation is occurring. In an electrolytic cell, the reaction does not want to happen. The only reason the metal is losing electrons oxidizing is because it is physically connected to the positive terminal of an external battery. The external battery's positive pole is violently pulling the negative electrons out of the metal. Wait, I have to push back here. You just defined the anode as the source of electrons in the wire, which makes it negative. How can the source of negative charge suddenly be called a positive electrode? That breaks the physical logic you just established. It is a critical distinction in perspective. In the galvanic cell, the anode generates the electrons from its own internal chemical energy it is the source of the negativity for the whole circuit. But in the electrolytic cell, the anode is just a passive piece of metal being acted upon It is not generating the electrical push the external battery is. Oh, starting to see. Because the anode is physically clamped to the positive terminal of the external power source, which is what forces the oxidation to occur, the entire anode takes on the charge of that external terminal. Therefore, the anode in an electrolytic cell is defined as the positive electrode. Oh, see. In the galvanic cell, the anode is the battery, so it gets the negative label because it's spitting out electrons. In the electrolytic cell, the anode is just a victim attached to the positive side of battery. So it inherits the positive label. Precisely. And following that logic, the cathode in an electrolytic cell is where reduction is forced to occur. How is it forced? Because it is attached to the negative terminal of the external battery, which is relentlessly pumping electrons into it. Thus, the cathode in an electrolytic cell is the negative electrode. So the labels flip entirely based on who is driving the bus. Galvanic, anode is negative, cathode is positive, electrolytic is positive, cathode is negative. It is deeply confusing to memorize blindly, which is why you must understand the driving force. However, there is a physical failsafe we discussed earlier that is immune to this label flipping. Do you remember how the ions in the salt bridge behave? Anions to anode, cations to cathode, A D A C to C. Does that really hold true even when the signs flip? The ions in solution never lie. In a galvanic cell, the anode is oxidizing, dumping positive metal ions into the beaker. To neutralize that positive buildup, the negative anions from the salt bridge flow to the anode beaker. In an electrolytic cell, the anode is physically forced to be the positive electrode by the external battery. Because it is highly positive, it physically attracts the negative anions in the solution. Wow. The mechanism of attraction changes. One is neutralizing a solution, the other is attracted to a charged plate, but the physical movement is identical. Anions always end up at the anode. It is an absolute universal truth. Anions to anode, cations to cathode. Let's apply this dual nature to a real world device: the lead acid battery in your car. A car battery is rechargeable. That means it has to be both types of cells. Exactly. When you turn the key in the ignition, the battery discharging. It is operating as a spontaneous galvanic cell. A solid lead anode oxidizes, a lead oxide cathode reduces, and the spontaneous flow of electrons cranks your starter motor. But once the engine fires up, a belt spins the alternator, which generates electricity. And the alternator pushes that electricity backward into the battery. The battery is now charging. It is operating as a non spontaneous electrolytic cell. The external power from the alternator forces the chemical reactions to run-in reverse, reducing the lead ions back into solid lead at the anode, storing potential energy for the next time you need to start the car. Discharge is galvanic. Recharge is electrolytic. We have the anatomy completely mapped, but we haven't touched a single number yet. If I have a piece of silver and a piece of iron, how do I know which one is the anode and how much voltage they will produce? This moves us from the physical construction of cells to the mathematical measurement of cell potentials, our next To measure the potential energy of a reaction, we measure its electromotive force. But voltage is a relative measurement. You cannot measure the absolute potential of a single piece of floating metal. You can only measure the difference in potential between two connected points. It's like measuring altitude. You can't say a mountain is 10,000 feet tall without a green on what zero feet is. A perfect analogy. To build a comparative scale, the scientific community had to assign an arbitrary sea level for electrochemistry. That C level is the standard hydrogen electrode, or SHE. What does the standard hydrogen electrode physically look like? It is an inert platinum electrode, submerged in an aqueous solution of one molar hydrochloric acid. We bubble pure hydrogen gas over the platinum at exactly one atmosphere of pressure, keeping the entire system at two ninety eight Kelvin or 25 degrees Celsius. Okay, standard conditions. Yes. The scientific community agreed to assign the redox reaction of hydrogen gas converting to H plus ions, or vice versa, a potential of exactly zero point zero zero zero volts. So zero volts. By comparing every other chemical element on Earth against this specific hydrogen setup, we generated a massive index. We generated the table of standard reduction potentials. We denote this value as E degree red or E naught red. The degree symbol, the naught, tells us it is measured under standard thermodynamic conditions: two eighty eight Kelvin, one atmosphere, and one molar concentrations. And what does this number physically represent? A standard reduction potential measures the innate tendency of a specific chemical species to undergo reduction to gain electrons. It is an electron affinity scale. The more positive the E naught red value, the greater the species' thermodynamic desire to gain electrons. So it's a measure of atomic strength in a tug of war. The element with the highly positive reduction potential is the strongest team on the rope. It is going to violently rip electrons away from anything weaker than it. That is exactly how you should conceptualize it. Let's say you build a galvanic cell out of two random metals, metal A and metal B. You look at a table, metal A has a reduction potential of plus one point volts. Metal B has a reduction potential of negative 0.5 volts. Metal A is the stronger team. Plus 1.5 beats net of 0.5. So metal A wins the tug of war. It successfully gains the electrons. Gain the electrons is reduction and reduction occurs at the cathode. So metal A must be the cathode. Which means metal B loses the tug of war. Despite whatever it wants, it is forced to give up its electrons to metal A. Giving up electrons is oxidation, which occurs at the anode. Metal B is the anode. By simply looking at the reduction potentials, you can predict the entire physical layout of a spontaneous battery. But if we are running an electrolytic cell, if we have our external pump hooked up, the whole point is that we are forcing the weak team to win. Exactly. In an electrolytic cell, the battery forces the species with the highly positive reduction potential instead. It makes the strong team lose. That is why it requires a massive external voltage to run. Okay, so once we identify the cathode and the anode based on the tug of war, how do we calculate the total voltage the battery will produce? We calculate the standard electromotive force or E naught cell. The formula is beautifully simple. E naught cell equals the standard production potential of the cathode minus the standard reduction potential of the anode. Just take the table value of the winner and subtract the table value of the loser. But I want to pause on the terminology here. We keep saying electromotive force. Force is a physics term that means mass times acceleration. We aren't calculating newtons here. It is one of the most misleading historical names in all of science. Electromotive force is not a mechanical force. It is a potential difference. It is a voltage. Let's define exactly what a volt is, mechanically. A volt is a joule per coulomb. Yes. A coulomb is a unit of electrical charge. A joule is a unit of energy. Therefore, a volt measures the amount of energy joules available to do work for every single unit of charged coulomb that moves through the wire. It is an energy density. It's the pressure of the electrical flow. Yeah. A high voltage means every single electron carries a massive punch of energy. Which perfectly sets up the final core pillar of this subject in section 12.3: Electromotive force and thermodynamics. We know the cathode wins the tug of war. We know how to calculate the voltage pressure of the cell. But how do we translate that voltage into total actual thermodynamic work? How do we calculate the delta G? We have to connect the electrical world back to the chemical world of chapter six. The bridge equation between those two worlds is paramount. The change in standard Gibbs free energy is equal to negative N times F times the standard cell potential. Delta G naught equals negative N F E cell naught. Let's isolate the variables. Delta G naught is the total thermodynamic energy available. E cell naught is the voltage, the Joules per Coulomb we just calculated. What are n and f? N is simply the number of moles of electrons that are exchanged in the balanced redox reaction. If a zinc atom gives up two electrons to copper, N is two. F is Faraday's constant. Let's unpack Faraday's constant. What does it physically represent? We know from the famous Millikan oil drop experiment the exact electrical charge of a single solitary electron. It is a tiny, incomprehensible fraction of a coulomb. But in chemistry, we don't deal with single atoms. Deal with moles. A mole is Avogadro's number. 6.02 x ten twenty three particles. So if you take the tiny charge of one electron and multiply it by a staggering six times 10 to the twenty third. You get the charge of exactly one mole of electrons. That value is roughly 96,485 Coulombs per mole. For the MCAT, we round Faraday's constant to 96,500 Coulombs per mole of electrons. So let me look at this equation again. Delta G naught equals negative NFE cell naught. The N is moles of electrons. F is charge per mole. You multiply them together, N times F, the moles cancel out. You are just left with total charge. Yes. Nf represents the total aggregate electrical charge moving through the wire. And you multiply that total charge by the E cell knot, which we just established is voltage. Total charge times voltage. This is literally just a core physics equation. Work equals charge times change in voltage. W equals Q delta V. It is the exact same fundamental principle mapped onto chemical moles. Total charge Q multiplied by the energy density per charge delta V gives you the total energy available to do work, which is W or delta The negative sign is the thermodynamic truth teller. If you calculate an E cell knot and get a positive voltage like in a spontaneous galvanic battery, that positive number multiplies with the negative sign. Your delta G knot becomes negative. A negative delta G confirms the reaction is spontaneous and releases energy. And if you are forcing a non spontaneous electrolytic cell, your calculated E cell naught will be a negative voltage. The negative voltage cancels out the negative sign in the equation, resulting in a positive delta G. A positive delta G confirms the reaction requires an input of external work to function. The math physically proves the thermodynamic rules. It is a closed loop of logic. However, all of this mathematical perfection hinges on that little knot symbol, the degree sign. Real life doesn't happen at exactly one molar concentration, especially not in biology. As a battery discharges, it consumes reactants and generates products. The concentrations are constantly shifting. To calculate the actual voltage of a cell at any given non standard moment, we must use the the Nernst equation. The Nernst equation modifies the standard voltage based on the real time concentrations. The formula is E cell equals E cell naught minus RT over nf times the natural log of Q. Let's tear this down. E cell is the actual voltage right now. E cell naught is the standard baseline voltage we calculated earlier. We subtract a correction factor from that baseline. The correction factor contains R, the ideal gas constant, T, the absolute temperature in Kelvin, n moles of electrons, F Faraday's constant, and finally the natural log of Q. Q is the reaction quotient from our chemical equilibrium studies. It is the real time concentration of the products divided by the concentration of the reactants. Look at how the mechanics of Q dictate the life of a battery. When you buy a brand new battery, it is packed with reactants and has very few products. The denominator of Q is massive. The numerator is tiny. Therefore, Q is a very small fraction. The natural log of a tiny fraction is a large negative number. And subtracting a negative number is just adding. So the real voltage of a fresh battery is actually slightly higher than its standard rating. Exactly. But as you use the battery, reactants are consumed and products are created. Q steadily grows larger. The natural log of Q increases, meaning you are subtracting a larger and larger number from your starting voltage. The E cell slowly drops. Eventually, Q reaches the equilibrium constant, KEQ. At exactly equilibrium, the math works out so that the correction factor perfectly equals the E cell naught. You subtract a number from itself, the voltage becomes exactly zero, the battery is dead. Equilibrium is death for a battery. Mechanically, the Nernst equation is beautiful. Mathematically on the MCAT, calculating a natural log by hand without a calculator is terrifying. So how do they expect you to use this on test day? They expect you to understand the proportional relationships, how shifting a concentration shifts the voltage. But more importantly, from our secondary biology sources, there is a simplified version of the Nernst equation specifically tailored for the human body. Going back to our biological concentration cells, the neuronal membrane. In a human cell, the temperature T is clamped at physiological body temperature, roughly three ten Kelvin. Because R, T, and F are all fixed constants at body temperature, we can merge them all into a number. Furthermore, we convert the difficult natural log into a much easier base 10 log. And when you smash all those constants together, what is the simplified biological Nernst equation? It becomes: E = 61.5 over Z times the log base 10 of the concentration of the ion outside divided by the concentration of the ion inside. The Z represents the charge of the specific ion you are measuring. For sodium or potassium, the Z is positive one. 61.5 divided by the charge times the log of the outside concentration over the inside concentration. That is infinitely more manageable. This equation calculates the exact equilibrium potential for a single ion across a cell membrane. Let's trace the physical reality of potassium. Due to the Na plus K plus pump, potassium is highly concentrated inside the cell. The chemical gradient pushes potassium out through leak channels, but as those positive potassium ions leave, the inside of the cell loses positive charge, it becomes extremely negative. And opposite charges attract. The newly negative interior of the cell starts electrically pulling the positive potassium back inside. It is a tug of war within a single ion species. The chemical concentration gradient pushes K plus out, The electrical voltage gradient pulls K plus in. The Nernst equation calculates the exact voltage at which those two opposing forces perfectly cancel each other out. If you plug in the physiological concentrations of potassium into the biological equation, it outputs an equilibrium potential of approximately negative 90 millivolts. If the cell membrane reaches negative 90 millivolts, the electrical pull perfectly matches the chemical push. Potassium stops experiencing net movement. But here is the biological reality. The resting membrane potential of a typical neuron is not negative 90 millivolts, it is negative 70 millivolts. Why doesn't potassium get what it wants? Because sodium is crashing the party. Exactly. Sodium is highly concentrated outside the cell, the chemical gradient is pushing sodium in. And because the inside of the cell is negative, the electrical gradient is also pulling sodium in. If you run the Nernst equation for sodium alone, its equilibrium potential is a massive positive 60 millivolts. So potassium is dragging the cell's voltage violently down toward negative 90 millivolts. Sodium is dragging the voltage violently up toward positive 60 millivolts. It's a thermodynamic compromise. They settle at a weighted average. Because the resting cell membrane has significantly more potassium leak channels than sodium leak channels, it is more permeable to potassium wins the tug of war. The final resting potential of negative 70 millivolts is much closer to potassium's goal of -ninety than sodium's goal of plus 60. And if we want to calculate that compromise exactly, we step beyond the Nernst equation to the Goldman Hodgkins Katz voltage equation, which factors in the concentration and the permeability of every ion simultaneously to calculate the true membrane potential. Your thoughts are just the shifting permeabilities of these ions altering the electrochemical math. It is chemistry becoming biology. To close out the thermodynamics, we must link our electrochemistry back to the equilibrium constant, Keq. We have two separate equations that both calculate the standard Gibbs free energy. From pure thermodynamics, we know delta G naught equals negative RT times the natural log of Keq. And from today, we know delta G naught equals negative nFE cell naught. Because both equations equal delta G naught, we can set them mathematically equal to each other. Negative nFE cell naught equals negative RT natural log KEq. The negative signs on both sides cancel out, leaving us with NFE cell naught equals RT natural log KEq. It's elegant algebra, but let's break down the physical truth of what this equation is telling us. If I'm looking at a reaction where equilibrium constant, Keq, is massive, say 10,000, what does that dictate about the battery? Trace the logic. A Keq greater than one means the reaction heavily favors the products and wants to move forward. If Keq is greater than one, the natural log of Keq will always be a positive number. Okay, so the entire right side of the equation RTLNKEQ is a positive value. Yes. And on the left side of the equation, FEcell and F are positive constants. Therefore, if the right side is positive, the Ecell variable must be a positive number for the equation to balance. And we know that a positive Ecell not means a spontaneous galvanic battery. It's a mathematically unbreakable chain. If KEq is greater than one, it favors products. If it favors products, the delta G is negative. If delta G is negative, E cell naught must be positive. They're all just different dialects of the same thermodynamic language. That holistic understanding is what the MCAT tests. Which leads us to the common student confusions and traps. We have already hammered the most confusing concept: the flipping of the enodekathode signs between galvanic and electrolytic cells. If you anchor yourself to the fact that the external battery dictates the forced reaction, you will survive the trap. What is the most devastating trap hidden in the math? The multiplier trap. It strikes when you are calculating E cell naught by subtracting the anode from the cathode. Before you can determine the overall cell reaction, you often have to balance the stoichiometry. Right. If the anode oxidation releases two electrons, but the cathode reduction only accepts one electron, the chemistry is unbalanced. You have to multiply the entire cathode half reaction by two so that two electrons are released and two electrons are consumed. And when a student multiplies the half reaction by two, their instant trained by months of calculating Hess's law for enthalpy is to multiply the standard reduction potential voltage by two as well. But you cannot do that. Voltage is an intensive property. Exactly. An intensive property does not depend on the physical amount of the substance present. The classic example is temperature. If you have a cup of coffee at 90 degrees Celsius and you pour in a second identical cup of coffee at 90 degrees Celsius, don't you suddenly have 180 degree cup of coffee, you just have twice as much 90 degree coffee. The potential energy is intrinsic to the chemical identity. A standard reduction potential defines the energy of a single atomic event. Multiplying the reaction by two just means that the specific atomic event is happening twice as often. It doesn't alter the intrinsic energy of the event itself. Never multiply the E not red values. You simply take the cathode value and subtract the anode value, regardless of the stoichiometric coefficients. It is a trap designed to catch rote memorizers. The other major trap is the unit trap hidden within the delta G naught equals negative NFE cell naught equation. Difference between Joules and kilojoules. It is. Faraday's constant is approximately 96,500 Coulombs per mole. Because it relies on standard SI base units, the result of the calculation will always output energy in joules. And the MCAT loves to list the final delta G answer choices in kilojoules. If you calculate the equation and get 250,000 joules, answer choice A will inevitably be 250,000 kilojoules. It's sitting there waiting for you to circle in a rush. You must divide your result by 1,000 to convert to kilojoules. The correct answer would be two fifty kilojoules. Check your units on every single thermodynamic calculation. Let's test this comprehensive understanding. We need a practice scenario that brings the biological and chemical applications together, moving past simple arithmetic. Let's evaluate a biological electron transport chain analogy using standard potentials. We have a generic biologic cell constructing a microscopic electrochemical gradient. The net reaction is the reduction of cytochrome c by an iron sulfur cluster. The reaction is F2 plus plus oxidized cytochrome c yields F3 plus plus reduced cytochrome C. Iron two plus reacting with oxidized cytochrome C yielding iron three plus and reduced cytochrome C. The standard reduction potential of the F3 plus to F2 plus half reaction is positive 0.77 volts. The standard reduction potential of the cytochrome C half reaction is positive 0.25 volts. The question is, what is the E cell naught? And based on the thermodynamics, is this a spontaneous biological pathway as written? Let's isolate the components. Step one, identify the oxidation and reduction happening in the given equation. Looking at the equation, iron starts at a plus two state on the left and goes to a plus three state on the right. Its positive charge increased, which means it must have lost a negative electron. Loss of electrons is oxidation. Oxidation happens at the anode, so the iron half reaction is acting as our anode. Correct. Now cytochrome c, it literally goes from oxidized to reduced in the equation. Gaining electrons is reduction. Reduction happens at the cathode. Cytochrome c is acting as our cathode. Step two, Calculate the voltage using Ecell0 = cathode minus anode. We establish cytochrome C as the cathode with a value of positive 0.25 volts. Iron is the anode with a value of positive 0.77 volts. So we take 0.25 volts and subtract 0.77 volts. 0.25 minuteus 0.77 equals negative 0.52 volts. Our standard cell potential is a negative 0.52 volts. Now translate that into thermodynamic reality. Is this pathway spontaneous? The E cell not is a negative number. When we plug a negative voltage into the equation delta G knot equals negative nFE cell knot, the two negative signs cancel out. Delta G becomes a positive number. A positive delta G means the reaction is absolutely non spontaneous? Exactly. As written, this biological pathway will not happen on its own. It is functioning like an electrolytic cell. For a cell to actually force this reaction forward, it would have to couple it with the hydrolysis of ATP using an external battery to provide the necessary energy. You successfully bypass the trap of just subtracting the smaller number from the larger number. You have to follow the chemistry of the written equation to assign the cathode and anode, even if it results in a negative voltage. That is the discipline required. You trust the physical rules over your instinct to find a clean positive number. We have covered an immense territory today. We map the physical anatomy of the galvanic cell spontaneously draining energy and the electrolytic cell forcing it back into storage. We trace the localized charge buildup that necessitates the salt bridge, we define the atomic tug of war using standard reduction potentials, and ultimately bridge the gap between electrical voltage and Gibbs free energy. We connected the abstract exchange of electrons to the physical reality of the universe. We explored industrial chlorine production, protein separation through isoelectric focusing, and the Nernst equations governing neurological concentration cells in our own brains. It is a remarkable synthesis. But as we close this deep dive, I want to leave you with one final provocative image to consider. We spent a lot of time on the neuron cell membrane. But think deeper. Think about the mitochondria inside your cells, the powerhouse. The site of oxidative phosphorylation. Exactly. How does a mitochondrion actually manufacture ATP? It uses the energy from oxidizing glucose to literally pump protons hydrogen ions across its inner membrane. It forces a massive concentration gradient. It builds a proton water tower. Biology calls it the proton motive force. An electromotive force generated purely by protons. The ATP synthase enzyme, the complex that physically stitches ADP and phosphate together to make ATP, is nothing more than a biological turbine. It sits in the membrane, opens a channel, and lets those trapped protons flow spontaneously back down their electrochemical gradient. The physical energy of those protons rushing down the gradient physically spins the enzyme like a water wheel, generating the ATP that keeps you alive. It is billions of microscopic galvanic concentration cells firing continuously in every Cell of Your Body You are quite literally a walking, breathing network of biological batteries, operating strictly on the thermodynamic and Nernst Equation principles we just unpacked. Keep that reality in mind the next time you feel bogged down in standard reduction tables. Electrochemistry isn't just industrial math, it is the exact mechanical spark of your existence. Thank you so much for joining us on this deep dive. You have mastered MCAT general chemistry, now go out there and crush it!
